text: Using paleoproteomics (ZooMS), my team identified a tiny hominin skull fragment at Riparo Bombrini (Level M4), in the Balzi Rossi site complex in NW Italy. Its age and cultural association material of the layer (43.5k cal. years BP, Mousterian tools) mean it's very likely a Neanderthal. 🏺🧪

The Iron Crown of Lombardy was made in the Middle Ages. Legend maintains its central band was beaten from a nail of the True Cross. It was used in the coronation of the first Holy Roman Emperor Charlemagne on Christmas day 800 and was worn again in 1805 by Napoleon when he was crowned King of Italy.

Mausoleum of the Julii situated in Glanum, a Roman village in s. France, modern St.Rémy-de-Provence, c. 40 BC.

Architrave inscription: 
SEX · M · L · IVLIEI · C · F · PARENTIBVS · SVEIS 
Sextius, Marcus and Lucius Julius, sons of Gaius, to their forebears.

Credit Lauren Heilman 
🏺 Ancientbluesky

This magnificent Gallic flagon dates to c450 BCE. It’s exquisitely cast & regarded as a masterpiece of early Celtic art. If you look closely at the spout, there’s even a little joke at work as there’s a bronze duck who will ride the flow of beer or wine as it’s poured out

🏛️BM 📷me
🏺 AncientBlueSky

"There is no altar anywhere in England, nor has there been any abroad, so handsomely adorned," wrote John Evelyn of the beautiful lime wood carvings of Grinling Gibbons, who was dubbed the "King Carver" of the late 17th century, on the altar of Christopher Wren's 1684 church, St James Piccadilly.

#MosaicMonday - Discovered 8 March 1764 in the 'Villa of Cicero'. A grand mosaic (dubbed 'The Sorceress'), signed by Dioskourides of Samos, likely representing a scene from Comedy. #Pompeii 🏺ClassicsBluesky 

Image: National Archaeological Museum, Naples (9987). Link - mann-napoli.it/mosaici/#gal...

1st c. CE Roman terracotta from Syria, depicting two female musicians on a camel. They each hold a tympanum, a hand drum. If you look closely, you can see traces of bright pink pigment that once decorated their shared cloak. #polychromy 

Princeton University Art Museum (y1952-53) 🏺

Carts are a frequent topic on Late Geometric Greek vessels, as part of funeral ceremonies and as vehicles of warriors. Models of carts are rarer and this one with four horses driven by a warrior with a shield is exceptionally well preserved. 750-740 BCE, from Athens. KHM Vienna. 🏺

Stein's Pillar, Oregon
A 350 foot tall (106 m) volcanic remnant composed of tuff (ash and ejecta from an eruption), welded together, and eroded over about 40 million years after the caldera it came from has largely vanished. Probably from the Yellowstone hot spot.

Tonight's goodnight image. Escomb Church, County Durham, England. The 2nd oldest intact building in all the British Isles. Built ~AD670 from stones pilfered from the nearby Roman fort at Binchester, including a complete arch and a stone marked by the Sixth Legion.

The vibe inside is amazing. 🏺

On my recent trip to Scotland I made a special visit to the Meffan Museum in Forfar to see this lovely little bronze bust of Minerva. She was discovered at the site of a Roman fort at Cardean and dates from the early 80s AD. I particularly love the very grumpy looking Gorgon on her armour!

The cathedral of St Mary, Limerick, is also home to the only complete set of misericords to survive from medieval Ireland. These beautifully carved hinged oak seats, dating from the 15th century, were designed to give support to clergy who had to stand during long services.
Details of one of the T columns in Göbekli Tepe, Turkey. It is approximately 12,000 years old, making it remarkably older than Egyptian Pyramids and Stonehenge.

The Madrague de Giens is a Roman shipwreck off the coast of S France, sinking mid C1stBCE. It was a huge merchant ship, 40m in length. Fully laden it could hold 520 tons of cargo. The amphorae found held wine, oil & other goods like garum. Its discovery was also a breakthrough in marine archaeology

Around 2,200 years ago, the artist Hephaistion left his signature on a mosaic floor in the form of a piece of parchment held down with small drops of wax. This perfect imitation of a sheet of parchment, with one corner curled up, was discovered in one of the palaces at Pergamon.

Thonis-Heraklion was founded in C8thBCE at the Nile’s mouth. Before Alexandria’s creation, it was the major port of the delta. Succeeding earthquakes & floods saw the city slip under the waves in C2ndBCE eventually disappearing c.900CE. It has been excavated since 1933
📷Franck Goddio🏺 AncientBlueSky

2,300 years old, this astonishingly life-like bronze head of Thracian king Seuthes III ~ with alabaster & glass paste eyes (bloodshot!), copper strip eyelashes & eyebrows, & maybe evidence of old injury to cheekbone (Nat Mus Archaeology, Sofia) Photo: Krasimir Georgiev

This is the Temple of Apollo in Syracuse. It was constructed in the C6th BCE in the Doric style and is one of the first temples in Sicily with a peripteros (a colonnade of columns surrounding the inner sanctuary). Like many ruined classical temples, it’s the haunt of cats. 

📷 mine
🏺 AncientBlueSky

More than forty archaeological sites in Cyprus dating potentially as far back as the Bronze Age that were thought lost to history have been relocated by University of Leicester scientists working for the Ministry of Defence. le.ac.uk/news/2024/ma...

At first glance you might think that this alabaster tomb might be from Westminster Abbey but this remarkable monument to Alice Chaucer is at St. Mary, Ewelme in Oxfordshire. She was the grandaughter of Geoffrey Chaucer. Note the stone cadaver behind the tracery at the bottom.

This is believed to be the oldest depiction of Jesus.

The 'Alexamenos graffito' (singular of graffiti), is trolling a Roman soldier named Alexamenos, apparently a Christian, likely around 200 AD.

A man is shown worshiping a donkey-headed god on a crucifix.

Text: "Alexamenos worships [his] God"

About 80 % of students thought this was a swanky straw hat from the 1950s. Truth is that it’s the oldest Roman grasswork "Panama hat" extant, from Fayum in Egypt. In 2017 they radiocarbon dated it to 420-568 CE. Turns out the “Dark Ages” was really more about cool shade (Pitt Rivers Museum, UK) 🎩

It’s Trier (or Augusta Treverorum) in western Germany. Here the Porta Nigra (started 170), the Aula Palatina (early 4th century), the imperial baths (also early 4th century) & the amphitheatre (mid-2nd century). Incredible remains.

📷 all open source
🏺 AncientBlueSky

This reticulated beaker has an inscription in glass letters: FAVENTIBVS. It’s an expression of good luck (perhaps short for "Faventibus ventis" – with favourable winds). The ‘cage’ of the beaker is also glass. These items are the pinnacle of Roman glass craft

🏛️📷Kunsthistoriches 
🏺 AncientBlueSky

The Iron Age wooden roadway at Corlea 1, Co Longford, dated to 148/147 BC. Truly a “Colossus of Roads” (I dug on this as a student, and was also at the Exeter conference where Barry tried out his dreadful pun for the first time 🥰) 🏺#Ireland #Archaeology

For EpigraphyTuesday: the funerary inscription of Julia Peiris, an obstetrix (midwife) who 'did no harm.' Notably, the first T of obstetrix is shoehorned in between the S and E. Dated to the 1st century CE, from Trier. Now in the Rheinisches Landesmuseum Trier.

AncientBlueSky 🏺

Decorative mosiac column from the  Temple of Ninhursag at Tell Al-'Ubaid, Iraq. Originally made of palm wood, coated with bitumen. Inlays of mother-of-pearl, pink limestone, and black shale were held in place by  twisted copper wires. Dated to c. 2500 BCE. 

MosiacMonday
AncientBlueSky 🏺

📷BM

Paisley Abbey was first built in the 12th century. In 1991, it underwent some necessary restoration work as 12 of its 13 gargoyles were so badly ruined from water damage they had to be removed. One stone mason had a little fun & one gargoyle bears a strong resemblance to the Xenomorph from Alien

This is an incredible relic from Ancient Rome. The bronze doors of the Lateran Cathedral (left) were originally the doors of the Senate house (right). They date to the reign of Domitian (81-96CE) & were moved to their current location in 1660 by Pope Alexander VII

📷mine 
🏺 ClassicsBlueSky

Most 3rd-4th c. CE Roman gold glass depicted Christian scenes. However, this piece depicts the god Oceanus reclining in the sea, indicated by wavy lines in pale-blue pigment. He holds a jar from which water pours; above are the legs of horses that probably pulled a chariot over the waves. #MetMuseum

This is a really lovely, but melancholy piece. It’s an Etruscan sarcophagus lid (lateC4th-early C3rd BCE) showing a touching, naturalistic representation of a middle aged husband and wife. They lie in bed, toes poking out, holding each other tenderly. Only death will part them

🏛️📷 Boston MFA

Basalt carved statue of a standing lion from the Sumerian city of Eridu, dated to the mid 3rd millennium BCE. Eridu is thought to be the earliest city built by the Sumerians, founded c. 5400 BCE on the southern marshes of the Euphrates River, Iraq. #AncientSiteSunday AncientBlueSky 🏺

Evidence for long-distance trade in the Irish Neolithic. These beautiful green jadeite axes from Donegal & Westmeath are c. 6000 years old. Studies have shown that the stone they are fashioned out of comes from a series of Neolithic quarries located high in the Italian alps

#OnThisDay in 9 BC, the Ara Pacis Augustae (Altar of Augustan Peace) in Rome is consecrated by Augustus. It had been commissioned by the Roman Senate four years earlier in celebration of Augustus' return to Rome after three years in Hispania and Gaul.

The Cloth of St Gereon is the oldest known European tapestry still existing, dating to the early 11th century. Parts of it exist in a number of museums.  The tapestry formerly hung in the choir of St Gereon's Church, Cologne.

A series of camels carved into desert rock faces in north-western Saudi Arabia are actually prehistoric, dating from 7,000-8,000 years ago - before either the Pyramids of Giza or Stonehenge were built.

Thamugadi in Algeria was designed to be home to 10,000 retired legionaries, but soon outgrew its original allocation.Because of the remarkable state of preservation of the ruins & the unique example of Roman urban planning with clearly delineated grids, it was declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site

For the 17th day of ClassicsTober (Atalanta), a sarcophagus depicting the Calydonian Boar Hunt. Meleager stands at center, and to his right is Atalanta with her bow, drawing an arrow from her quiver. Found in Vicovaro, now in the Musei Capitolini in Rome.

AncientBlueSky 🏺

The Valley of Fires (Carrizozo Volcanic Field) formed only 5,200 years ago when lava spilled out of Little Black Peak and Broken Back Crater (cinder cones/vents) covering the landscape with these black pāhoehoe lava flows. It feels like a combination of Hawaiian terrain with Southwestern flora. 🌿
Sometimes I think about how furry art is older than agriculture. This is the Lion-man. It’s 30 to 40 thousand years old, carved out of mammoth ivory and is the oldest known statue. Period.

My favourite piece of 18th-century bling: the "Oceans Coach", an ornate carriage made at the behest of King John V of Portugal to demonstrate the wealth of the Portuguese empire to Pope Clement XI in Rome in 1716. patrimoniocultural.gov.pt/pt/news/comu...

The Löwenmensch figurine, also called the Lion-man of Hohlenstein-Stadel, is a prehistoric ivory sculpture discovered in Hohlenstein-Stadel, a German cave, in 1939. Determined by carbon dating to be 35,000-41,000 years old, it is one of (if not *the*) oldest anthropomorphic art pieces.

A beautiful socketed axe and a casting mould fragment from Hungary. Socketed axes are between the most widely used tool types of the Late Bronze Age Carpathian Basin.
NHM Vienna #Archaeology🏺
Three Roman mosaic flowers, laid within a mosaic in Rome about 1900 years ago - colourful & beautiful! The mosaic they're part of is now in display at the Palazzo Massimo museum in Rome.

Two golden bowls or ceremonial caps from the Late Bronze Age (1000-850 BC), found in 1972 at Axtroki, in the municipality of Escoriaza, Gipuzkoa, Spain. Nicely displayed at the Museo Arqueológico Nacional, Madrid
🏺 AncientBlueSky

I just love this manuscript. European Columbines and Sweet Cherry, from Mira Calligraphiae Monumenta (a calligraphy manual!), made in Vienna by Georg Bocskay (text, 1562) and Joris Hoefnagel (illumination c. 1591-1596). 
At the Getty Museum, Ms. 20 (86.MV.527), fol. 28.

The world's oldest condom, made of pig intestines in 1640 CE, discovered in Lund, Sweden. The owners manual advised men to dip it in unboiled milk before use to prevent diseases when engaging with prostitutes. It’s washable and reusable. On display at Tirolean County Museum in Austria.

A touch of ancient colour! Discovered in 1834 at the House of the Ancient Hunt, Pompeii (VII.4.48), this panel shows a gorgeously-attired Ariadne handing the skein of thread over to a blue-chlamys-clad Theseus. 

ClassicsBlueSky 

Image: National Archaeological Museum, Naples (9048)

Part of the Valchitran Treasure, a hoard of gold vessels and discs from Bulgaria. Discovered in 1924, its date has been discussed ever since. Opinions based on stylistic comparisons range from the 2nd to the 1st Millennium BCE. Archaeological Museum Sofia. #Archaeology 🏺

The Mosaic of Bacchus, discovered in 2009 in Saint-Laurent-d'Agny near Lyon, is thought to be the oldest known representation of the god of wine on a mosaic in Gaul. It was created around AD 10-14 and decorated the triclinium floor of a Roman domus. Bacchus is crowned with ivy and holding a thyrsus.

This is a marvellous Roman mosaic found at Italica (birthplace of both Trajan and Hadrian IIRC). 

Its obviously very realistic in appearance, but it also seems to seethe with the energy and rage of an angry lion. Love it. 

🕰️C3rd CE
🏛️ Seville Archaeological Museum 
📷 Carole Raddato
🏺 AncientBlueSky

Though not much survives, the Mausoleum at Halicarnassus must have been a literally awesome sight. 

Here’s a reconstruction from National Geographic showing the whole city (left) and a closer view from DK. 

I also attach pics of 2 lions from the BM that decorated it (📷 mine)
🏺 AncientBlueSky

The landscapes of central and northern Germany were once full of megalithic graves. Many have been destroyed to produce material for road construction in the 19th century. Lübbensteine, near Helmstedt in Lower Saxony, Germany, ca. 3500 BCE. #Archaeology🏺

Stacked Roman amphora fragments at Monte Testaccio in #Rome, essentially an ancient dumping ground for unwanted vessels (reusing not being a notable phenomenon) - these are just a small number of up to 53 MILLION vessels abandoned here in antiquity!

For MosaicMonday, from the Archaeological Museum of Sparta, a mosaic fragment depicting a rather shifty looking Alcibiades. The 5th century BCE statesman is identified by the mosaicked inscription. Found locally in Sparta and dated to the 1st to 4th century CE.

AncientBlueSky 🏺

In the early Renaissance, it was popular for Europeans to import Islamic artisan goods, like the famous Baptistere de Saint Louis, probably originating from Mamluk Egypt but appearing in France in the late 1300s or early 1400s, signed by the artist Muhammed ibn al-Zayn

The reliquary of the jaw of St. Anthony of Padua (Basilica del Santo, Padua) is crazy enough as it is, but in the modern times they added two light sources, to illuminate it better. The lights reflect from a glass sphere that holds the jaw, looking like ghostly eyes.

(See the 3d photo for no-eyes)

Some lovely Roman glassware. This large turquoise bottle was found at the burial site of an affluent, though not elite, Romano-British couple in Verulanium (now St Albans). Although it’s a relatively simple piece, its survival is incredible & makes it special 
📷 mine 🏺 AncientBlueSky

Belt buckles and bracelets made from Spondylus shells, a species living in the Mediterranean / Black Sea in depths around 15m, were between the favorite ornaments of the Linear Pottery Culture in Central Europe between 5500-4800 BCE. Historisches Museum Regensburg. #Archaeology 🏺

Holy moly! This looks like some glorious Ottoman helmet, but no, it’s Iron Age Celtic. 

This is the Agris Helmet found in France, the work of Gallic craftsmen from c350BCE. An iron dome is coated in fine gold leaf & coral. It’s breathtaking. 

🏛️Musée d’Angoulême
📷Rosemania 🏺 AncientBlueSky
Been up to see the church at Greensted-juxta-Ongar, said to be the oldest wooden church in the world, it dates from the mid-to-late C11.

Currently on display at the Cluny Museum in Paris, this ancient spear head made from rock crystal was found in south west France and probably dates from the Ice Age. Although its shape is not unusual, the use of the crystal suggests it had special significance.

One of the many highlights in the Archäologisches Landesmuseum Baden-Württemberg in Constance are the #Roman fittings of a door in Lopodunum, modern-day Ladenburg. My favourite : a bronze lion head door pull. Dating 2nd. century AD. 

#RomanArchaeology

The faces of Palmyra. These are tomb portraits of Palmyran elites dating from c50-270CE. The sculptural style, as well as that of clothes and hair, show a natural mix of Roman & Syrian influences. But look at those eyes - they’re looking at you from depths of the past
🏛️BM
📷me
🏺 AncientBlueSky

In AD 130, probably on the night of 24 October, during the sacred annual festival of the Mysteries of Osiris, the beautiful Bithynian youth, #Antinous, drowned in the Nile and became the last god of the ancient world, worshipped for centuries, throughout the Empire. #Egypt #Rome

Late Bronze Age baby-feeding vessels from Vösendorf, Austria, about 3000 years old. They have a tiny hole in the tail, so you can fill them with milk, and then an infant would hold it, and suck the milk through the tail. Ingenious and so pretty! Photo: Enver-Hirsch / Wien Museum

Don’t know about you but I love funerary reliefs from Palmyra. I’ve seen many in many museums & they’re so distinct. When you see one, you know at once it’s Palmyrene. They’re a wonderful example of cross-cultural exchange with clear Roman, Greek & Asian influences 

🏛️BM
📷mine
🏺 AncientBlueSky

Not all ancient stone tools are for cutting and hitting stuff. Some are just shiny pebbles that look nice.

More than 20,000 years ago, in what is now France, people collected and carried small stones that may have had magical powers, or been used in games🏺🧪

journals.plos.org/plosone/arti...

I’m a big fan of our leporine pals, but putting aside this bias for now, this Greek-Sicilian coin is a masterpiece of silver relief work. Just look at the patterning in his ears, the individual whiskers, the taut body. Extraordinary. 

🏛️Mandralisca Museum, Cefalu
📷me
🏺 AncientBlueSky

#ClassicsTober23 17: Atalanta

A powerful figure from Greek myth here depicted by Tyler Miles Lockett slaying two centaurs after they attempted to rape her. Atalanta was a devotee of Artemis and like the huntress herself sought a life of independence.

🏺ClassicsBluesky

Love the old and new writing formats in this 4thC mosaic of Jesus and apostles. Jesus holds a #scroll while Paul holds a #codex - perhaps representing the Old and New Testaments? Anyway, it’s an early representation of the codex in Roman Christianity. Basilica of Sant’Lorenzo, Milan #bookhistory

Mosaic floor depicting a dog and a knocked-over gold vessel. Discovered in 1993 during construction of the new Alexandria Library, Egypt. Now currently in the Greco-Roman Museum in Alexandria.  Width: approx. 70 cm. Date: approx. 200-100 BC.

The Church of Saints Theodore was the first katholikon of Brontochion Monastery, an important monastic institution in Late Byzantine Mystras. It was built by its abbot Pachomios between 1290 and 1295.

4th century AD fresco of Diana; Via Livenza hypogeum in Rome.  
Often used to indicate a subterranean temple or tomb, the word hypogeum stems from two Greek words: hypo (under) and gê (earth), and can refer to any building built underground.

🏺 Ancientbluesky

The legacies of Roman Emperors can be varied. Vespasian, known for the Colosseum, is also more humbly associated with public toilets in Italy and France, known as Vespasiano and Vespasienne. This association arose as a result of a tax he imposed upon the collection of urine. 

ancientbluesky 🏺

Roman Theatre of Orange, Vaucluse, France, 1st century AD. Once home of pantomime plays and the famous attelana farce (Roman masked comedy, largely improvised and relying on stock characters), the theatre is still in use, principally for the opera festival Chorégies d'Orange.
🏺 Ancientbluesky

Evidence in Cambridge’s Museum of Archaeology of Mesolithic contact between Britain and the Baltic: an amber pendant from Star Carr, best known for its ‘ritual’ antler headdresses, around 9000 BC. At the time, the Baltic Sea was a self-contained freshwater lake

A 16th century German 'oath skull' (a human skull on which defendants swore their oath in Vehmic courts) engraved with the 'magical' Roman 'Sator square', mysterious palindromic word-squares found across the Roman world, comprising the words SATOR, AREPO, TENET, OPERA, ROTAS

These Roman glass and gold portraits are wonderful and eerie in equal measure. The realism is breathtaking. You’re looking into the eyes of people who lived 2,000 years ago and it’s haunting yet exhilarating. They generally date to the 2-3rd century CE. 

🏺 AncientBlueSky

The 11c St Sophia Cathedral in Kjiv inspired by Ag Sophia in Constantinople; much of the work was done by craftsmen from that city. Examples of the striking mosaics in the #Byzantine style: The Annunciation (the 2 figures are on adjacent columns); Church Fathers; the Eucharist; the High Priest Aaron

A late 4th c. fragmentary gray marble krater/vase with a scene of the Magi approaching the Virgin Mary and child Jesus in the upper register. On the other side (not seen here) is Christ enthroned among the apostles.

Palazzo Massimo Museum, #Rome
📷🇮🇹 flic.kr/p/2oKmoNs

🏺AncientBlueSky

Spearhead typologies for the ancient Mediterranean are wild. On one hand, you can describe ~90% of all spearheads from 600BC to 1AD in all cultures with, "iron spearhead with a 'leaf' or 'tear-drop' shape, conical socket and mid-ridge." 

But then there are, like, 17 subtypes. 1/

5th c. BCE small Greek terracotta naiskos (shrine) with the god Hermes and two goddesses or 'nymphs'. They all have large shield fibulae pinning their clothes at their shoulders. Clear remains of the original pigments. #polychromy 

Tanagra (Boeotia, Greece)
Altes Museum, Berlin

Church of the Archangels at Sige on the southern shore of the Sea of Marmara in Bithynia (modern Kumyaka, Bursa Province). The church, which is currently being restored, is at its core a domed basilica (c. 8th-century) with additions made in the Byzantine and Ottoman eras.

No, this isn’t the earliest known representation of Sam the Eagle from the Muppets. 

It’s a bronze griffin’s head which once adorned the rim of a cauldron given as a votive offering (probably to Zeus) at Olympia. 

It’s late C7th BCE and - to be frank - is fantastic. 

🏛️📷NY Met
🏺 AncientBlueSky

Journeying deep into Siberia's trackless taiga in 2019, archaeologists found forts built by hunter-gatherers 8,000 years ago. It's evidence that farming wasn't a prerequisite for complex societies – or monumental architecture – from a surprising part of the world.

A well-preserved example of a caliga – a Roman soldiers' leather shoe with hobnails hammered into the leather sole, providing a good traction both in dirt and on rough surfaces. However, on smooth surfaces such as pavement, they were quite slippery.
 
Photo: Musée d'Archéologie Saint-Germain-en-Laye

I love the archaeological term is "dark earth." It's a rich, almost black soil that shows up in English cities between the Roman levels (1st-5th C) & the Saxon urban revival (8th-9th C). Centuries of decay of Roman walls, doors, fences, thatch, life. Gone. But not gone. 🏺

The most magnificent of all ancient #Roman ‘cage cups’ is the 4th c. CE Lycurgus Cup, made of dichroic glass. In normal light, the glass appears milky green, when backlit, it glows a ruby red. The effect was made by adding silver and gold nanoparticles. 🏺 1/

📸 my own

A marble carpet in Siena's Duomo, created over the course of six centuries. Two thirds remains covered with special panels that protect it. You can admire the uncovered floor every year from 18 August until the end of October. Here: a detail of Matteo di Giovanni's Massacre of the Innocents.

Oh hey, it's my last CNN article for 2023! Did you know that fluctuations in Earth's magnetic field were preserved in baked mud bricks from the days of Nebuchadnezzar II? 
Archaeomagnetic analysis linked stamped, dated bricks to a power surge in the magnetosphere over Mesopotamia 3000 years ago 🧪🏺

This is a wonderful example of mid-C5th BCE Cypriot sculpture. It combines Greek, Persian & local styles, clothing & hair. The fellow is a very high status worshipper (identified by his garland) and this is his votive offering to a local god later associated with Artemis. 

🏛️BM
📷me
🏺 AncientBlueSky

This 1,700-year-old blue glass bowl, a little more than 8 inches in diameter, was found buried next to a woman at the center of a cemetery at the Roman settlement of Emona in present-day Ljubljana in Slovenia.  Its Greek inscription reads: "Drink to live forever, for many years!"

A glass Roman inkwell, in a lovely rich blue with white decoration - a relatively everyday & functional item, & yet a beautiful one. It was made 1700-2000 years ago - hard not to wonder what documents it once helped write!

Roman bronze busts of Silenoi ~ c. C1st BCE–C1st CE

These silenoi of Dionysus are thought to have decorated a banqueting couch - a very likely spot indeed for the drinking of wine and possible bad behaviour!

🏛 Art Institute, Chicago, 1997.554.1-2; original provenance unknown
🏺ClassicsBluesky

St Mary’s Church, Atherington, Devon brasses of Sir John Bassett (d.1529) and  2 wives, Anne Denys and Honor Grenville (1493-1566) 5 children to left, 7 to right. Honor’s 2nd husband was Arthur Plantagenet,illegitimate son of Edward IV. 

#historicalfiction #devon #history

Of all Pompeii's majesties, this humble terracotta drainpipe embedded in a stone wall is one of my faves. It shows that even in a cheap tenement above a noisy bar, there was sanitation. A private toilet.

That kind of amenity wouldn't be regularly found again in Western cities until the 20th C. 🏺

Greek bronze statuette of Athena flying her #owl (ca. 460 BCE). Owls of a type that can still be seen flitting about the Akropolis of Athens were associated with Athena, whose sanctuary was on top of that high rocky plateau.

🗃️🏺🏛️ Met AncientBluesky ClassicsBluesky art

This tubby pale green blown glass beaker - made in Gaul - is gorgeously decorated with three rows of mold-pressed green glass lion masks. Imagine greeting your guests with a set of these.🏺 

#Roman, 4th c. CE 
Excavated in Reims #BritishMuseum (1877,0507.2) 
📸 my own #ancientglass AncientBlueSky

The forum and Byzantine fort in Madauros, Algeria. It became a Roman colony for veterans in the 1st century. It was the birthplace in 124AD of the Numidian Apuleius who wrote the only Latin Roman novel, "The Golden Ass", to survive intact. St Augustine of Hippo also studied there.

Ancientbluesky 🏺

More spolia from my recent trip to the borderlands - the entire porch of the old schoolhouse in Rochester, Northumberland is built from carved Roman stones plundered from the ruins of Bremenium fort nearby. I particularly love the cute little window arch.

This Roman opus sectile (left) was found at Fishbourne Palace. It’s a wonderful decorative art. It ranges from simple but elegant designs like this to monumental artistic gems like this mid-C4th CE tigress attacking a calf now in the Capitoline museums. 

🏛️Fishbourne palace
📷mine 
🏺 AncientBlueSky

It’s #FindsFriday today so here’s a complete 14thC annular (ring-shaped) shoe buckle, tiny piece of medieval shoe leather still attached. Made of iron, pleased to have found an intact one as most of these are heavily corroded or the buckle is missing. Just imagining how this ended up in the river.

Outcrop of metasandstone in the 1.7 billion year old Yavapai Schist in central Arizona USA that has some intriguing relict sedimentary structures, including trough and hummocky cross-stratification and cobble-sized rip-up clasts. The huge hummocky crossbeds, with rip-ups, could be a tsunamite. 🧪⚒️

A rare relief fragment that I saw last year at the #BrooklynMuseum (47.120.1). Egyptian reliefs and paintings almost never portray elderly persons. Here, this courtier shows a lined face, sagging muscles, jowls, and deeply lined palms. A legacy of the Amarna period. 

Ca. 1336-1213 BCE, Saqqara.

I gave an interview this weekend on Neanderthals and early H. sapiens grinding wild plants into flour, so figured I'd post about it here as well to give bluesci a shot 🧪In short, our team found evidence from two sites in Italy pushing back flour production to 43k years ago. 1/n

This is statue of Saint Bartholomew, an early Christian martyr who was allegedly skinned alive. Notice: that's not a robe that he’s holding. It's his dissected skin. This stunning statute is by the Italian sculptor Marco d’Agrate, c.1562.

Les Catacombes de Paris ossurary contains remains of more than 6 million people. Tunnels are originally from mining Lutetian limestone (Paris stone) orig formed in Eocene epoch's Lutetian age (47.8 - 41.2 Ma). Stone quarried here used to build Louvre, Les Invalides, & Arc de Triomphe.

Some of the most intricately decorated pottery of Bronze Age Transylvania: Wietenberg Culture vessels with original white inlays from Cluj-Strada Banatului, c. 16th century BCE. MNIT Cluj-Napoca. #archaeology 🏺

This beautiful, shimmering, gleaming mosaic is 12th century from the Ducal Palace in Palermo. This particular piece primarily shows the Greek/Byzantine influence in Norman-Sicilian art but the lancet arch is Arabic & the Corinthian capitals are most likely derived from Roman examples. 

📷 mine

This cameo depicts Drusus, the younger brother of Tiberius. With its plasticity, depth and detail, not to mention the striking azure colour, it is, quite simply, a masterpiece of cameo work.  

🏛️📷Kunsthistoriches Museum, Vienna
🕰️ c 20 CE
🏺 AncientBlueSky

Standing locks of wrestling and dagger wrestling, from a 1910 facsimile of a 1410 manuscript mostly known as the Flos Duellatorum (Latin) - The Flower of Battle. It would be another 100 years before Europeans first set foot in Japan. 

First up, Standing Straight Armlocks: 🤼

An amazing Roman glass bowl in pale green transparent glass, made in a 'handkerchief' style shape with a wavy rim - which makes it look very modern, despite being made 1600-1700 years ago! (Photo: Corning Museum of Glass)

Blue Glass Beads; from late Bronze Age (3300-1200 BC). Found in 1885 when a farmer ploughed up a cremation urn at Kongehøj in Denmark.

Recent analysis shows they were made in Mesopotamia, 3100 years ago. Evidence of long-distance trade connections. 
📷 : National Museum of Denmark

#archaeohistories

So #TIL that many of the windows in Orvieto, Italy's 14th C Cathedral are paned not in glass but in thin-sliced alabaster! The idea was to let in light but keep out some of the roasting heat.

MedievalSky

A Roman tile from London with a tale to tell... scratched into its surface are the words "for the last 13 days Austalis has been wandering off on his own every day" - a way for one annoyed worker to try & get a shirking colleague in trouble, 1800-1900 years ago?

The tuff cliffs at Bandelier National Monument were deposited by volcanic eruptions over a million years ago then eroded by water and some of these layers eroded pockets like Swiss cheese. These ‘cavates’ were then used by the Ancestral Pueblo peoples as dwellings after their further excavations.🌿
The “nude goddess” of Babylon (1stC BCE-1stC CE) figurine has ruby eyes. Where did the likely come from? Myanmar. Evidence for long distance trade in antiquity is all around us.

Roofer's guarantee: Tho Everard laid me here. He said to l(ive) an hundred (y)ear - 1714'
Lead roof section from St. Mary, Ashwell in Hertfordshire.

This Babylonian kudurru stele records the gift of land from a father, Nirah-nasir, to his daughter, Dur-Sharrukinaia'itu, on the occasion of her marriage. Probably deposited in a temple, it gave her control over her own property. 🏺

Serpentine, 1100-1083 BCE 
Bibliothèque nationale de France  1/

The amazing Nijmegen Helmet, a Roman 'cavalry sports' silvered bronze & iron helmet with facemask, brow band, & side ear/neck guards (but missing the back of the helmet). It was made about 1900-2000 years ago, & found around 1915 in a bank of the river Waal 🏺AncientBlueSky

Detail of Patroclus & Briseus as she is being taken away.
Patroclus had promised her she would wed Achilles, but when she returns, Patroclus has already been slain. She & Achilles are both left to lament him.
The House of the Tragic Poet in Pompeii contains many Iliadic scenes.

🏺 Ancientbluesky

Dendera Temple, Egypt. Relief depicts Cleopatra & her son with Julius Caesar Caesarion.
After Cleopatra’s suicide, Caesarion fled but Octavian called for his return. Arius Didymus reportedly said, ‘Too many Caesars are not good.’ Octavian had Caesarion (aged 17) killed in 30 BC.

🏺Ancientbluesky

This is the Lycurgus Cup. It’s made from dichroic glass, meaning it changes colour depending on the direction the light comes from (red from behind, green from front). It shows Lycurgus’ death. He tried to kidnap Ambrosia who was turned into a vine which then strangled him
🏛️📷BM 🏺 AncientBlueSky

A Roman Samian ware dish, made around 1900-2000 years ago. Not much unusual about it - apart from having part of an oyster shell attached to its surface, suggesting it was once lost in a shipwreck, & only recovered long after it had become part of the underwater landscape.

The fortifications of Palaiokastro, the classical and Hellenistic Acropolis of Nisyros, an island near Kos in the South Aegean. 

Built in the mid-fourth century BC, these walls encircled the ancient city which has not been excavated.

📷 Discovering Kos.

🏺Ancientbluesky
"Sleeping Hermaphroditus," an ancient Greek marble sculpture, unearthed in Rome ca. 1600-1610, now in the Louvre. The mattress, by Bernini, was added in 1620.

Crumbs this is special. It’s a glass model of a boat. It was found at Pompeii & was made in the 1st half of C1stCE. The craftsmanship & skill involved in making this is beyond my comprehension, but what’s truly arresting for me is the sleek shape and intense colouring. A marvel
🏛️📷BM 🏺 AncientBlueSky

Chiricahua Nat. Monument (SE Arizona) has fabulous landscapes, views, and especially stone spires, pinnacles, and balanced and tumbled rocks, shaped by erosion after a cataclysmic volcanic eruption 27 million years ago covered the area with ash that hardened into rock. Gets remarkably few visitors!

The large sardonyx Blacas Cameo, one of the Augustan ‘State Cameos’ from the imperial inner court. It depicts the ageless emperor from behind, turned to the left, wearing the aegis of Jupiter and Minerva. 

Made in Rome, ca. 14-20 CE.

Taken 21 Dec 2023 at the #BritishMuseum 🏺 

AncientBluesky  1/

Kourion, an ancient city on the southwestern coast of Cyprus, was settled by Greeks from Argos who arrived in the 12th century. 

This mosaic depicts 2 gladiators being separated by a referee, late 3rd century CE, from the House of the Gladiators, Kourion.

This splendid Greek bronze panoply is from the mid-C4th BCE and was made in southern Italy. The muscled or anatomical cuirass is particularly splendid and even includes nipples and navel. I can’t say it was modelled on my torso sadly.

A childlike and rather terrifying cupid pipes to Heracles in the House of Marcus Lucretius, Pompeii. 
The Latin name Cupīdō refers to earnest or passionate desire. Less romantic, the English word, cupidity, derives from the same root.
🏛 Naples Archaeological Museum

This rhyton, or drinking vessel, is shaped like a goat’s head & is from Greek southern Italy. Given the number of similar objects found, it’s likely a mass production piece. That said, the quality of the moulding & decoration shows production values were high

The cathedral of Syracuse, Sicily, is a typically wild, ornate baroque extravaganza but inside, & built into its very fabric, are the remains of the vast Doric Temple of Athena built by Gelon after defeating the Carthaginians at Himera in 480BCE. Magnificent

📷mine 🏺 AncientBlueSky

No, these aren’t ancient ashtrays, you decadent beasts. They’re Roman glass dishes from Corfu (left) and Egypt (right). Both date from around the reign of Augustus and they’re delightful. Anyone familiar with Venetian glasswork will see similarities with millefiori.

📷 mine

To mark the winter solstice, a 1st century Roman sundial from Baelo Claudia (Bolonia, Cádiz), given a monumental backdrop at Museo Arqueológico Nacional, Madrid. 
Sunlight enters through the pinhole opening in the small dome. Eleven lines mark the hours.
🏺 AncientBlueSky
3rd century Roman mosaic bath sign depicts bathing sandals, curved strigils (to scrape off dirt, oil & sweat) & notice saying SALVOM LAVISSE (“bathing is good for you”) Roman Museum Sabratha, Libya

MosaicMonday, a panel from a larger mosaic depicting Odysseus escaping from Polyphemus by clinging to the underside of a ram. Dated to the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE. Found in Rome, now in the Museo Nazionale Romano, Palazzo Massimo alle Terme.

AncientBlueSky 🏺

Chepstow Castle, Monmouthshire is the oldest surviving post-Roman stone fortification in Britain. Located above cliffs on the River Wye, construction began in 1067 under the instruction of the Norman Lord William FitzOsbern. 

It was the southernmost of a chain of castles built in the Welsh Marches.

The most common perception of Classical sculpture - popular in fascist profile pics - is the kind of idealized human form found in Polykleitos. But by the Hellenistic era sculptors were exploring all kinds of bodies - including those marked by poverty, age, and combat (as here)

It’s #AncientSiteSunday! Today, Trier (or Augusta Treverorum) in western Germany. Here the Porta Nigra (started 170), the Aula Palatina (early 4th century), the imperial baths (also early 4th century) & the amphitheatre (mid-2nd century). 

Incredible remains.

🏺 AncientBlueSky 
ClassicsBlueSky

Porphyry pieces, including slab of an imperial Byzantine sarcophagus with cross, in the courtyard of Süleymaniye Mosque, which was built by the Ottoman architect Mimar Sinan in the 1550s. Porphyry, a purple stone from Egypt, stopped being quarried around the 450s.
Photos taken yesterday

Working on an upcoming post on Brundisium last week, and from the Museo Archeologico Francesco Ribezzo in Brindisi, the bronze head of a philosopher, perhaps Antisthenes. Possibly the work of Silanion. From the waters of Punta del Serrone, dated to the 4th century BCE.

AncientBlueSky 🏺

C.3rd century AD painting in the Catacombs of San Callisto, ‘The Good Shepherd’ is one of the oldest  depictions of Christ, here in Roman style dress.
Jerome’s translation of Luke 2:11 reads: ‘quia natus est vobis hodie salvator qui est Christus Dominus in civitate David.’
🏺Ancientbluesky

This is the oldest preserved human brain 🧠 at 2600 yrs old, found in Heslington, Yorkshire, UK in 2008.

The original owner was hanged, decapitated & the head immediately buried in mud.  Presumed ritual murder/sacrifice from the British Iron Age.

It's shrunk to 20% its original volume.

El Infiernito (Spanish for "Little hell"), is a pre-Columbian Muisca site in Columbia! It is composed of several earthworks, standing stones and burial mounds are also present. The site was a center of religious ceremonies and probably also served as a rudimentary astronomical observatory. 🏺More 1/2

This remarkable fragment of a 2nd c. CE Roman #mosaic from Carthage is featured in the exhibition ‘African & Byzantium’ at the #MetMuseum. In curator’s comments, it’s described as ‘men preparing a feast for a festival.’ To be exact, these are slaves, from all over the empire. 🏺 1/

This pre-Roman British shield, aka the Battersea shield, was fished out of the Thames in 1857. It is rightly famous. The work of a master craftsman, it is simple, yet breathtakingly beautiful with its elegant, sinuous lines. Old Father Thames, more please 
🏛️BM📷me
🏺 AncientBlueSky

The famous monumental statue of the Mexica (Aztec) goddess Coatilcue was buried by the Spanish invaders near the site of the destroyed Templo Mayor. Rediscovered in 1790, Antonio León y Gama made these illustrations of it for his 1792 book on the object, before it was reburied.

I had never heard of the Temple of Olympian Zeus at Akragas (Agrigento), but it was apparently the largest Doric temple ever built (though maybe it was never finished?). The "Atlas" figures are thought to be defeated enemies. Much of it now makes up the local dock.

Mosaic from the Villa of Fannius Synistor.
What I love is that the central motif is a representation of four gates as frequently seen in frescoes but it’s also a form that inspired the modern gates in Pompeii; at the Villa of the Mysteries the shadow of one such gate is cast across a mosaic floor. 🏺

Why is Jesus depicted as having long hair & beard?

In the oldest depictions, early 3rd century Rome, he's drawn as a typically middle-class Roman: clean-shaven with short, neatly cut hair, wearing a 'toga virilis', the national costume of Roman citizens.

Cataloguing some Iron Age animal bone from Prestatyn, Denbighshire and gasped when I noticed this butchered red deer femur - look at those cut marks! The fracture! 🍖 

Such intimate details of everyday life in the past make archaeology so special  ❤️

c.550-400 BC 
#FindsFriday

This Minoan jug dates from c2000 BCE & is typical of the Kamares style. This type of pottery depicts highly stylised, abstract floral or other natural motifs using red & white on a black background. It was used as elite tableware. It feels & looks very 20thC modernist.

🏛️📷Heraklion
🏺 AncientBlueSky

The tomb of 28 year old Maria Magdalena Langhans, who died during childbirth on the day before Easter in 1751. Sculpted by Johann August Nahl, it shows her and her baby breaking out on the day of resurrection. A copy of the stone in the parish church of Hindelbank, near Berne.

In 1972, Stefano Mariottini was snorkelling off the coast of Monasterace near Riace when he noticed a human hand sticking out of the sand. Thinking it was a corpse, he called the police. It was 2 ancient Greek bronze statues of "Warriors from Riace" from the 5th century BC. David Attenborough Fans.

From the most recent excavations, fragments of a painted ceiling from a house in #Pompeii, showing the lush abundance of nature's bounty. 

Posted on the former blue bird site by Gabriel Zuchtriegel, director of the Pompeii Archaeological Park.

Remarkably well-preserved 4th-century Byzantine mosaic from ruins of a villa in Mariamin, Syria, depicts women musicians playing organ, double flute, lyre, forked cymbals, and an eight-bowl xylophone-ish instrument. Two young boys pose as cherubs at far left

This is the so-called Coppergate Helmet, named after its find location in York. It’s Anglo-Saxon & one of only 6 helmets surviving from that period. If you look at the nose guard, it has wonderful fantastical animals weaving through the decoration

🏛️Yorkshire Museum
📷Museum Trust
🏺 AncientBlueSky

Ancient Olympic Games, quadrennial athletic event that was held in Olympia, Greece, from 776 BCE to about 393 CE. It was part of a religious festival that honoured Zeus, and the name Olympics was derived from Mount Olympus, home of the Greek gods.

The Kandariya Mahadeva Temple, meaning "the Great God of the Cave", is the largest and most ornate Hindu temple in the medieval temple group found at Khajuraho in Madhya Pradesh, India. It is considered one of the best examples of temples preserved from the medieval period in India.

In addition to the oldest examples of basketry known in Europe, dating to 9500 BCE, la Cueva de los Murcielagos in Spain, is also home to the oldest known pair of shoes in Europe! (Photo Credit: MUTERMUR project). #Archaeology 🏺

The Moundville Duck Bowl, a stunning example of Indigenous Mississippian culture and craft, carved from a single piece of stone. Discovered in the early 1900s in Alabama. Made in the 13th or 14th C. 📷 Moundville Archaeological Park

Book bag under one arm, squirming Christ child under the other ~ carved by an unknown master from a single block of limestone, this is the 3/4 life-size polychrome Nostre Dame de Grasse, dating from late-15th century (photo: Nick Thompson; Musée des Augustins, Toulouse)

The hominin skull from Steinheim, Germany is around 250,000 years old. It likely belongs to an early Neandertal population, but has been the subject of debate ever since it was uncovered in a gravel quarry in 1933.

Twelve Roman amphorae lost in a shipwreck off the coast of the Mediterranean island of Gozo 2100+ years ago; they then lay on the seabed until underwater archaeology brought them to the surface in 1961.

Excavations underneath Basilica San Giovanni in Laterano (Rome) revealed vivid fresco in what were 2nd c. CE elite houses, followed by a complex sequence of spatial reuse and rebuilding ending up with the first ever cathedral. 
🏺 ClassicsBlueSky

Very sexy folds developed in thinly interbedded limestones and shales of the Cretaceous Palombini shale formation at Acquarilli beach, Island of Elba.

This is one of the formations that was squeezed in an accretionary prism before the collision that generated the Northern Apennines.

Near Naples, in Calvizzano, the superintendence has just discovered a superb hypogeum tomb imitating a banquet hall. I've pasted three screenshots to give a clearer idea. Behind the intact doorway, three stone beds with paintings immortalizing fabrics, and funerary furniture in place 🏺AncientBlueSky

A megalithic monument in Spain that's older than the Pyramids was recently uncovered from its watery hiding place by a drought. 

The "Spanish Stonehenge" is about 7,000 years old, some 2,000 years older than Stonehenge itself.

Longmen Grottoes in Henan Province, China. Over 2,300 caves filled with over 100,000 statues of all sizes. 4th-10th-centuries CE

This is a UNESCO-recognized World Heritage site, representing the pinnacle of Chinese stone carving art.

Archaeologists in Germany have unearthed a sword from a Bronze Age burial, and the weapon is in such good condition that it still gleams. The 3,000-year-old sword, discovered in the town of Nördlingen in Bavaria, was found in the burial of a man, woman and child.

Hunter-gatherer societies on the Iberian Peninsula were making sophisticated baskets with decorative geometric patterns 9,500 years ago, more than 2,000 years earlier than previously thought, researchers in Spain have reported.

Bigger than life-size 1st century reproduction of the statue of Athene Promachos which stood on the Acropolis. Original by Pheidias c.456 BC. 

This marble statue was discovered in the Villa dei Papiri, Herculaneum. 

🏛 National Archaeological Museum Naples 

🏺Ancientbluesky

Santa Maria del Fiore, Florence.  More commonly known as the Duomo. The largest dome built in 1000 years

Thought I'd be obsessed with that dome, but really I can't get over the fit and finish of the tri-color marble cladding. Its patterns are visually perfect. Impossible to overstate the skill here
The Stollhof hoard (Austria) contains some of the earliest worked gold of Europe: two small gold discs. 4th millennium BCE. NHM Vienna. #Archaeology 🏺

Bologna's leaning tower: The Garisenda Tower in Bologna is not as famous as the Tower of Pisa, but it leans a little more. Lately, though, the dynamic of its movement has become worrisome, and city officials have closed a city-centre square as they study what to do about the tower.

Bramante’s Tempietto, door from inside.
Donato Bramante, Tempietto, c. 1502, San Pietro in Montorio, Rome. The “Tempietto” or little temple is a martyia (a building that commemorates a martyrdom) that marks the traditional site of Saint Peter’s crucifixion.

Revisiting the intricately-carved stone latticework of ‘The Tree of Life’ in the window of 16th-century Sidi Saiyyed Mosque in Ahmedabad, India, named after the Ethiopian nobleman (& ex-slave) who commissioned it

The shaman of Bad Dürrenberg are the remains of a 25-35 year old woman, who was buried 8600 to 9000 year ago in Germany. Around her, were the remains of an extraordinary head-dress, made from the bones and teeth of different animals such as deer, wild boar, crane and turtle.
via Dialogist//twitter

For ClassicsTober day 3; part of a statue depicting Asterion, the Minotaur. A 1st century CE Roman copy of a group with Theseus dating to the 5th century BCE, possibly sculpted by Myron. Found in Rome and now in the Museo Nazionale Romano, Palazzo Massimo alle Terme.

AncientBlueSky 🏺
The Pantheon Dome in Rome, made of concrete, was built between 25 and 27 BCE, and still standing without props or any other means of support.

New research confirms that fossil human footprints in New Mexico are likely the oldest direct evidence of human presence in the Americas, a finding that upends what many archaeologists thought they knew about when our ancestors arrived in the New World. 📰

Reliefs of battles, hunts, weddings, and legends on Roman sarcophagi are fine, but take a moment to admire the heads or masks carved at the corners (acroteria) of the lids. These are four of my favorites from the Capitoline Museums, Rome.
🏺 AncientBlueSky

A #Roman glass vessel in the shape of a mouse, with a widened tail pointing upwards as the neck of the vessel. It is made out of beautiful blue glass, with added decorations giving the mouse features - it is a lovely & unusual piece of ancient glass.

Besides their role as tools or weapons, axes always had social or spiritual meaning - as markers of status or ritual paraphernalia. 
Gold double axe, one of 19 from the Arkalochori Cave, Crete, 1700-1600 BCE. Museum Heraklion. #Archaeology 🏺

Oldest-surviving anatomical theatre, located in Padua and built in 1594. Over the entrance is the Latin inscription: “hic est locus ubi mors gaudet succurrere vitam.” Roughly: “this is the place where death delights to help life (or the living).” 

Photo: Rolando Paolo Guerzoni.

I’ve written before about the astonishing discoveries at Gobekli Tepe, at 12,000 years the world’s oldest megalithic site and one that is completely rewriting what we know about prehistory. They’re still excavating, still turning up more and more head-spinning stuff, like this statue…

A Roman iron trident-head, found during excavations in central London. It may have been used by a gladiator who fought in the Londinium amphitheatre, which is just a mile away from the find site - if so, a retiarius gladiator, who fought with a trident & net AncientBlueSky

Layers of history: here we see a fancy mosaic floor that graced a large room in a large Roman building which now resides 2-3 feet below the paving of Chichester Cathedral’s choir ambulatory. 

📷mine
🏺 AncientBlueSky

So this is cool: Las Trampas Wilderness sits on a couple of fault lines + its mountains were formed by seismic and volcanic activity. These are shell fossils from when this area was part of the seashore 3-4 million years ago!!
Venus of Brassempouy is a 25,000 year old ivory figurine discovered in France and is possibly the oldest representation of the human face

MosaicMonday; the emblema of a large mosaic depicting Bellerophon slaying the Chimera from atop Pegasus. Dated to the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE, the mosaic was found in Autun. It is now displayed in the Musée Rolin in Autun.

AncientBlueSky 🏺

It's WorldToiletDay today, and I've always had an appreciation for Roman latrines passed down from my MA advisor. Perhaps my favorite latrines from this summer at Aquinum (Aquino); all original, no reconstruction.

A bronze of Hadrian found in England. Any bronze statue surviving antiquity is rare, but in England rarer still. It’s an interesting piece - visually striking with penetrating eyes & purposeful mouth, but rudimentary is some ways, eg the overly long neck, the low forehead

The Golden Hat of Berlin is one of 4 found in Europe, all from the Bronze Age, and is also the best preserved hat among them.  These hats are actually astronomical calendars with which the seasons were calculated for the benefit of agricultural production. 🏺🏺🏺

10th century BC, Germany.

In Egypt, 3,300 years ago ~ a tawny cat hunts birds among marsh papyrus stems, as butterflies flutter about / & a Red Egyptian Goose displays the fine detail of its feathers (Nebamun Tomb Chapel, Thebes, 1350 BC, (c) British Museum) artinsociety.com/lost-masterp...

The Countdown to St. Patrick's Day.  This is the Giant's Causeway in County Antrim.   It's an area with 40,000 interlocking basalt columns, created over 50-million years ago. This is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.   The album cover for Led Zeppelin's "Houses of the Holy” was photographed here.

Researchers were able to demonstrate on the basis of the discovery of a more than 35,000-year-old perforated baton made from mammoth ivory at Hohle Fels Cave in Germany together with experimental studies, a way people of the early Upper Paleolithic manufactured rope
phys.org/news/2024-02...

Last month, the Maison Carrée (square house in French) was named a UNESCO World Heritage Site. 

It was inaugurated in 5 AD in the Forum of Nemausus (Nîmes) as a temple to Gaius and Lucius Caesar, grandsons & heirs of Augustus.

For FrescoFriday, from an unknown location in the vicinity of Pompeii, a portrait medallion depicting Hercules and Omphale, who can be seen wearing Hercules' lion skin. Now in the Museo Archeologico Nazionale di Napoli.

Are we doing FrescoFriday here on BlueSky? Well, regardless, enjoy these details from the gorgeous Garden of Livia - a beautiful Nymphaeum decorated in stunning technique to resemble a lush garden filled with birds. Now at the Palazzo Massimo museum in Rome!

🦔 Ancient Cycladic hedgehog! This little guy is from Chalandriani, Syros, Greece. He dates to the Early Cycladic II period (2800-2300 BCE). National Archaeological Museum, Athens, Greece. 📷 My own.

"I have come from the City. I bring you a welcome gift with a sharp point that you may remember me," says a Roman stylus found in London, most likely brought there from Rome.

A bit of a #Roman glass rarity. A spoon. 

Said to have been found near the Sea of Galilee, it was made from a single blown tube that was then tooled to shape the bowl and handle. Rare, made for the luxury market during the Roman period, lasting into Islamic times. 🏺

This astonishing landscape portrays Perseus rescuing Andromeda from ketos, a sea monster. It was found at the imperial villa of Agrippa Posthumus (murdered on the orders of Tiberius at his accession). It’s a bit different from the version in Clash of the Titans.

Yes, lots of people died at Pompeii and Herculaneum when Vesuvius erupted in A.D. 79 - but what about the far greater number of people who survived? Where did they go?

Fascinating archaeology article about how people moved on after major disasters in the ancient world:

Thermopolium of Lucius Vetutius Placidus, Pompeii. 

The ancient equivalent of the fast-food restaurant takes its name from the Greek θερμοπώλιον to describe a place where hot food is sold. In the fresco, the food is being watched over by the spirit of the house.

Archaeology often evokes digging, but (re)studying collections curated in museums is also a key part of the job. This past May/June, at the Museum of Albenga, our team began reanalyzing stone tools from Arma dello Stefanin made by some of the last hunter-gatherers in NW Italy ca. 10k years ago. 🧪

For #WorldBeardDay a rather wonderfully diverse take on ancient beards in ceramic, fresco, marble, and bronze mostly from around the Bay of Naples.

All seen in the National Archaeological Museum of Naples.

Carved stele inside the Table des Marchands, a Neolithic tomb at Locmariaquer, Brittany. The shapes may represent shepherds’ crooks.

📷Personal photo, July, 2023

Was in Nantwich today, and uncle pointed out this gargoyle of a woman with her hand in a jar being carried off by the devil. Apparently the stonemasons caught their landlady stealing from them, and added this to the church in her honour. Still there nearly 700 years later. That’ll teach her.

#OnThisDay - 30 October - in 1829 the House of Meleager, Pompeii (VI.9.2), was excavated and offered up some truly staggering painted stucco reliefs. Imagine eyes being cast on this for the first time in 1750 years. #Pompeii

Jewelry and a set of jeweler's tools were found stored in a pottery jug in Arad, Israel. The materials include shell, faience, bone, copper, and flint.  From the Early Bronze Age, c. 3000-2650 BCE.
At the Israel Museum, Jerusalem.

Boxed in.
Simple yet effective mosaic threshold with geometric design dating to the 1st century BC from the Roman Villa of Fannius Synistor in the Vesuvian area (Boscoreale). Currently on display in the Antiquarium at Boscoreale.

The Roman wall surrounding the city of Lugo in Galicia is the only complete Roman wall remaining. According to legend the Romans built it to protect a forest and not a city, the“Sacred Forest of Augusto”. The walls still contain 85 towers and have survived intact for 18 centuries.
ancientbluesky 🏺

Clay lamps may be some of my favourite Roman artefacts! This one's a little cracker, decorated with a fine scene of Odysseus, strapped to his mast, sailing past the Sirens: ca. Late 2nd Century AD. #Myth #Archaeology 🏺

Image: BnF (inv.65.5296). Link - medaillesetantiques.bnf.fr/ark:/12148/c...

I was in Abernethy last week to see @@504790 give a talk, which gave me the chance to visit and climb the town's amazing early medieval round tower. Built around 1100, it is one of only two in Scotland, although there are several in Ireland. Hard work, but worth it for the view!

This in Malmesbury (Wilts) is apparently the oldest inhabited building in England; converted into a house in the 1600s, it was originally the Saxon St Helen's Chapel, constructed around 900 AD. The large corner quoin (bottom right) is probably recycled from a Roman building.

The mosaic floor of the Alytarchus Stoa of Ephesus for this MosaicMonday. To the left is the protective roof of the terrace houses, to the right is the arch of Hadrian's Temple. Where people are walking on is the Curetes Street, one of the main streets of Ephesus.🙂🏺

For EpigraphyTuesday, a bronze helmet with a Greek inscription dedicating it to Persephone by Xena. Dated to the early 5th century BCE. Found in Locri, possibly from a Persephoneion in Manella. Now in the Museo Archeologico Nazionale di Napoli.

AncientBlueSky 🏺

Koi Krylgan Kala is an archeological site from Karakalpakstan Republic. Built by Iranian people from the kingdom of Khwarezm (a sister civilization of a perhaps more famous Sogdia) in 4 cent BCE. Koi Krylgan Kala was both a fortress, a settlement, and a Zoroastrian temple.

March in Cambridgeshire is well known for its Angel roof, however, set into the wall of the church hall opposite the church is this C17 relief that Pevsner suggests is a piece from a monument representing the Last Judgement. A pyramid of souls between two angels. A tad unsettling perhaps?

My scan Kingston Peak diamictite block on an alluvial fan. Block preserves evidence of snowball earth ~650Ma! Pebbles/cobble were trapped in bottom of glaciers that broke-into icebergs that floated to equator, melted & dropped stones and sediment onto/into seafloor sed

This looks to be a painted terracotta relief and the colours add to the sense of movement.

🏛 Pushkin Museum

Elite attire in the Mongol Empire: 

The aristocratic deel from the 13th-14th cent. cemetery at Bukhyn Khoshuu in Delgerkhaan County, Khentii Province, Mongolia.

#ClassicsTober23: Persephone. 

Every archaeological museum in Sicily is heaving with statues and other representations of Demeter and her daughter, Persephone. It shows how much corn growing meant to them. 

This polychrome example is from Syracuse &  is quite delightful

📷mine  🏺 ClassicsBlueSky

New OA paper by my team (lead: PhD student Silvia Gazzo) presenting taphonomic & archeological data to explain the presence of microshells (specifically Bittium) in Paleolithic sites, with Riparo Bombrini (Liguria, Italy) as a case study. We confirm their introduction was prob unintentional BUT... 🧪

I love the depth of his fresco. It feels like you’re actually standing in an orchard and could pluck one of those succulents fruits and gobble it up. It’s from the villa of Livia, Augustus’ wife, and is just one of many beautiful frescoes found there. 

🏛️📷 Palazzo Massimo, Rome
🏺 AncientBlueSky

Especially for @@42425, an ancient arch inside the church of St Andrew's, Corbridge. The building is mostly medieval, with a bit of Saxon, but this huge arch is all Roman. It was lifted from the ruins of the Roman town nearby and rebuilt here, a nice example of early recycling.

#TombTuesday - The Lion Tomb in the Qasr al-Bint Nabatean Necropolis of Hegra (now in Saudi Arabia). The tomb is decorated with a carving of two felines on either side of a rosette. The name of the stonemason who carved the tomb, Aftah, is engraved above the left feline.

The Crosby Garrett Helmet is an extremely fine Roman cavalry helmet dating from the late 2nd or early 3rd century AD. It consists of a face mask and a headpiece in the shape of a Phrygian cap topped by a griffin. It was discovered by a metal detectorist in Cumbria (UK) in 2010.

A selection of Roman glass vessels excavated from sites on the island of Malta; many of then would have been used to hold perfume or scented oil. They're quite simple in design, but still very striking, nearly 2000 years after they were originally made!

The 'Venus of Willendorf' was carved from limestone & tinted with red ochre by an unknown artist around 25,000 years ago.

Found at a Paleolithic dig site in Austria in 1908, it resembles other "Venus" figures from same period: it emphasizes aspects of female fertility.

But why?

24 Hephaestus, ferrarius et artifex deorum (blacksmith and master craftsman of the gods) fulmen fabricat (forges a thunderbolt) in his officina (workshop). One of his wheeled tripodes aurei (golden tripods) is waiting to deliver it to Zeus.
🏺ClassicsTober with 🐭 Minimus

I love this book. Though written in the 70s, its scholarship holds up. Horse collars, invented in China, reached Europe by the 900s and revolutionized agriculture.

The very first picture *anywhere* of a horse pulling a plough comes from the 11th C Bayeux Tapestry!

#medievalsky

Day 2 of my field trip looking at early medieval Moray and Nairnshire in Scotland. A 9th-century iron hand-bell, now in Cawdor Castle, but said to have come from the 14th-century church at Barevan nr Cawdor. Plus pics of the ruined church which preserves some of its gothic architecture.

medievalsky

Are you feeling peckish? If so, have some eggs, game birds and wash it all down with a jug of wine. This is a really marvellous fresco and reminds me of the famous Dutch still lifes of the 17th century. It’s actually from Pompeii.  

🏛️Naples Archaeological Museum
📷Kann Forget
🏺 AncientBlueSky

What these friezes from the temple of Apollo at Bassae may lack in elegance & sophistication, they make up for in drama & dynamism. The full friezes depict an Amazonomachy & a Centaurs & Lapiths battle. They vividly show the brutality & confusion of war with no clear winner

🏛️BM 📷me 🏺AncientBlueSky
A first-century-AD fresco from the Villa Arianna in Stabiae depicting a nereid reclining on a sea panther. She appears to be pouring a liquid into a dish. Naples National Archaeological Museum.

One of my favourite of Shropshire’s hidden gems is the 'Bayeux Tapestry of England'. In All Saints Church, Claverley. Spanning more than 15 metres in length, these lovely paintings date from the 13th century. The knights seem to leap to life. I'd definitely recommend a visit ✨️

A small but charming bronze Roman statuette of Pegasus. It was found in an auxiliary cavalry fort in Dormagen (Germany) - was the winged horse a symbol for the unit stationed there, or perhaps adopted as an unofficial mascot?

Although they look like they need a bit of a clean and polish, these blue glass lizards are delightful little objects. They’re Roman from the south of the Italian peninsula and date from the C1st BCE. 

🏛️ Archaeological Museum, Taranto
📷Mark Cartwright

🏺 AncientBlueSky

Proof that writer’s block is nothing new ~ this Pompeiian woman with gold-threaded hair engages us as she muses over her wax tablet. She’d soon have plenty to write about, so let’s hope she survived (Pompeii, 55-79AD, unearthed 1760, National Archaeological Museum of Naples)

I love old photos of ancient sites. This is a wonderful (anon) 19th century photo of the Roman Forum. 

Bullocks take a well earned break in front of the Temple of Castor and Pollux. In the background is the huge mass of the Basilica of Maxentius.

🏺 AncientBlueSky

malacca is a really concentrated hit of history: 16th century portuguese church (francis xavier's home base) got taken over by the dutch and used as a dutch reformed one, then in the 18th c the dutch let it go to ruin and built a new one... that the british soon took over and reconsecrated anglican

ReliefWednesday; a votive relief depicting Orpheus among animals, perhaps in a cave. A poet offers to him a scroll. Found at the acropolis of Sparta. Now in the Archaeological Museum of Sparta. Dated to the 1st century CE.

AncientBlueSky 🏺

Greek marble pelican’s foot shell ~ c. 400 BCE

The artistry involved in capturing nature is breath-taking. One of my favourite pieces from the ancient Greek world. Sadly, the provenance is not recorded…

🏛 The Met, 1995.19
🏺ClassicsBluesky

Remains of the Basilica of Maxentius and Constantine in the Roman Forum in Rome. Built between 308-312 CE, the 35 metres high basilica was the largest building in the Forum (with an area of about 5,600 square meters), and the last Roman basilica built in the city

From the archive ~ over 2,000 years old, a gold Greek roundel (medallion/disc) featuring bees at work. Incredibly, despite its level of detail, its recorded diameter is just 4cm (1½ inches) (Nasher Museum of Art, Duke University)

Bronze portrait of a Greek goddess ~ c. 300-270 BCE

This wonderfully detailed portrait suggests an idealised form of a goddess, often thought to be Artemis or Aphrodite, as both were connected with wavy and loosely bound hair.

🏛️ MFA Boston, 96.712
🏺AncientBluesky

AMAZING FINDS ALERT: not sure if this has been posted, but an incredible Hellenistic tomb was discovered in Calvizzano, Naples about a week ago. The tomb contains these frescoes - Cerberus with Hermes and another figure, and two sea-centaurs. Excavation is ongoing
📷Roberto Russo 
🏺 AncientBlueSky
Not quite "hidden", but still a gem: the Notre Dame de Mouzon, a former Benedictine abbey church in the Ardennes.

A lovely mosaic with a depiction of a menorah, a lulav, and a shofar at the center of the floor in the Jericho synagogue (also called the Shalom ‘al Yisrael Synagogue).

The synagogue was constructed toward the end of the Byzantine period or beginning of the Early Islamic period.

A self-reminder that Corleck Head exists: a three-faced stone idol from Corleck Hill, County Cavan, Ireland. About 2000 years old; unearthed some time in 19 century, but kept as a farm gate decoration for decades (which de-contextualized the artifact). Related to the so-called "Celtic head cult"

County Wicklow is the final edition of the Irish Archaeology Alphabet 🏺

The mountains and valleys of the 'Garden of Ireland' have a wonderful variety of monuments. Here's some of my favourites: the monastic complex of Glendalough, the mountaintop Seefin Passage Tomb, and Rathgall Hillfort.

The steel crown of the kings of Romania.

Forged from guns seized from the Ottomans at Plevna during the 1878 war of independence.

The ultimate symbol of 'FAFO'

This is the 14th-century pele tower in Corbridge, Northumberland. Built from recycled Roman stones, it was once the vicarage back in the days of constant Scottish raids. Nowadays it is an events venue with a gin bar on the ground floor.

County Meath is next in the Irish Archaeology Alphabet 🏺 

The Boyne Valley is ridiculously full of archaeology. Most associated with the great passage tombs of Newgrange, Knowth, and Dowth, but it has significant sites representing almost every era.

A few favourites; Newgrange, Tara and Loughcrew.

What a delightful little thing. This terracotta pig, whose back is decorated with small gobbets of coloured glass, is a child’s rattle. It’s Roman and dates to the 2nd century CE. So, if you’re ever feeling low, just think of this; it’ll stick a smile on your mush. 

🏛️📷NY Met
🏺 AncientBlueSky

This panel from a larger mosaic depicts the goddess Diana with two of her nymphs. They appear to have just been surprised by the arrival of the young hunter Actaeon. Things are not about to end well for the mortal!

📍Volubilis, C2nd CE
🏺ClassicsBluesky

Ferruccio Mengaroni’s colossal sculpture of Medusa, modelled on his own face. He was convinced that the piece would win him huge fame at the Monza Biennale of 1925. During installation, it fell, crushing him to death. Medusa survived intact. #WyrdWednesday

The famous golden fish from the Scythian hoard found near Vettersfelde / Witaszkowo in 1882. The presence of this hoard so far into northern Europe has been discussed controversially ever since. Around 500 BC. Berlin, Altes Museum. #Archaeology 🏺

Beeston Castle crag in Cheshire, reimagined as it might have looked in the Early Bronze Age period circa 4,500 years ago. No stone ramparts. The surrounding ritual landscape of wood pasture still intact with springs, some containing votive offerings, have possibly been in use for centuries.

#ReliefWednesday; from the Nationalmuseet in Copenhagen, a bronze relief depicting the god Sabazios surrounded by a variety of images associated with other deities. Dated to the 1st century CE.

🏺 AncientBluesky #RomanArchaeology

ReliefWednesday; votive stele dedicated to Mars Mider by Matutina, daughter of Cobnertus. The god is pictured with (according to the museum) a bull at his side. Dated the third century CE. Found in Forêt de Haguenau, now in the Musée Archéologique Strasbourg.

🏺 AncientBluesky

A startlingly beautiful image of an ancient Egyptian female acrobat painted on a c. 3,250 year old limestone potsherd. 
It was found in Deir el-Medina, the ancient village where the community of workmen and craftsmen responsible for the construction and decoration of royal and princely tombs lived.

Colossal Hittite lion statue, carved from basalt at the Ain Dara Temple complex (c1300 BCE) near Aleppo, Syria. Ain Dara was badly damaged by air raids during the Syrian Civil War, and the lion believed stolen.  

AncientBlueSky 🏺

Day 14 of Classicstober (Helen): from the House of Jason at Pompeii, a wall painting depicting Cupid/Eros bringing Helen and Paris together. Dated to the 1st century CE. Now in the Museo Archeologico Nazionale di Napoli.

AncientBlueSky 🏺

This is the Ashmolean Parchment, one of the oldest known love spells. Written in Coptic around 600 CE likely from Egypt, it binds Phello, son of Maure, to seek out and satisfy the desires of the caster, also listed in the text. Who cast it? Apapolo, son of Nooe.

Queer Pining: As old as time itself

We all love Minoan octopus pots, right, but this one is even more madly brilliant than the others. 

It’s absolutely humming with energy & detail, including the suckers, the sinuous writhing of the tentacles & the intense gaze. 

🏛️Heraklion Museum 
📷Wolfgang Strauber

For ClassicsTober and MosaicMonday, the central medallion of a mosaic depicting Medusa. Dated to the 2nd century CE. From Piraeus, now in the National Archaeological Museum in Athens.

AncientBlueSky 🏺

A little Roman child's shoe, delicately made from leather - this would have been a fairly expensive piece of footwear. It was found in the fort of Vindolanda (Britain), & may have once been owned by one of the fort commander's children 🏺AncientBlueSky
I am one of the volunteers who help to run a restored 18th century water-mill & we mill possibly the best stone-ground flour in Wales.  Look at this technology - hardly changed since Roman times.

Views from passages of cathedral crossing lanterns (tower with windows above high roofs of the main vessels). from oldest to newest, and also hairiest to safest.

St Albans (original build from the 1080s), Durham (1460s, above 1090s- crossing piers), Canterbury (1490-4, crossing earlier 15thc)

A late Roman jug, made of copper-alloy & decorated with a lovely enamelled pattern. It was made 1600-1800 years ago, & survives in amazing condition - aside from the fact that the green enamels seen today were originally red - as a beautiful piece of Roman metalwork! AncientBlueSky

Today's historical anthro art: a pirate transforms into a dolphin to escape the wrath of Dionysus and his satyrs after the crew tries to kidnap and ransom the god. From the frieze of Lysicrates' choragic monument in Athens, ca. 334 BCE, photo by Oxford's Ashmolean Museum.

Did you know that “curiosity” is not actually inherent to the story of Pandora as it exists in the ancient stories? It just became an easy way to blame a woman for something that was divinely ordained. #classicstober23

Marble statue of Dionysus, the god of celebration & wine. C. 1st-2nd century AD. His left hand is an 18th century restoration and holds a kantharos cup. In his right arm, he embraces a thyrsos associated with Dionysian revellers.

This helmet is, for me, the classic Roman legionary’s helmet (probably because it reminds me of the ones in Asterix). This example is C1stCE & is a technological marvel in terms of protection for the wearer, from the ridge across the brow, to the cheek pieces, to the neck guard

From the Getty Villa, a statue of a crouching Venus with Cupid. Dated to the first half of the 2nd century CE, based on a 3rd century BCE Hellenistic work attributed to Doidalsas of Bithynia.

This 6th century icon now on loan from the Monastery of Saint Catherine, Sinai, to the Metropolitan Museum is every bit as magnificent as the photographs promise -

A 4,500-year-old Egyptian dress was painstakingly reassembled from approximately 7,000 beads found in an undisturbed tomb in Giza, Egypt. The dress is thought to have belonged to a female contemporary of King Khufu (2589-2566 BC).

A beautiful garden scene from the fresco which once decorated the walls of a villa just outside central Rome, one which once belonged to the Roman empress Livia. This garden fresco is rightly still both well-known & much loved by people today! AncientBlueSky
Drinking with style: the 7-9th century CE hoard discovered in 1799 at Sânnicolau Mare/Nagyszentmiklós consists of 23 gold vessels - between them a golden drinking horn. KHM Vienna. #Archaeology 🏺

For FrescoFriday, a scene of a woman tuning a cithara as three other women listen and look on. Dated to 30-40 CE. From Pompeii. Now in the Museo Archeologico Nazionale di Napoli.

AncientBlueSky 🏺

I found this German helmet on the Somme in the early 1980s when I was 17. It was lying at the bottom of a mine crater at Carnoy, in the former no man’s land, where a farmer must have flung it after his plough dragged it up. The craters are long-since filled in.
Fragments of lustre-painted pottery found in excavations in Corinth, Greece, but which were made in Basra, Iraq in the 9th and 10th centuries. 🏺

This is a gold-mounted sardonyx cameo depicting the birth of Bacchus. Having helped deliver the baby, a nymph shows the infant to his mother, Semele, while a satyr looks on. A beautiful piece.

A fragment of Roman mosaic found in the remains of an ancient townhouse in Bath, England. The fact that it features mythical sea creatures implies that it could have once decorated a private baths. Now on display in the Roman Baths museum.

A Roman votive altar dedicated by Lucius Valerius Monteius to Cautes, a companion of Mithras, later reused in the city wall of medieval Barcelona; well, once their gods aren't important anymore, altars are just convenient blocks of worked stone...

Photo taken underneath the Piazza Navona in Rome reveals the reason for its unique shape: the remains of the Circus Agonalis, an athletics stadium built by the Emperor Domitian in the first century AD.

🏺Ancientbluesky

Visiting the fantastic Bronze Age exhibition in Bern, stunning objects such as the helmet made of the tusks of wild boars. From Mycenae, 16th c BC.
Hunting wild boars was dangerous, but popular with the Mycenaean elite. The helmet served as trophy and testified to bravery. 1/2

A bowl from Predynastic Egypt, made from Nile clay around 5500-5700 years ago. A beautiful red colour, its shape makes it tips forward slightly, perhaps as if to offer something - and best of all, it is supported by 2 little human feet!

Memory sanctions against Domitian as exemplified in this inscription from Puteoli now in the Penn Museum in Philly. I won’t ever forget Harriet Flower’s brilliant _The Art of Forgetting_ uncpress.org/book/9780807...

The beauty of Buildwas.
The English Heritage volunteer told us that the moles regularly dig up pieces of medieval pottery. 
Buildwas Abbey, Shropshire. Former home of the Cistercians.

Sousse Archaeological Museum.

Mystery sistrum: sistrums are often associated with the Egyptian gods, but this style is unique to ancient Etruria. As such it may provide early evidence for the introduction of the Egyptian cults in Italy. 

3rd century BCE. Now in the British Museum. AncientBlueSky 🏺

This is a beautiful necklace. It’s a late 2nd-early 1st century BCE Hellenistic piece and was discovered in a tomb still around the owner’s neck. It is made from gold, amethyst, chalcedony, emerald, rock crystal, pearl, and coloured glass. Truly wonderful.
