Exceptional things are also exceptionally hard to interpret in archaeology.  Golden scales. A symbol of power, a metaphor for the weighing of souls? Mycenae, Grave III, so-called Grave of the Women, Grave Circle A, 16th century BCE. National Museum Athens. #Archaeology 🏺

#OnThisDay - 12 October - in 1829 a favourite Roman fresco was discovered in the House of Meleager, Pompeii (VI.9.2). A grief-stricken Dido, alongside the personification of Africa, weeps as Aeneas departs. 🏺ClassicsBluesky

Image: National Archaeological Museum, Naples (8898)

🧪The volcanoes whose inner-most workings are exposed in the granitic rocks of the Sierra Nevada, transformed the ancient sediment layers into which they were intruded. Erosion later isolated the remnants of this metamorphic rock, suspending them above the granite as "roof pendants."👇🏼

Archaeological trenches around Basingwerk Abbey, Greenfield, Flintshire producing some medieval tile but no intact medieval contexts. What we have instead is massive remodelling of the topography in the post medieval period & a large amount of 1st-2nd c. Roman features & finds 1 metre down!
Birdoswald farmhouse on top of Birdoswald Roman fort on Hadrian’s Wall. The fort site has been almost continuously occupied since the Roman period

Feeling volcanic today, so here's some #Fieldwork prettiness for you! 

These basalt columns are found at Devils Postpile near Mammoth Lakes, California. After ponded lava filled the San Joaquin River valley 80,000 years ago, it cooled toward the flow's center, shrank, and cracked into polygons. ⚒️🧪🌋

Three ancient Roman shoes, all discovered at the fort of Trimontium in Scotland and now on display in the Trimontium Museum, Melrose. The Romans tanned leather by soaking it in tree bark and often decorated their shoes with fancy cut or stamped decoration.

The earliest known manufactured mirrors (approximately 8000 years old), found at the Neolithic site of Çatalhöyük in Turkey. These mirrors were made from obsidian (volcanic glass), and had a convex surface and remarkably good optical quality.

Before fleeing the eruption of Vesuvius, a resident of Moregine, near Pompeii, stashed this precious silver dining set into a container in a basement, hoping to return & retrieve it. No such luck ~ it lay there until excavated in 2000, perfectly preserved dererumgestarum.tumblr.com/post/6216030...

Standing outside the mouth of Arma dello Stefanin, July 2022. This site houses a long sequence of Upper Paleolithic, Mesolithic and Neolithic levels. Originally excavated in the 50s and 60s and then again in the 80s, my team and I have been working there for the past two years 🏺🧪

A very affecting 1st c BCE Greek bronze statuette of a frail old woman, her now-empty hands and the tilt of her head indicating she may have been shown in the act of spinning thread. Her chiton is slipping off her shoulder, and her himation is cinched at her waist. 

One of the Fates? 🏺 #GettyVilla

It’s almost #MosaicMonday and here’s one that would have delighted Agrippina the Younger - a dish of delicious mushrooms (minus the special sauce if you want to see tomorrow…). It’s mid-late 4th century CE and is in the Vatican Museums

This is a crackerjack example of ancient Egyptian glassware. This is an opaque, polychrome glass bottle in the shape of a ‘bulti’ fish. It dates from the 18th dynasty so it’s roughly mid-2nd millennium BCE. 

🏛️📷 British Museum

Wanapum flow of Columbia River Basalt entered a lake here, just south of I-84, a bit east of The Dalles on Rte 97. The transition from pillow basalt to columnar jointing ~indicates the lakes surface.⚒️ GeoHols for scale in left photo.

Fantastic outcrop of the Oberalm Fm. with marls and clay-rich beds (background sedimentation), and calcareous turbitites. 
The iconic peaks of the Barmsteine - landmark of my hometown -  were formed due to the resistance of the thick, almost vertical turbitite beds to erosive surface processes.

Stèle de Saint-Patrice monolith in the wall of a private garden, Lannion, Côtes-D'Armor. A large Christianized Gaulish stele which once held several statues of saints. Also called "menhir of justice", having probably served as a gallows. 🏺 More 1/

The oldest-surviving anatomical theatre in the world, built in 1594, and located in Padua. At the center stands a table on which the dissections of human or animal bodies took place. You can still visit it today. Photo: Daily Art Magazine.
Researchers working at a cave in southern Spain have found evidence that the skeletal remains of ancient humans buried there were dug up, modified and even used as tools by subsequent generations.

Saw this in Berlin in an excellent exhibition on miniatures and polychromy. It is a miniature shrine with statues of Hermes and two nymphs on a base from Tanagra in Boiotia (c.400-350 BC)! 

The roof is missing, but it would have finished off this Polly(theism) Pocket set.

The colossal bronze statue of Hercules that stands in the Vatican Museums. The 13-foot statue was unearthed in Rome in 1864, under a slab inscribed 'FCS' - Fulgor Conditum Summanium, indicating that the statue had been ritually buried after being struck by lightning.

When anthropology and geochemistry meets ! 🏺⚒️🧪

The Altamura man, Discovered in 1993 in a karstic Italian cave, was a Neanderthal that lived during the Middle-Upper Pleistocene between 170,000 and 130,000 BP. 

It is remarkably well preserved, thanks to the thick layer of calcite that grew on it.

A fascinating image of the more than human. A terracotta antefix showing a woman, possibly Artemis or an Amazon, on horseback with a goose(?) picking food beneath the horse’s hooves. 

Etrusco-Campanian, about 525-500 BCE, From Capua. Held by the British Museum AncientBlueSky 🏺

The C7thBCE Etruscan tomb of Montecalvario. It was an elite burial site with the most impressive grave good being the trappings of a chariot (here, in reconstructed form in the Museum of Sienese Chianti). The reconstruction is from the site
📷 AdamAh except the chariot which is mine 
🏺 AncientBlueSky

Is this the oldest street in Ireland? The Bronze Age village at Corrstown, near Portrush, had a wide cobbled street up to 10m wide and 95m long, with houses on either side and entrances opening onto it. 🏺

Statue of Vercingetorix, Alesia, France. 
 
Caesar’s noblest Gallic enemy surrendered himself to spare his men following the Battle of Alesia in 52 BC. Caesar himself lauded his opponent in his Commentaries and Plutarch also poignantly narrated the account in his Life of Caesar.

🏺 Ancientbluesky

Wow, I’ve never seen this before. It’s a Bronze Age pendant (c.1,000BCE)  thought to represent the sun that was found only 2 years ago in Shropshire. The profusion of triangles are almost hypnotic especially when combined with those incised lines that add texture and depth. 
🏛️📷BM 🏺 AncientBlueSky

A mummy portrait from Roman Egypt, depicting a young woman who lived & died just under 2000 years ago, shown wearing some beautiful jewellery. It is always amazing to see the face of an ordinary (if probably quite wealthy) person from the ancient world (Photo: Getty Museum) AncientBlueSky

#FrescoFriday with a touch of #FannyFriday thrown in for good measure. 

Erotic scenes were part of the vibrant culture of fresco painting in Pompeii. It was conventional to depict men with darker skin and women with lighter skin.

Most have seen the rather haunting Romano-Egyptian coffin portraits but this is next level. It’s a gold glass medallion incised with a family picture (C4thCE). It’s astonishing. They’re clearly portraits judging by the detail of each face. Remarkable

Coin!

Copper issue of Shahrwaraz, the Sasanian general & governor of Egypt after 617. Based on the issues of Maurice, it shows the “emperor” surrounded by the Sasanian Sun & Crescent emblem. Mint mark ΑΛΕΞ on the reverse. 

Sasanians ruled in Egypt at least until 628, but probably as late as 630.

I love ancient depictions of towns and how they deal with scale and the urban layout.

Was struck by this relief at the Pergamon Museum of a “river town” said to be from Apollonia on the Rhyndakos (c.150-200 AD).

Twelve Days of Mortality History Christmas - Day Six🎄⚰️: The hinged lid of this tiny silver coffin is decorated with a host of memento mori symbols. A winged hourglass, a pick & spade, a grinning skull and crossed bones. 18th cent (sold at Bonhams in 2004) 🗃️ #c18th

#FrescoFriday part 2: Apollo and lyre on a kylix found at Delphi. It dates from c 480BC and is infeasibly beautiful. 

Like Spacemen 3, Apollo is clearly going for the sit down anti-performance. Steve Marriott or Jimi Hendrix he is not 
📷Delphi Arch Museum 
🏺 AncientBlueSky
The Cave of Pan at Banias, north of the Golan Heights. Archaeologists discovered an ancient shrine dedicated to Pan and, in 2015, an extraordinary bronze mask depicting the god.

In ancient Rome, roads were dotted with white stones (cats' eyes) which reflected the moonlight, acting as street lights to help people walk on the street after dark. Pictured is a Roman road in Pompeii.

I felt rather unmotivated to post geology recently, but bsky is bringing my motivation back :)

This is the east coast of the Calamita on Elba, Italy. The sea breeze might make it appear chilly, but these rocks are actually migmatites intruded by leucogranites - estimated T > 700 °C *yikes*

Southampton Castle’s thirteenth century outer bailey wall. Back then, there would have been an earth bank and a ditch in front of it.

The royal castle would have been situated on a motte behind this wall. The tower block, seen here, stands on the motte, marking the site of the castle.

Sometimes it's the simplest little pots that inspire people the most: This tiny Roman BB2 Black Burnished cooking pot was found in a child's grave at Godmanchester. Was it a toy or could it have contained a last meal, lovingly prepared for the afterlife? #archaeology ⚱️

Agrigento today is a beautiful place - a hill town with gelateria, baroque churches & its famous temples, but it was also home to the tyrant Phalaris who roasted victims alive in a bronze bull. It’s said the contraption was made to amplify the screams of his victims
📷me 🏺 AncientBlueSky

A paper in Nature reports on artefacts from an archaeological site in Ukraine estimated to be around 1.4 million years old, which represent the earliest securely dated evidence for hominin presence in Europe. go.nature.com/4388Uk3 🧪🏺

This is a bronze gladiator helmet dating from the C1st CE. I think it’s a murmillo’s helmet rather than a Thracian, but shout if I’m wrong! The bronze is tarnished now but it must have been a sight to see, polished and gleaming in the Italian sun 2,000 years ago. 

🏛️📷BM
🏺 AncientBlueSky

The Parco archeologico del Colosseo has announced some stunning new mosaics found in a late 2ndC BCE domus ("of the Vicus") near the Horrea Agrippiana. Check out the stunning Egyptian blue tiles in particular! Italian Press release: colosseo.it/en/press_kit...

The Cascajal Block is an Olmec artifact from roughly 900 BCE that could represent the oldest known piece of Mesoaemerican writing.

Some glyphs are clearly pineapples, bugs, torches, etc. but otherwise this hasn't been deciphered yet

This is the oldest statue in London. Carved in the 2nd century, this effigy of Minerva was rediscovered in the 19th century when the head of King Alfred was added of Coade stone, and  it was installed in the garden at Trinity Church Sq in 1826.

Ancient DNA is amazing, Part 4,982. DNA of 400-yr-old cow bones in Hispaniola and Mexico hints that the first cowboys in the Americas were enslaved Africans. Analysis backs up historians' belief that classic cowboy stuff like herding from horseback and using lassos were created by Africans, too.

A most unexpected sight in the baptistry in Cremona, Italy. The crocodile is suspended from the roof as an art installation. It gives the impression of being sucked into a portal to another world (in fact, the dome of the baptistry).

Wood is rarely preserved in archaeological contexts. A Late Bronze Age grave from Behringersdorf in southern Germany is an exception. It has parts of a wooden scabbard and of a quiver with bronze arrows. NHM Nürnberg #archaeology 🏺

Part of a colossal marble statue from the 1st century AD, probably of Jupiter, that once stood on Rome's Capitoline Hill. Today it's a commanding presence in the wonderful National Museum of Archaeology (MANN) in Napoli, where the modern god Maradona's flags fly in every street. 🏺#AncientBlueSky

The display of ancient sculpture in a disused power plant at Rome’s Centrale Montemartini is delightful, and the positioning of a bust of Antinous next to a piston still dripping with lubricating oil is absolute ~chef’s kiss~

Good news everybody! 

Under our scope, plastic samples should glow bright orange. This first sample, on limpets from a hydrothermal vent 1600m deep, does not appear to contain microplastics. 

This is the shells-attached sample. The other one is basically an orange field of nothing.

The Monastery of Vivarium with fish swimming in pools constructed by Cassiodorus

Bamberg, Staatsbibliothek Msc.Patr.61; Cassiodorus, Institutiones & various scientific texts; 4th quarter of the 8th century; Monte Cassino; f.29v

Chartres cathedral, constructed within a generation after a fire in 1194, is rightly famous for its stained glass windows. But after visiting today I think the exterior carvings deserve equal adulation - truly incredible sources for material culture around 1200. #medievalsky

The Genoese built a series of towers and walls to fortify their colony at Galata (across the Golden Horn from Constantinople) in the 14th and 15th century. In addition to Galata Tower, much more survives than is commonly known.

Witnessing the vibrant colours of freshly uncovered frescoes in Pompeii is a privilege and joy that never fades.
Seeing the latest discovery of a mythological scene—Phrixus seated on a ram while his sister Helle drowns—in the context of the room it decorated is no exception. 🏺

Most of the historic ceramics that I've copied have a surface design. 18th C. British agateware is different, made of two clays wedged together. During the trimming process the marbling is revealed. I love how this one turned out! Sherds were recovered in St. Augustine, Florida .
#maniarchaeology 🏺

The one everyone has been waiting for is tonight's Irish Archaeology Alphabet 🏺 

Tipperary! As well as having distinguished residents, Tipp does medieval heritage better than anywhere else in Ireland. Here's a few favourite sites; The Rock of Cashel, Athassel Priory, and our local St Patrick's Well

County Roscommon is next in the Irish Archaeology Alphabet 🏺 

The complex around Rathcroghan is one of Ireland’s most important archaeological landscapes, while Roscommon Castle may have been the prototype for the famous royal castles of Edward Ist. 

Pics: Rathra, Rathcroghan, Roscommon Castle
Archaeologists discover ancient sandals buried in a bat cave 6000 years ago. Some basketry from same site is even older, dating back 9,500 years to Mesolithic period. arstechnica.com?p=1972204

Fort Rock, a tuff ring about a mile in diameter, north of the town of the same name in central Oregon. Wave action in the pluvial lake here in the Pleistocene eroded it into the amphitheater shape we see today.⚒️

This simple yet beautiful jade-coloured glass bottle was found in a C2nd CE grave in St Albans. It’s a lovely little piece and incredible to think that it survived unbroken for the best part of 2,000 years in the earth. 

🏛️Verulanium Museum
📷 mine

County Tyrone is tonight's feature in the Irish Archaeology Alphabet 🏺 

Lots of very cool monuments here, a few favourites include the remarkable stone circles and alignments at Beaghmore, Tullaghoge Fort and the ogham stone at Aghascrebagh (who's placename means the 'Field of the Writing').

Two hazel wattle or hurdle structures, a Middle Bronze Age trackway at Annaghbeg, Co Longford, and a medieval fishweir at Boarland Rock, Fergus estuary, Co Clare; separated by thousands of years in time, and by thirty-four years of an archaeological career…

Nazca figure
This polychrome figure is in remarkable shape for its age - around 1,800 years old!
The ceramic pre-Columbian spouted ceremonial vessel comes from the Nazca civilization, which flourished on the southern coast of Peru between 200 BCE and 600 CE.

#Art #Peru #Nazca

This incredible bronze cuirass was found in Maresse, France. It was made as the Bronze Age was transitioning to the Iron Age & dates to the C9th/8thBCE. Along with several others, it was given as a votive offering. Give it a whopping wow, please 

🏛️📷 National Archeological Musem 🇫🇷
🏺 AncientBlueSky

🏛 The Erechtheion on the acropolis of Athens. 
🏛 Completed in 406 BC. 
🏛 Named after the mythical Athenian king Erechtheus. 
🏛 Built from marble from Mount Pentelicus. The marble contains traces of iron which has oxidised giving the marble a honey colour.
📷 IGmele_snaps
🏺 Ancientbluesky

An interesting find! It looks very much like it's the burial ground of the Premonstratensian abbey of Woodburn, a late 12th century foundation. Woodburn had links to the Anglo-Norman lord John de Courcy, who was himself the builder of the nearby Carrickfergus Castle.

medievalsky 🗃️

A murmuration of brick in Wickham, Hampshire of 1648. This has to be one of my favourite brick buildings. A glorious attempt at a polite frontage in a vernacular building is made all the more divine by its idiosyncrasies. Full of personality.

County Longford is next in the Irish Archaeology Alphabet 🏺 

A small (and often overlooked) county, but there are some really interesting sites. The Aughnacliffe Dolmen is perhaps my favourite portal tomb, the Cistercian ruins of Abbeylara Monastery, and the remarkable Corlea Trackway from 148 BC.

A famous example of Ukrainian palace and estate architecture, the Galagan Palace in Sokyryntsi is a striking monument dating back to the 18th and 19th centuries.

The palace was built between 1823 and 1829, commissioned by Pavlo Halahan and designed by Pavlo Dubrovsky. ⬇️

Following in the footsteps of Hadrian in Bythinium-Claudiopolis (Bolu, Turkey). As the birthplace of Antinous, this may have been where Hadrian first encountered the beautiful young Greek boy. Hadrian was to grant Bythinium numerous privileges and establish a local Antinous cult after his death.

Moustache, torc, er ... Mickey Mouse ears?

In 1996 a breathtaking discovery was announced from #Glauberg in Hesse, Germany: The sandstone stele of an #IronAge warrior (5th century BCE) - coming with some fascinating little details. Fascinating enough for a little #SundayArchaeology thread. 🏺😉

The Colosseum in Rome was also known as the Flavian Amphitheatre. Construction began between 70-72 AD during the reign of Vespasian.

🏺Ancientbluesky

The Kummerhy is an excavated round barrow in northern Germany. The standing stone is about 2 metres high, and has many cup marks.
Photo by our much missed friend and contributor Holger Rix, who left this world in 2017. 🏺 More /1

This is absolutely magnificent and unique. 

It’s an ancient British bronze helmet and is the only Celtic horned helmet ever found. It was found in the Thames by Waterloo so was likely a votive offering deposited in the river. Originally, it would have gleamed like gold

🏛️BM 📷me
🏺 AncientBlueSky

The Rotunda of Thessaloniki is is usually identified as the Mausoleum of Galerius, who made Thessaloniki an imperial residence around 300, though it has also been attributed to Constantine. It was converted into a church between the late 4th and the early 6th century.

Wild that the statue of the tetrarchs that Venice took from Constantinople in 1204 still has a 100 years before it’s been in its new home as long as its old. Probably a product of the way I learned European history (Romans, static noises, high Middle Ages - cc @@5265) but still!

#RuinoftheDay -The Mithraeum House in Augusta Emerita (Mérida, Spain), built around three collonaded courts with an interior garden and a central pool. The quality of the floor mosaics is very high, the most fascinating one being the cosmological mosaic that once covered the floor of the triclinium.

An iron dagger was found in King Tut's tomb but the Egyptian iron age was 250 years after his death.
Outside of Egypt there was a small amount of iron work but it was only used for small jewelry and ceremonial items. It was worth more than gold.
One theory is the dagger was made out of a meteor.

One of my favourite mosaics from the Vesuvian area because of its simplicity and the curiously modern, stylised look of it.
From the caldarium in the bath complex at Villa Pisanella, Boscoreale now on display in the newly reopened Antiquarium at Boscoreale.

🐴  Greek Geometric Statuette of a Horse (750–725 BCE), probably Arcadian. A small but powerful status symbol, this solid-cast horse may have served as a dedication in a Greek religious sanctuary.

The long tail helps to steady the figurine for standing.

This magnificent miniature masterpiece is an early C3rdBCE tetradrachm of Lysimachos of Thrace. It depicts Alexander with the divine horns of Ammon & on the reverse Athena holding a tiny winged Nike (victory). This side also has the logo “of King Lysimachos.”
🏛️📷Chicago Art Institute

Detail of the ‘dancing figures’ from the so-called Villa of Cicero at Pompeii. The fluidity of the poses, as though floating in the air, combined with the arresting diaphanous material of their dresses is simply stunning.

The Three Lions of Kinver. 

Fragments of salvaged medieval glass in the north windows of the Foley Chapel, St Peter’s Church, Kinver, South Staffordshire.

I love this. It’s a postcard of the temple at Abu Simbel from 1923. The photographer is William Goodyear. Don’t know about you but this positively reeks of a cross between Agatha Christie’s Death on the Nile and Karloff’s The Mummy. Also, that font…magnif. Art Deco-tastic!

One of the wonderful painted Romanesque capitals atop columns at the Church of Saint Austremoine d’Issoire, in France. The church dates back to the 12th century, and was restored in the 19th century arteviajero.com/articulos/sa...

The burnt out ruins of 13th century Holy Trinity Church ⛪️ stand high on the hill above the town of Buckfastleigh, #Devon. Destroyed by arson in 1992 — it’s an atmospheric place with many stories to tell.

The Hungarian Rocks, or stones, are a group of tall vertical cliffs located in the middle of a spruce forest in the Carpathians.

They are known for their unusual shape and are considered one of the least studied megalithic complexes in the Carpathians.⬇️

As @@144449 mentioned, the Australian National Museum will be returning this vase to Italy after it emerged it had been illegally sold. It features Herakles & the Nemean lion. It’s a good example of black figure Magna Graecian pottery.
📷me 🏺 AncientBlueSky

This morning we dredged at 3.4 km depth, and pulled up a bunch of basalt. I was lucky enough to score a piece with two prominent prototype xenoliths in it; chunks of earth mantle.
The Drususstein, on a beautiful blue day. The massive concrete core once clad in marble, set up to commemorate Drusus’ death by his troops in the  #Roman city of his founding, Mogantiacum (Mainz)

The Nile and the flood plaines of Egypt contained rich aquatic resources- it is no surprise that fish were frequently depicted in art already from prehistoric times. A swarm of Predynastic stone fish and fishing gear, KHM Vienna. #archaeology🏺

Marker of social status, calendar, spiritual paraphernalia? The Berlin gold hat, made from just ~500g of gold, 75cm high. Late Bronze Age. Museum für Vor- und Frühgeschichte Berlin. #Archaeology 🏺

A fantastic cross section of an Athenian Trireme, aprox 450BC. I'm always struck by how vibrant the paint must have looked against the turquoise of the Mediterranean sea. The classical world was clearly wonderfully colourful. #maritimehistory #history

Medieval upper class dining equipment. An aquamanile, a vessel in the form of an animal or human figures. It contained water for washing the hands over a basin. #archaeology 🏺

3,000 year-old Ancient Egyptian limestone relief of young woman wearing elaborate wig, crowned with large lotus flower. Her sheer pleated dress is painted blue & she holds a ‘menat” musical instrument used to propitiate the gods

This gorgeous scene might be mistaken as part of Livia’s garden fresco but this is a later example. This fresco comes from the House of the Golden Bracelet at Pompeii. The idea of garden decorations such as pillars, masks, and frescoes with frescoes is at play.

I was recently working with BNCR San Pantaleo 29, an absolute charmer of a MS, and one that features one of my favorite weird medieval toponyms: the _Golfo del Tim_ or "Gulf of Tim"—just east of Tripoli, Libya. Strong Holy Grail vibes here.

For MosaicMonday, some mixed media in the 12th century pavement mosaic of Otranto Cathedral. At the top of the tree in the south aisle, a mysterious Atlas figure holds a disk done in opus sectile. Maybe the earth? the sun? the heavens?
Medievalsky
Tucked away in a chapel in Old Saint Paul’s Church in Edinburgh sits a radiant and transcendent work by Alison Watt…

OnThisDay, the Castu Isidis, the first day of the Isia festival (according to Philocalus) dedicated to the goddess Isis. The Castu Isidis marked the search for the body of Osiris. Pictured, the Temple of Isis at Pompeii.

AncientBlueSky 🏺

Just noticed that the Countdown/Woolworths Roma tomatoes use the Leaning Tower of Pisa to form part of the M.

Pisa is more than 300km from Rome. And the tomatoes aren't specifically associated with either region.

A Hellenistic/Roman-period glass bowl, decorated in a series of colours & spirals. It is a beautiful piece of glass, despite some signs of weathering & age - even so, it looks pretty amazing for a vessel some 2100-2200 years old!

Rock-cut church with domed cross plan in Kilistra in Lycaonia Province (modern Gökyurt, Konya)

On February 17, 1600, philosopher, cosmologist, and unrepentant heretic Giordano Bruno was burned at the stake in the Campo de' Fiori in Rome. A notary sketched the execution as it happened. In 1889, a statue of Bruno by Ettore Ferrari was unveiled in the Campo, where it stands today #earlymodern
The tools that transformed the Neolithic world. Axes enabled people to durably clear land for agriculture. Stichbandkeramik, 5000-4400 BCE. Germanisches Nationalmuseum Nürnberg. #archaeology 🏺

A Roman tombstone dedicated to a soldier named Gaius Lovesius Cadarus, who died at the age of 25 after 8 years service with the Roman Army in the Legio XX Valeria Victrix. He was originally from Emerita in Spain, but died while garrisoned in Chester (Britain).

Whenever there's a nip in the air - I warm myself up by thinking of my visit (during the heatwave) to this remarkable yew at St. George, Crowhurst in Surrey. Reputed to be 4000 years old. It predates the medieval church and christianity itself.

for the mosque pic fans, here are my four best from the Rüstem Paşa mosque, which was made even more beautiful because it was hidden amid the most aggressive, multilingually yelling, and crowded market you can imagine. part of my spirit is still sitting here.

For the seventh day of Classicstober, a votive relief depicting Persephone purifying an initiate into the Eleusinian Mysteries with water. From Eleusis and dated to the late 5th century BCE. Now in the Archaeological Museum of Eleusis.

Graffiti in St. Mary's Church, Lydiard Tregoze, dates all the way back to the #15thC. In the #19thC, when the glaziers installed new windows, they couldn't resist.

Rock crystal is insanely difficult to carve, so seeing five pieces from Carthage (Tunisia, North Africa), ca. 200-500 CE, is a real treat.

Pliny believed that rock crystal came from solidified snow, and it was thought to protect from a variety of ailments.

Carved from wood, this 16th-century figure shows a corpse inside a coffin. The body is decaying and worms are spilling from the stomach. The coffin is a memento mori, an object that reminds the viewer of the shortness of human life & the inevitability of death.

'The quartzite sarcophagus in the burial chamber of Tutankhamun. The winged arms of goddess Isis and Nephthys are outstretched to envelop the basin in a protective embrace.'

A really beautiful Roman glass bowl in a striking emerald-green colour. It was made about 2000 years ago, but survives in such beautiful condition that it looks like something you could buy in a shop today! (📷: NY Met Museum)

Billowing robes, diaphanous cloth, sinuous rhythmic poses - these are two of the Nereids from the Xanthian tomb of the same name. They are from southern Turkey and pre-date Hellenisation (they’re early C4thBCE), but still show enormous Greek artistic influence. Mesmerising

🏛️BM 📷 me
🏺 AncientBlueSky

Some clay pipe bowl loveliness for you this morning. Getting harder to find complete clay pipes in the Thames, they *do* wash up but more likely you’ll find just the bowl or perhaps a partial stem with a makers’ name. This is a beauty; 19thC pipe bowl in acorn design with a tiny acorn at the heel.
Weaver’s Cottage, Rawtenstall Lancashire today. Windows were to bring in extra light for operation of the looms. Raised hierarchy of centre window common to this area - abstract of a Venetian window?

An 8th century spearhead turned into one of the most important regalia and relics of the Holy Roman Empire: the Holy Lance. Said to be the lance with which Longinus pierced the body of Christ, it lead the way into numerous battles as a token of victory. Imperial Treasury, Vienna.🏺

This mural of Julius Caesar is located outside the Roman Walls of Lugo. The city walls are the most complete example of Roman military architecture within the Western Roman Empire. The mural was created by the graffiti and urban artist Diego AS. It was awarded the best mural in the world in 2022 🏺

Portrait bust of the Roman emperor Trajan ~ c. 108 CE

Often lauded as the ‘optimus princeps’, his reputation as being the best since Augustus 👀 can be attributed in part to Pliny the Younger’s effusive Panegyricus! 

🏛 Kunsthistorisches Museum, Vienna
🏺ClassicsBluesky

Another mosaic from Italica. This time, a splendid parrot. It is part of a larger mosaic floor that portrays many types of birds. 

🏛️Seville Archaeological Museum
📷 Carole Raddato

A favourite place in the rain for #StandingStoneSunday: #Avebury with a tranquil-faced sarsen and a stand of beech trees in luxurious copper gold (don’t have many beech trees near where I live in Wales - love em)

Nuptials of Zeus and Hera in the House of the Tragic Poet, Pompeii.  

The House of the Tragic poet is so named because it contains several depictions of scenes from Homer including the taking of Briseus. 

🏛 Naples Archaeological Museum

Statue group of Persephone (as Isis) and Pluto (as Serapis), from the Sanctuary of the Egyptian Gods at Gortyna on the island of Crete. Dated to the mid-2nd century AD. 
Heraklion Archaeological Museum.

Incidentally, correct me if I'm wrong, but as far as I know, the "12th-century" "castle" that Trump is extremely proud of owning in Aberdeen is actually a *19th-century* *house*, incorporating portions of an 18th-century house, built on a 14th-century foundation.

This week on the blog! We're looking at what I term the 'omni-spear' - the extremely common design of iron-tipped one-handed spear with a mid-ridged 'leaf shaped' tip, used in Greece, Italy, Gaul, Spain, the Near East and Beyond.

acoup.blog/2023/11/10/c...

The Eleusis Amphora, c.660 BC. Almost 1.5 metres tall. Features the earliest representation of the blinding of Polyphemus by Odysseus. 

🏛 Archaeological Museum of Eleusis

🏺 Ancientbluesky

More than 4,000 years ago, a musician lifted their head in song as they rested their hands on their harp. Maybe their song was about myth, history, or folklore. Or maybe it was just a merry song for a Friday night.
Marble statuette, Greek, c. 2700-2300 BCE.
Getty Museum, item no. 85.AA.103

🐶🐰 Check out this cute sculptured corbel (corbel 33) featuring a dog and hare, outside the Norman church of St Mary and St David at Kilpeck, Herefordshire, England. The church was built in 1140 in the Romanesque style. 📷 Adam / Ipernity.

A medieval gold posy ring found in a  by a metal detectorist in a Pembrokeshire field has been declared treasure. The gold and enamel finger ring is decorated with crocus or tulip like flowers and inscribed ‘Honour for Thy Virtue’ inscribed in italics, followed by a capital letter B. #Medieval

Last June at camp in Algoma, I saw a peculiar Phoebe; pinkish-red in colour. It turns out it was the first record of diet-induced aberrant plumage (erythrism) in this species. The editor of ABA's Birding magazine invited me to write an article, which was just published. #birds #birding

What does a chickadee look for in a home? How about northern flicker, or a purple martin? The 1923 University of North Dakota bulletin "Houses for North Dakota Birds" instructed kids how to build bird houses customized to each species they might hope to attract. 🪶🗃️

Cassin’s Finch brings #BirdGlamour to conifer & quaking aspen forests in western North America! Similar to the House Finch but with a pointier crown, Cassin’s Finches are excellent mimics and add other species’ songs to theirs. They’re also a bit salty: they love salt and visit mineral deposits. 🐡🧪🐦

Flies, fabulous flies. Estimates vary, but there are about 1 million fly species in the world and only around 14% of them have been described. Here are few gems from my little patch. 🧪

Black Scavenger Fly (Sepsis sp.), Marsh Fly (Dicta sp.) and Tachinid Fly.

A Grey Catbird I saw at MacGregor Point PP in Ontario.

Catbirds like to skulk around in the bushes so it was a treat to see one in the relative open. They are related to thrashers and mockingbirds, so as well as their namesake mewing, they have a vast repertoire of elaborate vocalizations.

Down on the flood plain in search of a rare visitor to these lakes. A morning of mud-splatter and cracked ice; of snipe flashing off the wet meadows and grey partridges thundering from thickets. And then there it was, the sudden immensity of a white-tailed sea eagle watching the winter waters.

“The left side of its body was covered in shimmering spring-green feathers, the classic coloring for females. Its right side, however, was iridescent blue, the telltale marker of a male. The bird appeared to be a bilateral gynandromorph: female on one side and male on the other..."

(gift link)

Female Red-winged Blackbirds are acrobatic insect hunters! 

A few years ago I happened upon one dancing on pond muck - I was entranced. Now I go look for them and feel joy at seeing and hearing their little contact calls to fledglings on the shore. 🪶🌿
A white-tailed ptarmigan along a trail up La Plata Peak, CO. The smallest grouse, it stays at in the alpine/subalpine year round, mainly eating willows. It's been recorded nesting at over 13,000 ft 🪶

Two nēnēs at WWT Slimbridge! Native to Hawai'i, and the rarest goose in the world, at one point numbering only 30 birds due to hunting and introduced predators like mongoose, pigs, and cats. Decades of conservation efforts have helped reintroduce the nēnē and native numbers are over 3,800 today 😊🪶

American flamingos are one of the largest species of flamingo. Found in the Caribbean and along the northern coast of South America, these extraordinarily social birds live in flocks that sometimes number in the thousands. A pigment in their food gives their feathers their flamboyant pink color.

Western meadowlark in front of a great egret over a vernal pool! 

Earlier this year, we had record rain in Southern California. Our local park has  vernal pools with fairy shrimp! 

An abundant water year made it possible for the scene to happen as western meadowlark’s winter in on our bluff￼ 🪶

Prob the best studied climate-related range expansion of any European #birds is the Cetti’s Warbler. This skulking brown bird is the sole European representative of a largely Asian genus & is a resident species of reedbeds & wet scrub. 1/N 🌿🌏 🧪

Pics Nick Goodram CC BY 2.0 flickr.com/search/?user...

3rd plumage white-tailed eagle.

The white-tailed eagle (Haliaeetus albicilla), known for its distinctive white tail in adulthood, is one of the largest birds of prey in Eurasia, thriving near large bodies of water.

Photo taken in Lohja, Finland, February 2024.

A Torrent Tyrannulet (Serpophaga cinerea) delivering some snacks to the kids. These tiny flycatchers (about 10 cm or 4 in) live on fast moving rivers and streams and catch insects right at the edge of the water. I found this nesting pair in March of this year. #birds 🪶#costarica

A park ranger in Santee SP in SC once pointed out to me that the round nesting holes in the trees in the middle of Lake Marion are from Pileated Woodpeckers, but the large oval ones were made decades ago by Ivory Billed Woodpeckers, and this fact honestly haunts me - all that's left is a void. 🪶

I've seen 72 species in Texas. 68 of them were before breakfast today at Audubon's Mitchell Lake preserve. What a place! Black-bellied whistling duck, Couch's kingbird, Bewick's wren, loggerhead shrike, ladderback woodpecker, white-faced ibis & vesper sparrow were all lifers.🪶

The Velvet-purple Coronet is a fabulous hummingbird that looks velvety black in low light, but catch it at the right angle and their feathers shine purple! They are found in the cloud forests of Ecuador and Colombia.

kirtland’s warblers are the rarest breeding warbler in the united states. they’re also tail-bobbing chonks with no fear of humans and a peculiar love of fire. this one has a pink mandible which means it was born just a few weeks ago!

Swans in Europe, Asia, and the Americas are white. For centuries in Europe, there were idioms like "might as well look for a black swan" which was assumed to be impossible.

But all along, Australia was full of black swans 🪶 They're pretty easy to find here.
Minnippi Park, Brisbane
#ausbirds

This spring, when doing partial garden tidying, I left the dead swamp milkweed stems tall because birds liked to perch there. I'm so glad I did; I discovered that female Baltimore orioles carefully work the fibers out of them, creating beautiful long string for weaving their nests. Not only that, +

A Blue-winged Mountain Tanager enjoys some fresh fruit. These birds are common and widespread in the Andean cloud forest. I believe the plant is a Clusia sp. Birds provide an important service to the forest by spreading fruit seeds. 
🧪🌿🪶
Bellavista Cloud Forest Reserve, Ecuador

Just for fun, I wrote a piece for Mental Floss on 10 facts you should know about Ivory-billed Woodpeckers! The most surprising, to me, is that there was a Cuban population that definitely persisted until the late 1980s (but, uh, it's probably extinct now too). 🪶🧪🌎

Here’s a young swan in London’s St. James Park. It was not fully white yet because it’s one of this past season’s cygnets. It was very calm and quiet, whereas most of the other nearby waterfowl were very animated and loud. I guess you could say that there was a low cygnet to noise ratio.

Snow petrels live in the most extreme environments. They breed amongst boulders high on Antarctic nunataks where summer temperatures may be -15 Celsius. To find food they have to fly hundreds of kilometres back out to the edge of the sea ice 

@@317 

🌍 🧪🪶🦑

I've just learnt that in Dutch 'waxwing' is 'pestvogel,' 'plague bird', a name given to it in the Netherlands and in Flanders because in the past its arrival sometimes coincided with outbreaks of plague.
I find this little fact too fascinating not to share.

Tufted Titmouse checking out a tree cavity for possible nest site - a variety of cavity nesting birds rely on pre-existing sites that require grasses sticks, fluffy stuff furnishings, but not actual excavation - keep your dead trees if you can! 🪶🌿

cool bird thing: my friend is a guide in costa rica, and his friend found a gull - most gulls are very rare there. he sent me blurry photos, which i told him looked like lesser black-backed gull. today someone got better photos, and it was a lesser black backed, costa rica's second ever! 🦉🪶

See the hole behind this hawk's tongue? It's the glottis (opening to airway/lungs) & why it's so important you do *not* drip water into a bird's mouth or force them to drink. If you find an injured bird, please contact a rehabilitator right away, its very easy to do harm when meaning to help. 🪶

Learning the ropes as a young Red-tailed Hawk is challenging - annual survival to one year is about 36% - this youngster is likely looking at a gray squirrel (which is tricky prey) running up a tree & hiding - a bit baffled, the hawk circled the tree & then looked off into the distance 🪶🌿

First post. Popped up to Corbally Reservoir to see this lively little Bufflehead this morning.

A rare visitor from North America, this is the second one that I have seen in Ireland, following a sparkling drake at Quoile Pondage in December 2020. 

#Birding

Bird photography tip 🪶 📷

With modern digital sensors, you don't want to be shooting in direct sun. The shadows are too harsh and the highlights too bright.

Try overcast (Townsend's solitaire), shadow (fledgling Anna's hummingbird), bright fog (Trumpeter swans), or rain (Little wattlebird).

For #SciArtSeptember 14: Glowing

Many birds have UV-reflecting parts. In Red Grouse (Willow Ptarmigan), combs above the eye reflect much brighter in the UV spectrum in females and immatures than in adult males. Likely to do with carotenoids absorbing UV but read more at the reply: 🎨🐡🦉🧪

Near Yaquina Head Lighthouse on the Oregon Coast.

The little penguin-like, white-bellied cuties are the Common Murres. They lay their eggs on bare rock. Their eggs are unusually pear-shaped so they roll in a circle instead of rolling off a cliff. Brilliant! #birds

A rare treat morning: mid-week birding at RSPB St Aidan‘s near Leeds. Soundtrack provided by BH gulls, Cetti’s warbler, and several booming bitterns (which remained elusive). Also saw skylarks singing, kestrels mating, 2 handsome black-necked grebes and my first chiffchaff of the year. #birds

Today's dinosaur-reminiscent bird is the crested caracara, a pair of which I saw in western Mexico a couple days ago. They look like hawks but they're actually hefty falcons that mostly eat carrion. This one, perhaps perturbed by my arrival, is making its rattling call. #birds #birding #Mexico

Back with another raptor, this one spotted in Serengeti NP, Tanzania 🦅 I believe the ID on this one is a dark chanting goshawk (M. metabates). The chanting goshawk genus Melierax is named for their vocal behavior, particularly during breeding season 🧪 #SciArt

What do Northern Saw-whet Owls & Barbie have in common? PINK! Northern Saw-whet Owls are very nocturnal, making it hard to age them by flashlight. The trick? UV light! Newly molted feathers have fresh porphyrins, which glow bright pink under UV light. Year 1 owls are very pink! #BirdGlamour 🐡🧪

This immature Great Blue is growing up! Its dark crest is coming in nicely, and those long stringy feathers on its chest -- used both for protection from rough reeds and branches, and for intimidation of other herons -- are starting to appear. Lost Lagoon, Stanley Park, August 27 🪶

This is a male Olivaceous Piculet (Picumnus olivaceus). He's busy removing wood from the nesting cavity that he's building with his mate. These are very small woodpeckers, and their cavity entrance is only 2.5cm (1 in) wide.

Seen last night at my friend's house in Bijagua, Costa Rica.

Migratory swift roosts are one of my favorite natural spectacles! Here in Portland, hundreds of people of all ages & walks of life come out to watch 10K+ Vaux’s Swifts funneling into a chimney at dusk, cheering as the swifts evade a hungry hawk waiting for dinner. Incredible!

My fav gull! 90% of them nest on Isla Rasa in the Gulf of California but a small population began nesting on an island in a small lake and then on the roof of a McDonalds in Seaside, CA near Monterey. The island eroded & McD's burned down, but some friends of mine built a new island for them!
Northern Fulmars aren't normally a beach birds but windrows of copepods were making for an easy meal so large rafts of fulmars were following the banquet. Last June along Jan Mayen, north Atlantic 🪶🦑

Red-capped Cardinals can be found broadly throughout the Amazon river basin. They belong to a genus of tanagers, not cardinals, because apparently we like common names for birds to be confusing. 🪶 🧪
Cuyabeno Reserve, Ecuador 2022

more old pics, a red kite flying low over my parents' house around xmas time! a true UK conservation success story, they are a keystone species as they feed mainly on carrion, which helps prevent the spread of disease. they're also beautiful to look at!! #ukbirding 🪶

4 bald eagles (1 adult & 3 juvies, perhaps the triplets born last year on the lake) trying to hunt a cormorant or kleptoparasitize it, aka harassing it until it gives up fish that it caught - a strategy often used by eagles since they are big enough to bully other animals to steal food! #SciComm 🧪🦑🦈

🧪The Andean cock-of-the-rock, a clear example of sexual dimorphism! Males have a large crest and vibrant orange coloration, while females are more subdued in their brown plumage. Interestingly, even their iris color is sexually dimorphic, with only females exhibiting that striking blue 👀

The level of fluffiness of this anhinga resting on a tree is amazing. As their feather coat is not waterproof (like ducks), I saw most of them sunbathing with spread wings soaked in water.

But this one was already air-dried in the warm sun of Costa Rica 
🪶

New bowerbird paper! Male great bowerbirds build display courts that are linked to mating success, and we found that object availability 🧱 rather than male building ability 👷 explains variation in court quality rb.gy/vznmm7

🦅📉 Arizona's bald eagle population was reduced to three pairs in 1970! 😮‍💨

Due to elimination of DDT, habitat protection, & artificial reproduction efforts there were 69 breeding pairs, & a record 87 young bald eagles hatched during 2019. 📈

#ArtAdventCalendar Day 5: Bald Eagle, Lynx Lake, AZ 🪶🌿🐡

More good news! We've found a record number of the scarce Brown Hairstreak #butterfly (Thecla betulae) eggs at Rock Edge SSSI. Forty nine eggs around the site, including 2 triplets and 2 doubles. Only 3 eggs on Blackthorn, 36 on Cherry Plum and 10 on another Prunus species...
#UKWildlife #OX3

This handsome little scooter is a Cinnamon Flycatcher. They call the Andean cloud forest home and can be found from Venezuela south to Chile. Like most flycatchers you can see them sallying from a perch to catch flying insects. Montezuma Rainforest Lodge, Colombia 🪶🧪🌿

🪶🦉Rose-ringed parakeet, enjoying some winter sunshine in an Antwerp park, where they established a feral colony in the last decade. They seem to perform assembly flights above the city at dusk, before roosting together. 
(halsbandparkiet, Psittacula krameri)

Today's #101CuriousTales concerns the Woodpigeon, one of Europe's most numerous #birds. Such high numbers of a farmland bird makes these a major pest species. In the UK Woodpigeons are resident, but here & elsewhere in Europe huge numbers will soon be moving south.  1/N 🧪🌎🌿

Bird Glamour loves Halloween colored birds! The Baltimore Oriole loves ripe bright-colored fruit, especially oranges. They also eat many insects we consider pests, like tent caterpillars. Female orioles weave elaborate hanging sock-shaped nests and will recycle old nest materials. 🐡🧪 #BirdGlamour

Pale-billed Flowerpecker seen on the IIT Bombay campus, Mumbai, Maharashtra, India.

They're hard birds to get a good look at: Tiny (only 8 cm long), and they spend most of their time foraging for fruits and flowers (often from epiphytes and mistletoes) in the tree canopy. 

#birds

Blue Dashers - one of the most common dragonflies I see throughout the season - they perch on a favorite spot, get chased by another Blue Dasher, circle around to chase the one that took his place - the females have sporty stripes and are not as easily seen while they oviposit #Invertebrates 🌿

Swallow-tailed Cotinga in the Serra do Mar outside Rio de Janeiro last month - a #bird I hadn't expected to see as bad weather forced me to cancel a planned day of higher altitude birding, so a wonderful surprise when this one turned outside the lodge as I waited out rainstorms. 🪶 #BirdsOfBrazil

🧪Meet the Steller's sea eagle (Haliaeetus pelagicus), among the largest of all eagle species by both weight and wingspan! This individual is one half of a mated pair being cared for by Woodland Park Zoo as part of one of the U.S.’s few conservation breeding programs for this species 🦅

Ridgway’s rail! 🪶

This endangered species is easily heard in parts of the Bay Area, but very hard to see as they skulk in marsh grass. At Arrowhead Marsh, I’ve seen one just twice in 15 trips—fleetingly, at a distance. 

But yesterday, because of the very high tide, long, clear sightings of FOUR.

Frigatebirds have tiny little legs and feet. They can't land on water because they are unable to run for a takeoff roll like waterfowl. So what is a thirsty, dirty frigatebird to do? Crash and fly, my friends. Amazing to see this.
Floreana Island, Galapagos, 2018

Birding level reached: 🪶 Resplendent Quetzal 
This bird, a member of the trogon family, is one of the most extravagant natural users of metamaterials. Backlit, its feathers appear a dull gray, but when light reflects, they light up, traversing blue-green-golden depending on the angle. #wildlife

„Small farms with natural landscape features such as shade trees, hedgerows and tracts of intact forest provide a refuge for some tropical bird populations, according to an 18-year study in Costa Rica.“

Burrowing owls (Athene cunicularia) are found throughout the Americas wherever vegetation is low, and soil permits burrows.

They're prevalent in the mountain states of Colorado, Texas and New Mexico, especially in area where prairie dogs create convenient burrows.

(pic: Wendy Miller)

Bird irises come in a rainbow of colours – but what are the causes behind them?

Some ideas, and a call for more data gathering and research, over on the British Ornithologist’s Union blog.

#Birds
#Ornithology

bou.org.uk/blog-corbett...

When I see a hawk perching, it's usually very far away and 80 to 100 feet up.  This red-shouldered hawk plopped down on a branch that was less than 15 feet up and relatively close.  Huntley Meadows Park, Alexandria, Virginia, USA 30NOV2023. #birds

Pacific Golden Plover seen on the Kona Coast of Hawaii. 

Pacific Golden Plovers breed in the summer months in Siberia and Alaska, and migrate south to Asia, Australasia, and Pacific islands for much of the winter. The Hawaiian population is well adapted to humans and are common park birds.

#birds

A bit of direct comparison of two of the gnatcatcher species in coastal California

The blue-gray gnatcatcher, wider range throughout continental US, has extensive white on the outer tail feathers

CA gnatcatchers have limited range to coastal SoCal and Baja CA and show only narrow white edges

📷🪶

Kāruhiruhi fledglings on nest. Kāruhiru is the Māori name for the pied cormorant or shag. Photos taken at the Chelsea Heritage Park in Tāmaki-Makarau (Auckland). NZ has 12 of the 18 cormorant species, that exist globally. 
#addBirder
#birds

Honing photography skills picking off wary warblers certainly helps with flighty rainforest birds. This handsome friend is a Common Tody-Flycatcher and it never stopped moving! 
Montezume Rainforest Lodge, Colombia
🧪🌿🪶

A snazzy Golden-naped Tanager pausing for a photo. These birds came be found in the Andean cloud forest from Colombia to Bolivia. They are omnivorous and dine on fruit and insects. 🌿🧪🪶
Bellavista Cloud Forest Reserve, Ecuador

After a week of chasing Parakeets on my bike, I’ve finally located the East Oxford parakeet roost. The local flock of 35-40 birds all roost in conifers just off Church Cowley Road, opposite the cemetery. Now, is there a north Oxford roost too?

Few wildlife spectacles epitomise the brutality of the natural world quite like the sight of a black-headed gull, carrying a croissant the size of its head, being chased by eight herring gulls over Morrison’s car park.

🧪🐡A purple-banded sunbird (Cinnyris bifasciatus) photographed in Maasai Mara, Kenya. Sometimes called “Africa’s hummingbirds”, sunbirds fulfill the same niche or ecological role as their hummingbird counterparts. They are specialized nectarivores and important pollinators.

A pair of Trumpeter Swans nests (dangerously close) to a road ~10 minutes from my house; I usually drive by and check on the cygnets every couple days. These little fluffballs become the largest native waterfowl in North America in no-time flat - the adults are 6+ feet long and weigh over 25 lbs 🪶

Gang-gang Cockatoos! These endangered beauties are roughly the size of Galahs and limited to a small area of coastal South-Eastern Australia. The local flock visited our garden yesterday, and I counted *sixteen* of them!

I grew up in a pretty competitive birding environment, mostly friendly but occasionally a little catty

I just love the Champaign Illinois birding crew in comparison. Folks helping each other find and ID birds, crediting others by name in rare eBird reports, so pleasant

Surfbird, a shorebird finely adapted to foraging on rocky coastlines in the surf zone, photographed today on the edge of San Francisco Bay.  Love the tiny water droplets beaded up on its back.

#birds

On the Hunt - Northern Water Shrew 🦋 
The North American Water Shrew is our smallest diving mammal at only 14-16cm long. It is primarily nocturnal, hunting in the pitch black of rivers and ponds by using its long whiskers to feel and blowing air bubbles which it then inhales to smell for prey.

A great white egret wading thought an irrigated rice field at the estuary of the river Ebro, Catalunya. Despite invasive human intervention, the delta still teams with life and remains one of the main stopovers for flock of birds migrating from Europe to Africa.
📷 #photography #photocatalunya

Collared Incas are snazzy little hummingbirds. Most of the time when you see them, they look like they are wearing a black tuxedo. But if you catch them in just the right light, you can see some green to their feathers. 🧪🌿🪶
Tatama National Park, Colombia

At dusk, a Great White Egret is stretching its wings and about to take flight from a lagoon that is right next to San Francisco Bay. The water is aerated fresh water mixed with salt water from the Bay which enters when the tide is rising. A variety of birds often feed here.
#Birds 🪶
#Photography 📷

The resident Kingfisher at Rye Harbour Nature Reserve has been catching some rays of sunshine in the saltmarsh opposite the Discovery Centre. Amazing glimpses of metallic copper and cobalt blue.

sussexwildlifetrust.org.uk/news/kingfis...

📷 Barry Yates

Canada jays are quintessential northern birds - omnivorous, with thick fluffy plumage. They don't migrate, and breed in late winter (Feb/March) before arrival of spring migrants to their boreal forest home 🪶
This Broad-billed Motmot has evidence of its nest burrowing on its bill. It was busy digging until I rudely interrupted it this morning at Tapir Valley, Costa Rica.

Here’s some travel inspo for Bird of the Week: The Blackpoll Warbler has the longest migration of any North American warbler. Each fall, most Blackpolls migrate over 2,000 miles across without stopping, sometimes flying over 80 hours straight! Learn more at 
bit.ly/3ReK8Lb. 🪶

In May 2022 4 Glossy Ibis arrived on the Hampshire coast. They were mobile at first eventually preferring floods along the River Meon. In March, right image, they were still in winter plumage but by April they’d attained full summer. They moved on in summer but are back again now. #birds 🪶#ukbirding

First river trip of the year, in February of all months… took Kayak Horseman out from Lathrop Homes down to just past the Cortland bridge. Spotted a pile of trash that looked like a heron then saw this prehistoric, gigantic-mosquito-looking dinosaur-ass birb fly out from behind it.

I love birding as a way of connecting with Nature; cycling is also something that is very important as a way to wind down. Last year, I decided to combine them with an Oxfordshire 'Big Year' by bike - here's a write up of the 177 species that I saw:
oxonbirding.blogspot.com/2024/01/an-o...

It's about as far from the sea as you can get in the UK, and in the crowded south-east, but the diversity of birds around Oxford is just brilliant. Some of the highlights of the 167 species I recorded within 10km of home in 2023. No apologies for some distant record shots - it's birding!
An Anna's hummingbird (Calypte anna) showing off that stunning iridescent gorget, aka the lil patch of feathers on its neck. Photographed in Seattle, WA 🧪🐡

🧪Rough day for that fish 😅 this is the pied kingfisher (Ceryle rudis), a species of cerylid aka water kingfisher. Cerylid kingfishers are true to their name and are specialized fish eaters. Photographed in Lake Naivasha, Kenya. I didn’t even notice the fish in its mouth until I was editing later!

The eared grebe has a piercingly red eye — totally freaky. In the fall almost all of them fly to Mono Lake or Great Salt Lake and pig out on brine shrimp and alkali flies, losing most of their flight ability and doubling in weight. They migrate at night. #Birds

💙 GM BlueSky! 💙

By their 3rd year, male Blue Grosbeaks acquire their full adult blue plumage. Regardless of age, males in particular often raise their crown feathers slightly when stimulated, giving the appearance of a small crest. 

Often?? I've only caught this 'crown' once! 😁

📷 P. caerulea 🪶🌿

Another forest bird I was extremely fortunate to see on my Kaua'i trip just over a year ago was this Puaiohi, a very rare thrush endemic to the highest elevations of the island. In addition to its harsh raspy calls, Puaiohi sing a song that essentially consists of their name 🪶
Red-throated Ant-tanager is a striking bird. It growls from bushes as you walk by. This one is a male. The females are russet gold and have some red on the throat also. #birding Belize

🔊👂 Southern ground hornbills are quite vocal! These social birds can inflate their brightly-colored throat pouches to let out loud, booming calls. This is done to announce location or attract a mate.

📷: Zookeeper Taylor S

Remember when we were talking about the difference between the red spots & beak lengths on downy and hairy woodpeckers? 🪶
1 - downy (single wide red patch) 
2- hairy (two red spots) 
3- downy (cute tiny beak) 
4- hairy (tree-smashing jackhammer beak)
Golden-crowned Sparrow, once called a Miner Bird in California because its call was said to commiserate "No gold here!" #birding Whitaker Ponds, Portland, Oregon

Thinking about how cute bowerbird courtship behavior is. Males build their little structures because the ladies like them, young males stop by when the adult isn't around to practice, and they like to collect pretty things. Amazing. 

Pic is a Great Bowerbird bower in Far North Queensland, 2019 🌿🪶

A Little Blue Heron and some White Ibises doing some afternoon fishing.

Ibis Fact: White Ibis nests can have nests from ground to as high up as 50ft 

L.B. Heron Fact: These Herons have white feathers that transition to its blue color over the first year of their lives/

#FloridaWildlife #Birds

Fall migration had started here in Minnesota, bringing with it nighthawks in need of help. Living most of their lives on the wing, nighthawks even drink while flying, swooping down over water to grab a sip. It makes them very difficult patients since they don't readily eat or drink without aid.

Hello Bluesky. Here's the 'Type Specimen' art for my favourite bird, the Yellow-throated Scrubwren, from my soon to be published 'Little Brown Birds of the Sydney Region'. This bird has a fascinating, feeding and breeding ecology with many exciting mutualisms and commensalisms. #birds #birding

We got super lucky while in Ecuador in finding the elusive White-faced Nunbird. These birds are typically very hard to find in the dense foliage of the cloud forest, even when you can hear them singing! 1/2 🧪🌿🪶 
Bellavista Cloud Forest Reserve, Ecuador
My first Scottish Myrtle Warbler in Ayrshire yesterday - showed extremely well and very active, calling frequently and chasing other birds off #ukbirding

This is the Superb Lyrebird and of all the bird species we get, this is the one that brings me the most joy. I see (and hear) them regularly but rarely get a picture, they’re so shy. Today I got out of my car and there they were, in the bush a few metres away. It is a good day. 🪶
#ausbirds #WildOz

The Apolinar's Wren is a small wren endemic to the Andes of central Colombia. Small and chunky, overall pale brown bird w some barring. Locally known as "Chirriador"(“creaker" in English) due to its song, which sounds like a creaky wagon wheel. birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/...

The fish hatchery in Blue Lake, California is covered by a mesh of ropes to prevent Ospreys from filching all the trout. Purple Martins return every spring to nest in the pipes that hold up these ropes.
#birds
Nelson's sparrow perched in a valerian on its territory, where we'd regularly hear it giving its hissing "distant gunfire" song 🪶
Some of the birds I saw at the National Aviary this afternoon. The orange and blue one is the Guam kingfisher, sadly extinct in the wild. Only about 100 of them survive in captivity.

Eastern Bluebird, perched on a post at McMaster Forest, Hamilton, Ontario #HamOnt

Bluebirds love spots to perch like this so they can look down to hunt insects on the ground that they can swoop down and catch -- almost like being tiny hawks.

#birds #EasternBluebird

For #WorldShorebirdsDay I wanted to share some Sanderlings! These birds live on coastlines all around the world and are an icon of tidal shorelines. They also migrate incredible distances: some birds fly from the southern tip of South America up to northern Canada and back each year! #addBirder

One of my favorite photos from Kaua'i was of this Apapane feeding in an ohia tree, the signature Hawaiian native forest tree and main food source for these honeycreepers. Apapane are endemic to Hawaii and are found on most of the larger islands 🪶📷

In Drogheda today I revisited my first birding “patch”. 12 yr old me balanced across these metal rails into a woodland to discover birds like Treecreeper, Spotted Fly, Woodcock and Snipe. Lots of fungi today plus Siskin, Buzzard & Sparrowhawk that I never ticked there in the 1980s.

Adventurers Fen about half an hour before sunset. Hundreds of fieldfares and redwings in the scrubby bushes, struggling in the strong winds. A marsh harrier making it look easy. Great shimmering clouds of lapwings. And starlings gathering as the light went. A favourite place. #birds🪶#ukbirding

A northern scorpion, Paruroctonus boreus, the northern-most scorpion species in the world. I found this one, who was quite chunky and so I assume was a pregnant female, on a rainy day in the Pryor Mountains of south central Montana. #Arachtober

A sea of shorebirds! I really hope I get to see this phenomenon again. Thousands gather along the Delaware Bay as they migrate north, eating horseshoe crab eggs to fuel their journey. Just a stupendous sight 

#birds 🦑 🌿

Woodland caribou on the barren lands. These two bulls were spotted in the Churchill Wildlife Management Area where they spend the summer taking advantage of the open area and wind to avoid biting insects.

Sandhill cranes in a corn stubble field, Long Point, ON, in March. The increase in numbers of these birds in the last twenty years in Ontario has been amazing. 
 #addbirder

Another year gone, and our thoughts turn to which were the best jackdaws of 2023. The third best jackdaws were the ones at Harlow Carr. The second best were the ones near our house. The best were the jackdaws at Beamish (pictured). Well done to them, and commiserations to all the other jackdaws.

If you're fortunate enough to see large flocks of migratory snow geese, you quickly appreciate the different colour morphs: white (with black wing tips) and blue (dark body and white head). You might also see some young blues that are all dark grey 🪶

These pink Black-headed Gulls are seen every spring at less than 1 in a 1000 frequency. This one is an extreme example. It is male and if you look at all the pics, you'll see the pinkness extends to the undertail coverts.

Photos © Barry Yates

#BirdGlamour celebrates the Spot-breasted Oriole! Originally from Mexico and South America, the Spot-breasted Oriole was introduced to Miami in the 1948 as escapees from the pet trade and are now are found in suburban habitats. Both males and females are brightly colored and sing. 🧪🐡🐦

NEWS 

Federal prosecutors have charged two men with killing 3,600 birds, including bald and golden eagles, in a scheme where they trafficked body parts of the birds for a premium. 

Most of the bird killing was done on the Flathead Indian Reservation according to prosecutors.

The Kolea are back! After flying for 8000 miles or so down from the arctic, an entire flock (about 50 or so) arrived at Kealia Pond NWR last week. Pacific golden plovers are amazing....this one still showing some breeding plumage. :) 🦉📷🌿

Been a bit of a rough week but greatly improved by a trip to my local wetland near Leeds (St Aidan’s). Saw my first marsh tit for ages, 100 or so redpoll, and (eventually) the glossy ibis that has been there this week. Grateful to the marsh harrier who kindly flushed it for us. #birds

I was this morning assisting bird ringing at Ruissalo, Turku. Mainly great and blue tits (my fingers remember those), but also some not that common species, such as nutcracker, nuthatch and long-tailed tit. 🤩🪶

Buenos días 🌻

Hoy el verdecillo. En esta época, los machos se visten de amarillo intenso para ligar. Le veréis y oiréis siempre en las ramas más altas. 
He tenido que buscar un parque nuevo con árboles todavía bajitos para poder hacer la foto.
 
Serín verdecillo - European serin (Serinus serinus) 🪶

back with some badass birds 🧪🪶 (Sorry I've been away finishing up my PhD) 

Today: the Secretarybird. These long-legged birds stalk grasslands and savannahs searching for prey like snakes and lizards, swiftly stomping them to death when they find them.

📷: eBird;Marco Valentini, Brian Healy
Surely one of the most spectacular finches - this male Brambling one of several feeding on beech mast up at Blenheim Palace this morning. The orange tones match the dead beech leaves pretty well

Sharp-shinned Hawks, AKA "Sharpies", are Accipiters. Unlike Buteo hawks, i.e. Red-tails, Accipters don't soar then dive on their prey. They hunt small birds so they are fast & agile in order to ambush & pursue their prey through trees and shrubs. 🪶🧪

Size difference is a big cue in distinguishing hairy and downy woodpeckers, but they rarely pose side-by-side. Here's another tip! Check out the red on the back of the male's head. On a downy (L), it's one patch; on a hairy (R) it's two with black in between 🤓🪶

Polders Poelgeest NL light SW wind. Pink feet moving north this am another sign of spring + 1st black tailed godwit alighted on Veerpolder 3 white storks inc 2 bill clapping on pylon shelducks up to 15, 3 oystercatcher a snipe 14 lapwing south the female scaup again on Leede #birding#ornithology

While looking for squirrel pictures I was reminded of one of my favorite warbler photos of all time. Audubon's Yellow-rumped Warbler in a tangle of dead and weathered branches. I found this little guy at Lassen National Park. The Audubon's form is mostly found in mountainous western North America. 🪶

I do adore these little birds. Tufted Flycatcher (Mitrephanes phaeocercus) are small flycatchers that you find in the higher elevations of Costa Rica. They're fun to photograph because they'll use the same perch over and over. This was in the forest at Savegre Lodge in San Gerardo de Dota. 🪶

This great blue heron was wading in a pond that was barely puddle depth.  I thought to myself "there can't be any decent sized fish in such shallow water".  WRONG!  #Heron is smarter than me.  Huntley Meadows Park, Alexandria, Virginia, USA 5DEC23. #birds

11 days to the winter solstice, but there was a decent amount of breeding bird behaviour in the Lye Valley this morning: Stock Doves were defending a nest hole from Jackdaws, Mistle Thrushes are now in full song. Best of all, was a Water Rail, only the 4th record here @@341846

Researchers surveying Ghana’s Mole National Park have found three critically endangered vulture species nesting there

This is the first observation of nesting hooded vultures in the park and the first white-backed and white-headed vulture nests seen anywhere in the country:

A very lucky Rock Pipit escapes the formidable 2w female Pallid Harrier at Warham Greens, Norfolk. She always arrives at roost with a full, sometimes very full, crop. The most efficient hunter on the marsh. #birds

Pelagic cormorants yesterday morning.  Beautiful iridescence & a bit of shitposting. 😎🪶 By the time they leave San Diego to breed they will have gorgeous bright red facial skin and a white patch on their flanks that helps ID them especially in flight.

Should probably do some bird-posting here. 

Superb fairy-wrens, cute little golfball sized critters that pair up for life, but then just constantly get amongst it: more than two-thirds of chicks are not related to the male of the pair

Sandhill Crane migration is in full swing. Although they can often be found in open fields, huge numbers of them spend time resting in the wetlands and grasslands. You'd think that such large and loud birds would be conspicuous, but they're surprisingly good at silently hiding. 🪶

I was standing on the boardwalk in Mimico Waterfront Park, looking 4 winter ducks like buffleheads in the protected bay. Only mallards. Then I heard the loud clapping of pigeon wings as a flock took to the sky all at once. No dog, no running kids… oh! Death from above? Not a falcon. Red-tailed hawk.
Bonus: sometimes when you’re measuring baby Grasshopper Sparrows an actual grasshopper insect hops by so you can point out that the sparrows are also the length of some prairie grasshoppers

Ready for release! This little hummingbird patient has been with us for a few weeks, he came in after a suspected window strike, thin and unable to fly. From Minnesota, he will join others now in a long migration south, all the way to Mexico or Central America! 🪶

This snazzy bird is a Yellow-eyed Junco.

In Veracruz, Mexico, native people called the Yellow-eyed Junco the "caster of fire" or "lightning bird." They believed that their eyes gathered sunlight during the day and released it at night. - All About Birds
🪶

In 1877, the ornithologist Elliot Coues wrote a short poem to voice his disdain for house sparrows. One of the birds' greatest crimes was their "extraordinary salacity," which was "positively embarrassing for ladies" to witness. 🪶🗃️

Though I know it’s the most colorful, flashy birds that usually draw us into birding, I’ve come to be more interested in the subtleties that distinguish diverse but sometimes overlooked groups, like sparrows. Here, though soon to be renamed, a juvenile Harris’ Sparrow in Bixby, OK USA. 🪶

We were happy to find a pair of Powerful Woodpeckers in Ecuador, where they aren't very common. If you wonder what an Ivory-billed Woodpecker looked like in the wild, the Powerful Woodpecker is in the same genus and looks similar.
Female (L), Male (R) 
Bellavista Cloud Forest Reserve, Ecuador

We spotted this handsome Barred Fruiteater skulking around and at first thought it was a thrush. These birds are true to their name and only eat fruit. They can be found in the Andes Mountains from Venezuela south to Bolivia. Tatamà National Park, Colombia 🧪🌿🪶

We have been hosting gracklepalooza at our place as more than a hundred had gathered in preparation for their journey south. Most have moved on but we still have quite a few. This male common grackle was foraging in the trees  and really showing off the colours!

Scaly-breasted lorikeet 🪶 dangling by one foot from a tree hollow. This bird and its mate were inspecting this as a possible nesting site. The upside-down entrance was a drawback, but not necessarily a dealbreaker. #ausbirds
Brisbane QLD
Lark sparrow in the desert. I see them with the large flocks of cute little Brewer’s sparrows and abundant White-crowned sparrows that have the good sense to spend the winter in the desert.  🪶

One of my favorite local birding patches is a park with a mowed lawn, a few gigantic trees, and a shrub-lined creek

It's a great migrant trap, those shrubs get loaded with warblers

Today, though, there was also a gorgeous Barred Owl in the lawn trees, my first there! (photo taken from a distance)
This male Eurasian Kestrel dragged some fresh roadkill into a field. Soon joined by Mrs Kestrel who took over the feast. He doesn't look happy. 🪶

This female #pileated #woodpecker seems to be daydreaming while clinging to a thin branch.  She really seemed to enjoy the blueberry-like fruit on this tree.  My arborist-fu is weak, so if anyone can tell me what type of tree that is, I would be grateful. Burke, Virginia, USA 13NOV2023 #birds

The yearly goldfinchening has begun!
The bare feather patches on their necks are their currently full crops (a food pouch that allows birds to eat a lot quickly and slowly send it to their stomach to digest). They need to eat every 30-40 minutes all day at this age!

Hi 👋 Sharpie! After a bit of light rain must be a good time to do a little grooming. Keeping up that handsome appearance is hard work. Skinny legs and a dark hood and about the size of a Mourning dove - good tells for a Sharpie. 
Sharp-shinned hawk 
Daily visit #birds

Osprey build their stick nests high up and close to shallow water fish sources. It’s fun watching them hunt along a nearby river, as well as brood on their stick nest down the street atop a baseball field floodlight. #SciArt 🧪

The parents of last year’s colt are back. I don’t know where they wintered over, but the male is trying to get his winter mud off. Looks like a very different soil color from what we have here. Both birds in this mated pair are looking good.🪶

1/ The Slaty Flowerpiercer is endemic to Costa Rica, found in mountains above 1300m, and is one of 18 flowerpiercer species found only in Central and South America. #birds

Slaty Flowerpiercer 
(Diglossa plumbea)
November 25, 2024
Suenos Del Bosque, San José, CR
Over the river on Exminster Marshes; mostly trying to photograph flying Curlews - mixed results - distracted by fishing Little Egrets and a sizeable flock of Golden Plovers. #ukbirding

My favorite pictures of the year:

I was in this nearby park to photograph the butterflies on the lavender but kept my ear out for birds. This juvenile Black Redstart flew past and ended up on this tree branch.
(June 2023 in Zurich)
#birds

The flame bowerbird is one of the most brilliantly colored bowerbirds. The male is a medium-sized bird, up to 25 cm long, with flame orange and golden yellow plumage, elongated neck plumes, and a yellow-tipped black tail. It builds an "avenue-type" bower with two side walls of sticks.

🧪Check out this bald eagle migration in Harrison Mills, Canada 🦅 Each fall, bald eagles flock to the Harrison/Fraser Rivers, drawn by the return of spawning salmon. I expected a lot of eagles, but was not emotionally prepared to see them just casually walking around everywhere like pigeons in NYC 🙃

🪶🦉Almost as beautiful,  the little egret isn't really that much smaller than yesterday's great egret. Especially if you keep in mind the cattle egret, which is chicken-sized. Getting more common in Belgium every year. Lovely. (Kleine zilverreiger, Egretta garzetta)

Check out this really incredible example of xanthochroism in a warbler. This is a condition of excess yellow color deposition in the feathers, but in this case the naturally melanic patterns seem to be overriding the excess yellow, making for a gorgeous bird 🪶
macaulaylibrary.org/asset/163805...

Not much in the way of rocky shores around here so the Surfbirds have to settle for mudflats has they travel to.from their alpine nesting sites. Anchorage Coastal Wildlife Refuge #Alaska Now on some rocky shoreline somewhere from Alaska to Chile (perhaps the longest but narrowest winter range).🪶🦑

Golden Tanagers are native to the cloud forests of South America from Venezuela to Bolivia. They eat a combination of fruit and insects and tend to travel in mixed flocks. 
Bella Vista Cloud Forest Reserve, Ecuador 🧪🌿🪶

Today in badass birds, the Kori Bustard 🪶

Male KB are the heaviest birds capable of flying. The picture taken in Kenya shows their elaborate mating display where males puff up and strut about to attract females.

Ladder-backed Woodpeckers (Dryobates scalaris) are native to the SW US and MX. They can be inconspicuous and quiet, requiring a bit of time and patience to find. Their small size and agility make them deft foragers.

Woodpecker Wednesday! 🪶🌿

Most woodpeckers are black and white. This is true in South America too, but the Crimson Mantled Woodpecker breaks that trend. They are considered common, but this summer was the first time I saw one. 🪶🌿🧪
Bellavista Cloud Forest Reserve, Ecuador
Two things little songbirds love: a place to feed and a completely out-of-control rambler rose world to hide in! Our resident blue tits love it!

Masked Trogons are common throughout the Andes cloud forests. These birds eat fruit and they also catch flying insects. This male was busy looking around for a meal.
Bellavista Cloud Forest Reserve, Ecuador 🪶🌿🧪
The Little Blue Heron is one of the birds that walks all along the shore of the water here. It's either fishing on the other side or observing on this side. Either way it's fun to see. 🪶

The mandarin duck (Aix galericulata) is considered one of the most beautiful birds in the world.
      It is a native of China and Japan.The first mandarins were imported to Britain in the mid-18th century, but it wasn't until the 1930s that escapes from wildfowl collections started breeding here.

This is Cassin's sparrow--a bird of the SW grasslands that, until yesterday, had never been recorded in the East Bay before. It was spotted at Albany Bulb in the am & several dozen happy birders went to see it. I LOVE how much communal joy a little brown bird (w/ a stunning voice) can generate. 🪶📷

Fledgling California condors at Marble Canyon in northern Arizona. 
722 (right) was photographed in December 2013, and 1150 (left) in March 2023.
Both hatched from eggs laid in the wild.
My favourite photography subject at the moment is the kuaka (bar-tailed godwit). It’s about this time of year that they set off on the longest migration in the world - from Aotearoa to Alaska. 🪶
🦉🪶 From afar, the lapwing looks like a black-and-white bird with a flying disorder, but up close, it is far more colorful.  Still unable to fly straight, tho. (kievit, Vanellus vanellus)

A MacGillivray’s warbler unexpectedly hopping around in the Bronx. A vagrant species usually found west of the Rockies, it was found by a 13 year old on their local Christmas bird count! #birds #birdsnyc 🪶

American Kestrel
