This beautiful black-throated green warbler, a rarity here, was found a few days ago at a park pretty near where I live in Los Angeles county, and I got to see it up close yesterday! 🪶📷

Catching you up on my 2023 adventures to date: I returned to Costa Rica after a COVID-prolonged absence. This year’s tour featured some of the best macro opportunities I’ve had to date (not always a given during the dry season). Red-eyed tree frogs are often a highlight for clients (shot in situ).
I love taking photos of birds because it slows you down. You need to look carefully, walk slowly, sit down sometimes and don’t move for a while. Then you see the world around you come to life. 🪶

Everyone stop scrolling and go buy THE SAINT OF BRIGHT DOORS by @@64002 RIGHT NOW

Weird fantasy, bleak horror, the absurdism of religion, race, caste, bureaucracy, and capitalism. The loneliness of parental abuse. All told with Vajra's sharp and beautiful prose. One of my top reads this year.

I need all my friends to read A NOBLEMAN'S GUIDE TO SEDUCING A SCOUNDREL by @@4103219 so they understand when I start screaming about how Luke's offer to change his handwriting is genuinely the sexiest thing a man has ever done in a book

Since I’ve been having some lovely conversations about American folk horror, today’s book recommendation is Maynard’s House by Herman Raucher (1980).  A Vietnam vet with PTSD “inherits” a house from a fallen soldier in a remote area of Maine and decides to hole up there for the winter

FIREBUGS by Nino Bulling is a fantastic existential-crisis-right-beneath-the-surface book; Bulling's cartooning is at the same time sparse and explosive, perfectly fitting a story whose silences feel like drowning more than anything else. Glad D&Q is giving this book a wider release.

Finished LeGuin's The Dispossessed. The story of a space communist visiting a capitalist planet and the psychic horror of experiencing capitalism for the first time. At times it's more manifesto than narrative, but what a manifesto. One of the most thought-provoking sci-fi novels I've ever read.

Well, just powered to the end of Fiona McFarlane’s *The Sun Walks Down*. One of the more exceptional novels I've read in a while. Beautiful writing and genuinely acute insight into characters … and also into the Australian desert landscapes … in a tense, missing child plot. Major recommendation.

Just read The Ballad of Perilous Graves by Alex Jennings. A fantasy masterpiece and instant classic. As a fellow New Orleanian, I'm amazed how Jennings evokes and captures our bizarre city's spirit. The book is up for a 2022 World Fantasy Award and rightfully so. A landmark novel.

Emily Carroll’s horror can be seen to draw upon a longstanding tradition in Canadian literature called “the garrison mentality” in order to create impossible situations of tension, anxiety, and of a dangerous world that really wants to kill you. 1/9

Sleep is denied me so here is a PSA. 

Delta Sly Honey by Lucius Shepard is one of the finest Vietnam War ghost stories ever written. 

archive.org/details/BBC_...

But if you want a book that’s out now, Tracy Sierra’s NIGHTWATCHING is a thriller so fucking scary you should read it with the lights on, but it’s also full of twisty ideas, and it threads an incredibly tough set of needles again and again. Even if you’re a chicken like me, it’s great.

i love books where the authorial self-insert isn't even thinly veiled, like in "the sorrows of satan" (once a massive best-seller, now all but forgotten) where Marie Corelli helpfully includes virtuous bestselling novelist "Mavis Clare."

I've read a lot of Matheson that I haven't liked, but the reputation I AM LEGEND has of being one of the best horror novels, and one of the best science fiction novels, and according to some one of the best novels of any kind, is completely deserved. A once-in-a-lifetime idea, perfectly executed.

Huge congratulations to Irish author Paul Lynch, who has been awarded the 2023 Booker Prize for his novel Prophet Song, a book set in a dystopic Ireland descending into authoritarian rule. Wonderful news! You can listen to Paul read from the winning novel at Kennys, here: youtu.be/nTNdiITpFqU?...

If you’ve never heard of His Master’s Voice, by Stanisław Lem, it’s probably one of my favourite works of science fiction, mostly because it’s just a carefully-written work of philosophy of science with absolute bangers on every page. I’d call it epistemic cosmic horror

I just read FORGET THIS EVER HAPPENED by Cassandra Rose Clarke and couldn't believe it wasn't on my radar before. '90s-set small town summer wlw romance w/a mesmerizing paranormal mystery with time & fate itself as stakes + monsters who aren't monsters

I loved ROAMING by @@373722 and Mariko Tamaki—a gorgeous balance between the smallness of intimacy and the overwhelm thereof, with an airy spontaneity that allows the book to truly come alive. An emotional and visual feast from one of the best creative duos out there.

If a gorgeously written, New Orleans-set mystery novel where the sleuth is a deeply fucked up lesbian ex-punk rock nun (who was probably also an addict/alcoholic) sounds right up your alley, I cannot recommend Scorched Grace highly enough. 👍 Wow. What a read.

Currently reading the late Michael Shea's MR. CANNYHARME, which went unpublished in his lifetime. But damn is it amazing, with exquisite writing, Lovecraftian menace, and a street-level tour of San Francisco's Mission District.

I am currently reading Anna Smith-Spark's A Sword of Bronze and Ashes. It is a mind-blowing book. I don't think I've ever read anything quite like it. it's a story about the ragged, tearing wound you get when high myth gets stitched to the grind of mundane life. It's incredible.
If you are a space person, you are going to want to read Samantha Harvey's ORBITAL, a remarkable evocation of life on the International Space Station. Stunning, beautiful, profound.

finished reading 'Saint Of Bright Doors' by Vajra Chandrasekera. 

You need to read this. One of the best books I've read this year. Chandrasekera created an incredibly messy and meaty world full of terror and religious wonder, with beautiful prose that doesn't let up for the entire book.

It's "Fantasy Novel Friday" on Shelved by Genre, discussing the most extremely fantasy novel entry in Earthsea so far with THE FARTHEST SHORE, the tale of a long-awaited monarchical restoration that didn't seem important until just now, plus an anti-drugs PSA. rangedtouch.com/2024/03/01/t...

I finished Jaime Hernandez's MAGGIE THE MECHANIC, and it wasn't at all what I expected but still a pleasure. It's honestly kind of shocking how little friction there is, in terms of Hernandez finding his footing; it's perfectly established, immensely assured but never "showy."

Wonderful Sunday reading: this Mexican novel, a fragmentary non-chronological Bildungsroman based on Yukio Mishima’s travels to Mexico and the US as well as his formation as a writer. One of the best Mexican books I’ve read recently. I hope it makes it to translation

Recommended read: The Blue, Beautiful World by Karen Lord. Stellar example of SF exploring the human condition with insight and compassion in a dramatic tale of ordinary people trying to cope when their small planet is caught between powerful alien races who may decide humanity’s fate is irrelevant.

A BULLET IS WAITING is much more a romantic melodrama thriller than anything hardboiled (heck, there’s a lil lamb!), but as someone who grew up in the Rockies & the southwest, I’ll always be a sucker for something set in this part of the country (Utah, here.) A colorful, comfy #Noirvember pick.

Starling House by Alix E. Harrow.

A gorgeous gothic novel with a haunted house at its center and metatextual footnotes. I love how this story explores how history is created and our understanding of the past, how the stories we tell become what's true.

From a literary version of Spielberg’s Duel to the novel Mary Wilson vetoed because of its “cockstands” and one of the most monstrous fathers in literature: I chose ten gems from the Booker shortlists across the decades:
If you like when True Detective does the “maybe something supernatural is going on or maybe it’s just this place’s vibe” I can’t recommend Tana French’s books enough

I should have read this decades ago. Extraordinarily daring for its time, in its depiction of sexuality, age and feminism, it's also a brilliantly-paced, multi-viewpoint story, which races the reader along. Marvellous. It's early days, but it's probably my book of the year.

I began my week with this wondrous book, a needed balm when the world hurts and I’m drowning in work. It is a masterful novella that packs a sophisticated and deeply affecting reflection on the historical layers of loss in a text of perfectly distilled economy. A most deserved Nobel.

Rereading JESUS’ SON again—because I always am—and marveling again at the incredible efficiency of this opening. A single paragraph of setup to establish the ground situation, then a “but” to break it. I’d spend three pages doing this work, and to lesser effect.
I’m incredibly happy to announce that THE SAINT OF BRIGHT DOORS has won the IAFA Crawford Award for a first fantasy novel! vajra.me/2024/03/04/t...

He Who Drowned The World by @@166678 is brilliant, staggering, and hurtful. It, even more than SWBTS, is an incredibly nuanced exploration of gender in an excellent political fantasy. It is brutal, but it has a light shining through. Just fantastic.

Reading Skin Thief by Suzan Palumbo.  Propagating Peonies might be the best story about the deep ambivalence at the heart of Buddhist practice I’ve ever read. This book is so ridiculously good. Deeply embodied stories about the spiritual homelessness of being pulled between 2 worlds

I was always going to read Rachel Nuwer's MDMA book 
'I feel Love', but it really has exceeded my expectations. It's fascinating and informative, not only on the history and current state of psychedelic science as a whole, but on the nature of trauma. Warmly recommended.
love reading Auden because you get sheer devastation like "ghosts must do again / what brings them pain," then the goofiest possible use of the word "sexy"

Black Mischief is one of those ‘secondary’ Waugh novels—like Put Out More Flags—that I’ve never felt the urge to reread. But here Will Lloyd makes the case for it as the novel where “Waugh discovers that he is serious.”

Currently reading an ARC of @@208442's A MASK OF FLIES, and let me tell you, this is thing is SOLID. Great pacing, dialogue, violence, remarkable sense of place. Perfectly poised between that sweet spot of crime and horror. SO GOOD. us.macmillan.com/books/978125...

DEAD SET is probably my least known novel. It’s about a young punk—Zoe—who discovers her dead dad’s soul on an LP in a strange record shop, but loses it, and must travel to the underworld to save him. Almost no one has heard of the book, but it means a lot to me.

Am reading The Little Rebel, by French author Jérôme Leroy and it is amazing. The story of tensions and terror in a French city, it’s immensely powerful and thought-provoking. It’s only 80 pages and we’ll worth a read.

If you’re ever worried the thing you’re writing is too “out there,” just remember that Philip K Dick’s semi-autobiographical novel VALIS about a guy named Horselover Fat who gets shot w/ a pink laser beam that contains the wisdom of the gnostic God has 28,824 ratings on Goodreads

I’ve spent the evening with Essex Hemphill’s 1992 poetry and prose collection, Ceremonies. Dealing with black masculinity and queerness, the AIDS epidemic, kink and sex, and essays on the problem of Robert Mapplethorpe and the joy of Paris is Burning. Highly recommend

The greatest living ghost story writer is surely Reggie Oliver, who not only writes well, but also in a variety of styles including pastiches. He’s quite remarkable. Much of his work can be purchased through Tartarus Press.

Having slept on it I'm confident saying that The Malazan Book of the Fallen is the best fantasy series I have ever read. Completely engrossed me from start to finish. I read ten books, 1200 pages a pop, in four months. I could not put them down. The easiest recommendation I've ever given. Read 'em!

JR Drunk On All Your Strange New Words by Eddie Robson. Very good, extremely well-plotted and well-written, with memorable and vivid characters. I have to say it reminded me a lot of... nothing I have ever read before because it was so startlingly imaginative and ingenious. Highly recommended.

Picked up Alexievich’s elegant, brutal, heart-breaking, and image-filled history, told as interwoven oral histories, of Soviet women who served in WWII, from laundresses to snipers, and did not put it down until I finished.

I’m shifting my 2023 reading thread from Twitter with #50, which is Room Temperature by Nicholson Baker. Baker is the apotheosis of James’s writer “on whom nothing is lost” and he turns what should be a curse of King Midas proportions (Remember and be forced to share every detail!) into a blessing.

PINK SLIME by Fernanda Trías (Heather Cleary transl.) is a sombre ecological catastrophe novel. The narrator lives in a coastal city infested with a toxic form of algae. The fish have died, the rich have moved away, and the protagonist suffers through isolation, depression and a family drama.

After finishing BRIDGE by @@179489, I screamed out loud and then lay motionless, hugging the book. What a breathtaking, masterful, relentless tale of grief and messy mother-daughter relationships and multiverse-hopping parasites. Please read it.

laurenbeukes.com/books/bridge

Today's book rec is Emil Ferris' MY FAVORITE THING IS MONSTERS. Her haunting work grapples with childhood, family trauma, the political crises of the 1960s, and the legacy of the Holocaust, drawn in a style that feels like a fresh amalgam of Robert Crumb, pulp horror, Lynda Barry, and George Grosz.

I haven't been so impressed with a collection of horror stories as i am with Adam Golaski's WORSE THAN MYSELF in a long, long time. Uses ambiguity like a straight razor. Several of these stories are some of the scariest stuff i've ever read. Big recommend.

My Agatha Christie odyssey continues. Having never read any of her works before a few months ago - yes, I know - I’ve just finished this, her fourth Poirot outing (I’m reading the Poirots in order). It’s wonderfully assured, full of humour and wit with a plot that is greater even than its parts.

"Her novels are not the opposite of popular fiction, but an elevation of it. Characters love and hate, are guilty and duplicitous, and have secret sex while trying to be good."

I wrote a guide on where to start with Iris Murdoch for The Booker Prizes website:

If you're looking for horror, some good reads off the top of my head:

Devolution by Max Brooks
Mexican Gothic by Silvia Morena-Garcia
White Horse by Erika T Wurth
Leech by Hiron Ennes
The Twisted Ones by T Kingfisher
Mary by Nat Cassidy

Excellent book. Posits the rise of ISIS not as a genuine upheaval of devout religiosity but rather as another strand of right-wing populism that attacks the intellectual class and offers a sense of action, however deeply evil, in a liberal world that seems paralyzed by inaction.

I read a lot of terrific books this year, but the one I just finished is the one I most want to recommend. It challenged and deepened my understanding of borders in a way that’s going to stay with me for a long time. 

The Naked Don’t Fear the Water, by Matthieu Aikins

Stephen King had a birthday!

Here are ten horror writers you should read to enjoy the power and variety of the genre he mainly works in:

Jeffrey Thomas
Kathe Koja
Gemma Files
Jeffrey Ford
Steve Rasnic Tem
Nicole Cushing
Paula Ashe
John Claude Smith 
Scott Nicolay
Leia C Von Hessen

Jonathan Strange & Mr. Norrell is one of my very favorite books--it's a remarkable feat of imagination.  Susanna Clarke created a suite of characters and an imagined parallel 19th century England that were compelling and the structure with extensive footnotes was very original 1/2

I've dreamed so often of an afterlife as a great alien sea and sky haunted by titanic storm and beast, and @@19459's THE FISHERMAN feels like an account of that un/familiar land. An uncanny, gorgeous novel that hums with a shared primal awe of all that never dies beyond human life.

HOW DO YOU SPELL UNFAIR by Carole Boston Weatherford & Frank Morrison is not only a great read-aloud but a master class in how subject can lead to the perfect structure for a NF picture book. Such a great mentor text for NF writers of all ages!

Just tore through Alix Harrow's Southern Gothic, STARLING HOUSE.❤️❤️❤️

"TL;DR—coal companies are poison, and lots of small-town folks would rather let chthonic, misshapen monsters eat their faces than get therapy. 10/10, no notes, will absolutely read again when I'm prepared to cry into a hanky."

HYSTERIA LOLLY & LADY VANITY by Ali Seay is a ferocious & powerful pair of tales. One, a darkly pleasurable read about true justice; the other a horrifyingly relatable struggle of unhealthy obsession. Emotion, gore, gratification, catharsis, horror- it's all here & it's GREAT. (Grindhouse Press)

I'll be honest where my literary expertise lies - it's in paranormal 'high strangeness'. With that in mind I recommend one of my favorite gateway drugs: John Keel's Strange Creatures From Time and Space, full of WILD firsthand stories, it's the best primer on the weirdest aspects of our reality

Currently reading Malcolm Lowry’s Under The Volcano, recommended by a friend as being outstanding in my go-to genre of “seedy drunk Englishmen going to pieces in foreign climes”. One of the most intense, brutally hallucinatory books I’ve encountered in ages. Hurts the head, fantastic stuff.

『고딕 성당, 거룩한 신비의 빛』을 읽었다. 근데 정말 새삼 내가 고딕 건축에 대해서 (관념적으로 말고 실질적으로) 아는 바가 별로 없었군.

암튼 종교사적 관점에서 고딕건축을 해석하는데 있어서 건축공부한(+유럽에서 유학한) 가톨릭 사제만큼 기대를 충족시켜주는 저자가 있을까 싶다. 특히 용어와 단어선택 같은 게 정말 거슬리는 거 없이 완벽하게 편안함…
dalcrose.craft.me/6SduZZ0eNwhpbj

I think I've decided on books for my course on disability and the nineteenth-century imagination: 
Allison Kafer, Feminist Queer Crip
Jane Austen, Persuasion
Walter Scott, The Black Dwarf
Maria Edgeworth, Ennui
Wilkie Collins, The Mooonstone
Charlotte Brontë, Jane Eyre

Finished @@64121‘s EVERY WOMAN KNOWS THIS while I was on vacation. Great collection of feminist horror, at turns gothic, surreal, visceral and funny. Hightower does body horror exceptionally well and can also thoroughly creep you out with the merest hint of a threat.

Comrades, I resisted reading this for many years because I mistakenly thought it was about a fashionista. Instead what I found was a feminist Jewish Canadian erotic espionage nouveau roman. Easily the first great book I’ve read in 2024

Just reread King's short story GREY MATTER. Scrappy rocket fuel. This time, it made me appreciate horror set during a heavy snow storm. How emptied out everything is, how lonely. How isolating and cold. Need more like it.

Flash fiction is a vital part of the SFF genre, but it's very rarely recognized in its awards. If you're interested in seeing a Flash Fiction category added to the Nebula Awards, please check out this open letter. SFWA membership is not a requirement to sign.

docs.google.com/document/d/1...

Today's book recommendation is Patrick Modiano's DORA BRUDER, a book about being haunted, and how an experience of haunting leads to obsession. It is about the doomed effort to salvage the pieces of a human life from the genocidal wreckage of history. Above all else, it is a book about loss.
Persuasion was published on this day in 1817. Making it 206 years of every other love letter not being good enough.

“Brooklyn Crime Novel” is Lethem’s best since Chronic City, an agglutinative interrogation of all three words in its title that has the maximalist verve and structural ingenuity of Dos Passos. It’ll have you rethinking what the novel can be. Preorder it!!!

I got a chance to read @@21959's new book, Wolf of Withervale, which I highly recommend reading :D It is a rich, well-crafted opening to a new saga. World-building is done incredibly well here, and the main character is an excellent queer icon that is easy to relate to, especially for me 😅

Maggie Nelson’s memoir, The Argonauts, encompassing her transitioning partner and meditations on queer motherhood is maybe one of the best things I’ve read. I’ll be teaching it next semester in the second half of my queer memoir course

First recommendation from my queer sci-fi reading list, Some Desperate Glory by Emily Tesh. A cracking read with aliens, military cults, parallel worlds, and an unlikeable lead who you completely understand and root for as she grows and changes.

Rereading the Cazalet Chronicles and am dazzled all over again by what a masterpiece they are. I’m always banging on about their brilliance, but structurally they’re astonishing. Completely convincing inhabitation of dozens and dozens of characters.

New blog post: on Emily Tesh's impressive first novel Some Desperate Glory, which pokes at some popular recent tropes in both YA and space opera, and barrels through a whole trilogy's worth of story in a single novel in an extremely satisfying way.

Saw someone else reading this and have started a reread. DOLLY by Susan Hill. It's traditional English ghost story meets harrowing horror. Deceptively picturesque, deviously quaint then the deep supernatural darkness knocks you off your feet!

Mantel’s books (particularly the Cromwell trilogy & “A Place of Greater Safety”), in addition to being gorgeously written, have deeply impacted how I think about history, & the stories we tell ourselves, both in the short & long terms. Also I got this on my arm permanently, so it has to be her.

Gillian Bradshaw’s THE BEACON AT ALEXANDRIA is finally (as of July 2023) available in ebook!! This is one of my very favorite comfort reads: a young noblewoman in Roman Ephesus escapes a repellant marriage by disguising herself as a eunuch & studying medicine in Alexandria.

I'm currently reading an early proof of The Silverblood Promise, the debut novel by James Logan (JFB, April 2024). Oh man, if you liked The Lies of Locke Lamora you're going to *love* this. 

The pyramid game is one of the most sadistic things I think I've ever read; worthy of Hellraiser, almost.

Un des livres, si ce n'est le plus important que j'ai lu pour comprendre Wakefield et le mouvement antivax actuel. C'est limpide. Deer est un journaliste fantastique et son livre explique avec méthode que la plupart du temps, tout ce qui compte c'est la reconnaissance et le pognon. Pas les patients.

Hallucinations by Oliver Sacks is the best book I have read on the subject. Informative. Empathetic. Scientific but an easy read. I have nothing but respect for this man’s work. Hallucinating is a fascinating part of the human experience.

Gatecrashing my Books of the Year list is this masterful piece from @@22511, an "anti-memoir" full of dense, intricate pieces about writing and life that  have the common aim of forcing you to look at the world in a different way. Complex, thought-provoking, top-quality stuff.

In December 1980, Vivian Stephens launched a new line of contemporary category romance at Dell: Candlelight Ecstasy. 

The line pushed the envelope when it came to sex & sensuality on page.

But how sexy are they? And how do the books hold up in 2023?
#romancebks #romancescholarship
bit.ly/46dtxMH

Okay, I think i'm gonna wrap this thread up with a favorite of mine. It's arguably not horror, but deeply horrifying, so

It's Ken Russell's The Devils. I've never seen a better treatise on power and the church. It's scathing, it's gorgeous, and it's got hot priest Oliver Reed 🥵

The Saint of Bright Doors by @@64002 is such a unique & great book about the son of a messiah he was raised to kill. I was sucked in by the complex & realistic post-colonial but still oppressive world, fleshed out characters finding their way in it, & really 🤯 use of the narrator’s POV. 🔥

it is incredibly hard to review a book that has :waves hands: just absolutely everything in it; every single page there's some brilliant line I wanted to point out. 

(I loved how Emma Straub, in her newsletter, described it as Kelly doing a backflip - or six - on every page)

Fox Wife by Yangsze Choo is unlike most things I read.
The writing is heavy in the best way.
Like I can put it in my pockets and stay rooted to the earth while my head is filled with snow.
If you've only read Gender Trouble--or found it too dense to be enjoyable--I highly recommend Judith Butler's more recent work on nonviolence and grievability

💙📚 2024-7

Winner of the 2023 Booker, deserves all the accolades. I'm not sure I've ever read a book that affected my physically like Paul Lynch's Profit Song. I had to put it down at times. I had to remember to breathe. The anxiety-inducing story of Ireland's fall into fascism is simply brilliant.

Just finished @@346220 's MAD SISTERS OF ESI and it's magnificent, a tale of tales wrapped in iridescent spacetime, an infinitely nested creation myth, a portal and a ship and a campfire woven of prose. Reminded me of Calvino, and Clarke's PIRANESI (but spacefaring).

This really is a roller coaster of a read. What at first appears to be a light-hearted tale of 1930s bohemian London goes on to encompass illness, destitution and tragic loss, but still finds room for comedy. A gem of a novel. My first by Barbara Comyns but not my last.
Please read Dark Harvest by Norman Partridge, which is a lot like if Ray Bradbury wrote a small-town supernatural slasher.

In one of my audiobook groups someone asked for “weirdest/grossest/most disturbing” horror novels. Some people are suggesting Dean Koontz and James Patterson. I mean, I guess experiences may vary. I just sent them a link to @@62103’s Splattepunk awards.

Just finished Lucifer by Dan Watters, Max Fiumara, Sebastian Fiumara et al. 

Couldn’t hit my sweet spot more if it tried. Gorgeous art, great story and definitely a strong compliment to the wider Sandman world.

If you see this, post a book you’ve read more than once.

Spare, bleak, and brutal, WE WHO ARE ABOUT TO… demolishes many of the village pieties of genre SF. It’s also very funny. 

It was originally published in GALAXY during the editorship of Jim Baen. Which is to say, people are complicated.

No Shelter But The Stars by Virginia Black, for a story that primarily takes place on a barren rock or in space, this novel is so viscerally lush with emotional depth! Even the most spartan passages hit so that you feel it with your whole chest. Just a hugely satisfying read.

Sapphic books | WLW

Found a wonderful architecture bookstore in Amsterdam, simply called Architectura & Natural Boeken. The urban planning books were completely different than what you’d seen in an American bookstore. It’s like we’re having a completely different conversation than the rest of the world.

MAEVE FLY is dark, perverse, and incredibly violent. It’s a new era of AMERICAN PSYCHO with a gut punch ending. There are secrets lurking in the basement, and they are drenched in blood. It all comes together with the perfect climax that you don’t see coming until it’s too late. I loved this book.🕸️📚

I've heard many books described as "something meets Twin Peaks." James Kennedy's Bride of the Tornado is the first book I've read that lives up to that description. I'm about halfway through and it's so weird and unsettling and a little funny and romantic, and I'm loving it.

The Saint of Bright Doors reads like a novel-length short story in the best way possible: full of fresh ideas, fast-moving, with an evident care applied to each and every sentence. Remarkable and highly-recommended.

Finished Solenoid by Mircea Cartarescu, tr Sean Cotter. A battle at times, but worth the effort I think. A richly imaginative stream/waterslide of consciousness slipping between dream states and reality for a nobody Romanian school teacher. Lots of great lines and tough paragraphs.

Finished Farthings by Charles Boyle – 113 short pieces (a para each) about life as a one man publishing house CBEditions. I enjoyed it enormously. Like Alan Bennett's diaries only with a sharper edge. Miraculously free of industry bitterness, but lots of wry insights about how publishing works.

I have finished reading the latest @@4966 book, What Feasts at Night, and can tell you that the author is one of the best writers of the experience of having a nightmare that I have ever read. Just, captures the fuckin' WEIRDNESS of bad dreams.

A++ 
10/10
No notes.

i read 3 james m cain novels last night bc i couldn't sleep and i tell ya the famousest ones (double indemnity, the postman always rings twice) are way less good than mildred pierce, which is Very Very Very Good Indeed

Announcing the winner of the 2023 Cercador Prize: @@241041’s translation of Ana Paula Maia's OF CATTLE AND MEN.

The Cercador committee says OF CATTLE AND MEN "lays bare the brutal labor of the slaughterhouse trade," hailing the novel as a "new breed of western."

Incredible little work, this - like Ford’s WELL-BUILT CITY by way of WE HAVE ALWAYS LIVED IN THE CASTLE. One of the most effectively moving finales I’ve ever read. Remarkably consonant with MOTHTOWN. What a time to be alive and reading books about death.

I’ve been reading Discworld for the first time this year and I cannot overstate how good it is. It’s funny, smart, and has just layers of “oh that’s a good point…” that makes you think and hopefully grow.

I wish I had read them sooner.

Absolutely blown away by C. J. Leede's MAEVE FLY, which sets itself higher and higher hurdles and then backflips over every one of them. It invites so many comparisons veteran authors would quake at, but it earns them. Mean, nasty, solipsistic, amazing.

The Case of Cem by Bulgarian writer Vera Mutafchieva deserves to be part of the 20th century canon of global literature. Now, thanks to International Booker winner Angela Rodel, Anglophone readers can discover this epic novel. These are finished copies. Let me know if you want an ARC.

Just finished Malcolm Lowry’s first novel, Ultramarine. A bit purple, and fascinating regarding how much a writer insists on getting out of the “will he cheat on his lady back home question” and whether or not he will get in a fight with the ship’s resident tough dude. Not a bad intro to Lowry.
if you like heists and crooks who aren’t very good at being crooks, i’m begging you to read donald e. westlake’s Dortmunder novels

Amal El-Mohtar is wonderfully generous to THE SAINT OF BRIGHT DOORS in the New York Times: “the best book I’ve read all year. Protean, singular, original … I can’t remember the last time a book made me so excited about its existence, its casual challenge to what a fantasy novel could be.”

Really enjoying Witches Abroad so far. Love how the witches play off of each other, and also love what the story does with fairy tales. Actually wonder if Shrek took any inspiration from it. Also very much feels like Pterry visited New Orleans once and was like. Ah yes. I know this place. Uh. GUMBO

Finished The Sarah Book by Scott McClanahan. An unpasteurised study of the feral grief of relationship & personal breakdown. Simultaneously one of the funniest & saddest books I have ever read. One of the best too. I'm used to book sadness, book poignancy, book emotions – but this is the real thing.

Today's book rec is Paul Beatty's THE SELLOUT, which possesses in great measure what all powerful satires possess: beneath its comedic madcap zaniness there is a scratching, a tearing; an overwhelming sense of the terror of history bubbling up from below; a profound and wholly justified cosmic rage.

Happy New Year! Starting Pedro Páramo by Juan Rulfo, translated from the Spanish by Douglas J. Weatherford. First book of the year. For newly formed novella book club. And because Sontag called it “one of the masterpieces of twentieth-century world literature.”

SPOOKY COMIC WEEK 
DAY 2: SEANCE TEA PARTY 

A charming book about grieving for lost time, and moving from childhood into teenage-hood. The main girls’ friendship is endearing & warm like a soft blanket. In true Reimena Yee fashion, the art is whimsical and breaks panel borders in engaging ways!

One of the books that folks brought up a lot when @@89192 asked about the most intimidating book on their TBR was Arno Schmidt’s Bottom’s Dream, miraculously translated by John E. Woods. There is good reason for the intimidation. Here is page 1 of 1,493!
finished reading "Crossings" by Ben Goldfarb today, I really enjoyed it!  absolutely fascinating science, and a gorgeous & compelling writing style.
the Tiffany Aching books (starting with The Wee Free Men) are also a good recommendation if you're more into YA stuff (they're also some of Pratchett's best work in general)

PROMISE is another SUPERB batch of strange, eerie, unearthly stories from @@4103588. Weird futures, fantastical technologies, and unusual histories populate these worlds where Reality shimmers like a mirage of a dream: it may seem similar to ours, but it's never quite the same.

I read an excellent novel (The Round House by Louise Erdrich) cover to cover in one very long sitting yesterday and am still gently buzzing with it. It’s the very best way to read a novel, when you can make the time. I wish I could do it more often.

This is a terrific novel. Doherty was from Scotland but worked as a miner near Rotherham - he's really important in that tradition of post-war working-class Yorkshire fiction that leads to Stan Barstow, John Braine, David Storey and the rest.

Semi-recently read Poe's PYM and Mat Johnson's PYM back to back and I highly recommend this, the experience is absolutely hilarious. I was going to write an essay about it but frankly, Johnson's book kind of incorporates that essay already. If I do write about this, it's gonna be about shoggoths tho

It’s time. APPARITIONS, my Deaf-centred prairie gothic horror novel, is now loosed upon the world. 

It’s the most personal book I’ve ever written, but as Martin Scorsese says, the most personal is the most creative. May it find its way into the hands of those who need it.🤟🏼

FRIENDS AND BOOKWYRMS, I NEED ASSISTANCE.

Give me your favorite high fantasy.
But do not give me the following:
Tolkien
Rothfuss
BrandoSando
GRR Martin
Robert Jordan

And do not give me scifi. I can tell the difference.
I need like 3 or 4 titles. Let's see if you pick what I've already got.

Reading Vaults of Vaarn and indie rpg companies keep proving why they deserve more attention than WotC. This book is a condensed, tight rpg with a fascinating setting and incredible tools that you could play in every week until you die all in less than 150 pages. 10/10.

reading Manhunt this evening by Gretchen Felker-Martin, & one thing that strikes me is how beautiful everything is. The way she describes the settings, the world, the fog, the mist, the burned out buildings. There are tender moments, & beautiful, & when combined w the violence & terror it's dizzying
BookTok, I'm watching all these "top books I read in 2023" and so many of you are putting out all or mostly white lists and I really need you to try harder to read diversely.
Finally reading Jeanette McCurdy's autobio. She is a fantastic writer. Crafting and curating these vignettes and they each have an excellently written arc...

I just finished N.K. Jemisin's The City We Became, and it's gorgeous.

THE HANDYMAN METHOD is one of the creepiest books I’ve read in a while. It’s a Nick Cutter novel so it’s also very squishy and gross. The run up to the ending is a series of mind-blowing revelations that expose the reader to dark pacts, familial secrets, and a finale full of consequence. 📚🕸️

I’m a sucker for meta-fiction, and Rich Chizmar’s CHASING THE BOOGEYMAN was a masterpiece in that regard. The beauty of the follow-up, BECOMING THE BOOGEYMAN is that I can’t 100% confirm this text message is fiction. (Can’t speak to the reality of @@3114’s text).

Book 1 of 2024 is done. Dexter & Sinister Detecting Agents by Keith W. Dickinson.

I finished Martha Wells' Witch King and loved it SO MUCH I feel bereft now that it's over. Fabulous writing, and some of the best found family I've read in ages! I adore everyone and wish I had another thousand chapters of them and their fascinating world. (And more plot, because I'm so hooked!)

Quick thanks to @@4101358 for recommending Ann Aguirre‘s books recently. I’ve very much enjoyed her Fix-it Witches series. Fantasy romance. Mostly light but still some neat world building and fun supporting characters of varied ages and a distinct dearth of toxic masculinity! ❤️

I love when a book does something so incredibly well that it sticks with you. It's been 5 weeks since I finished A Nobleman's Guide to Seducing a Scoundrel and I'm still going both WOW and OUCH about the depiction of how people who have been abused blame themselves. Reading it was cathartic.

If I had the power to compel 330 million Americans to read one and only one book, it would be The Autobiography of Malcolm X (1965).

No better way to spend a Sunday than reading a book from cover to cover, and few better books to read from cover to cover than this one. The humour, lightness of touch and spirited voice somehow both leaven and highlight the horrific poverty that she endured. Thanks for the recommendation, Bluesky!

I have just finished Bombshell by Sarah MacLean, and it is--to me-- the platonic ideal of a romance novel. Banter! Smexy! A thick thighed hero who's got big Roy Kent vibes! A heroine who's proficient in explosions! I giggled REGULARLY. 

10/5 stars, highest rec.
Finally read Mariko Tamaki and Yoshi Yoshitani's I Am Not Starfire this week and it was great. Never has it been so clear that a book's haters have judged it entirely by the cover.

I am finally reading the Murderbot books after almost everyone I have ever met telling me to do so. And, one book down, they DO NOT disappoint. It reminds me of various 2000Ad strips, in a very good way.

Book #15 of 2024: JESUS’ SON by Denis Johnson, a book I reread once a year. “All these weirdos, and me getting a little better every day right in the midst of them. I had never known, never even imagined for a heartbeat, that there might be a place for people like us.”

More than two months since the release of the Far Reaches collection and the individual stories are still rocking the bestseller charts on Amazon: Six for six at the top of the science fiction category, in fact. That's pretty cool.

Really enjoyed A Psalm for the Wild-Built it was the perfectly sized solar punk novella I needed to unwind. I’m reading a historical romance* set during the space race of the 1960s and it’s got me thinking:

Wouldn’t it be incredible if countries “raced” to achieve zero emissions?

finally read this essay and it's really good. But in mirroring my own memories of this time, a lot of the New Weird was heavily dude-coded. Indeed my favorite novel of that milieu The Etched City by KJ Bishop was never mentioned! (You should read it) textjournal.scholasticahq.com/article/1856...

Had my first five star read of the year! 

The Last Dragoners of Bowbazar by @@161278 is a gorgeous novella using the novella length perfectly, and full of lush, sexy, ethereal descriptive prose that had me rereading paragraphs just to savour them. So, so good.

This book… it breaks almost every 1983 category romance rule, and you can tell that Sandra Brown reveled in that freedom. If editor Carolyn Nichols wanted to make a statement with Loveswept 1, mission accomplished!

This morning I finished reading Catriona Ward's LOOKING GLASS SOUND, and it was an absolute wonder of a book.

Did doubt that any American man in the history of America has ever opened a carton of watercress sauce, but maybe that was just another meta-treat in a book filled with them.

Cancer Eats the Heart is very much an antichrist novel, but unlike one you've probably ever read before. It's also a cult novel, but doesn't function like a normal cult novel in the way the stories unfold. It has serial killers, too

but even they act in ways strange and broken

ok i finished @@64002’s the saint of bright doors and it’s one of those rare books that the best kind of recommendation i can give is… just that you must read it. for me to talk about how it made me feel would rob you of something special. an incredible read, and on so many levels.

Finally getting to dig into Corpsemouth, the latest collection of transcendent horror shorts from John Langan, one of the most original writers of our era. At launch, it got a fraction of the attention it deserved. What a wonderful book.

Delighted to be celebrating publication day for my second BFI Film Classic. It was a challenge and an honour to get this book on THE RED SHOES over the line in time for the film's 75th anniversary and the beautiful BFI Powell and Pressburger extravaganza that is taking place nationwide this autumn.

2024 is a year of cozy books (so far). Currently re-listening to the audiobook(s) for The Wayfarers series b/c you can't go wrong w/ Becky Chambers. Also physically reading the new Travis Baldree book.

What are your favorite cozy queer reads? (I prefer fantasy, but want to hear all answers!)

💙📚 🌈📚

So overjoyed (and startled!) that DREAMS BIGGER THAN HEARTBREAK is nominated for the Lodestar Award. (Not a Hugo, but given out at the Hugo Awards.) DREAMS is the middle book of the Unstoppable trilogy, where I really started to experiment more with breaking all the rules.

I find so much pleasure in reading Helene Hanff that I just don't want her books to end. I bought this unassuming little number from Kennys Bookshop in Galway, and it has spent some time in Limerick. It smells like a book nerd's idea of heaven, and is perfect in every way.

I finally read Legends & Lattes (in....about four hours? It was a fast read) and it was EXTREMELY cute and didn't trigger all my "this is twee fucking nonsense" issues many other "cozy queer fantasy" books have, highly recommended

“Danny Fingeroth has written the definitive book on Jack Ruby. No speculation, just deep research and hard facts which, because of the quirky subject, wind up as a super-entertaining read.”—GABE KAPLAN, who met Ruby and did one show at the Carousel Club.

The book is extraordinary, as everyone says. Beware, though. It's boobytrapped. Not this passage, however, which I’ve written out as a poem, as a psalm, which is what it is. The whole book is biblical. Fierce subjectivity burns itself into glass.

Visited the St George's English Bookstore in Berlin, which didn't have nearly enough @@133 books *but* I did pick up a nice copy of Anansi Boys and the people running it are deeply lovely so I'd recommend it to everyone.

Piranesi was really good but extremely short, if nothing else I appreciate that it understands what it means to "rescue" someone lost in that way. I also imagine the literary allusions play well on people really into the classics. For me it was mostly a clever little book.

There’s very little I like more than reading or listening to a new Stephen King book, but re-reading one you’ve only read once is high up on my list of pleasures. Back into Fairy Tale and I love it so much. Into the mystic as the arms get swole.

Last one today, I swear, as I finished listening to Cemetery Boys, by Aiden Thomas (narrated by Avi Roque) 💙📚👀🌈📚

I loved it. Trans LGBTQ Latinx protagonist! Magic. Spirits. Family. Friendship. Murder. Love. All rolled up in a surprisingly cozy read. A little slow paced but kept me invested AF 👍

Happy publication day to this amazing piece of work from Nathan Tavares! In the vein of writers like Sam Miller, Bendi Barrett and LP Kindred; I can tell that this book was crafted by a gay man. It's cuts to the core of our experience in the backdrop of a cyberpunk world none of us want. Loved it!!

Urban fantasy mixing poker, Bugsy Siegel, Tarot, and Arthuriana into a book I loved so much I spent my 20s trying to write something that made me feel the same way.

I will always love Martha Wells for writing an entire series of books with numerous, nested parenthetical asides (because it's exactly how I think), and making them compelling, amazing reads. A desperately needed service.

I just read 3 sequential parenthetical asides in a row and I'm literally 2% into this book. I love the way the murderbot is written.

Martha Wells is a gift. This is how I tell stories. This is the kind of story I love to see.

Out next week from Penguin! I was delighted to read this ahead of publication - it's a really exciting, fascinating and well-written book about travel, trade, mobility, connections, encounters, hostilities, & imagination in the late medieval world. A must-read!

Metaphysical Animals (Clare Mac Cumhaill and Rachael Wiseman) is very enjoyable so far. I enjoy the way the authors weave the philosophy and the anecdotes and the history together. I loved my time in Somerville College, and this book makes me think even more fondly back on it.

My third book of the year was Redshirts, by John Scalzi. It's a classic parody, but also critique of writers and the people who make money on them, an existential tome of philosophy, and an examination of interpersonal relationships.
Wonderful to hang out today with John Hendrickson, author of "Life on Delay," a milestone book about #stuttering. Great guy, too.

In 1997, Sarabande published LAST CALL, a poetry anthology that became a formative text on the lived experiences of addiction. Now, more than 25 years later, editors Kaveh Akbar and Paige Lewis offer this companion volume for a new generation.

I just finished reading the Devil Takes You Home. I enjoyed all of it (a lot) but @@18332 wraps the whole book tighter than a noose in the final 50 pages. Really brutal. Really satisfying. In the acknowledgments, Iglesias says he’s just getting started. That better be a promise.

I’m just over half way through this book, Zoe Baker’s Means and Ends: The Revolutionary Practice of Anarchism in Europe and the United States. It’s pretty good, not quite what I expected. Anyone else read it? Thoughts?
Black Cake by Charmaine Wilkerson has superb storytelling, beautifully woven together in a complex family history that crosses oceans and generations. It’s a warm and delicious embrace of a novel.

What is after capitalism

The paucity of our speculative fiction betrays that whatever it is, it is a future that capitalism itself cannot imagine. It is helping us to know that it is a system for children that we have grown past. Everything must die. It is preparing itself.

"Commit yourself to the noble struggle for equal rights.”

“I must confess that over the last few years I have been gravely disappointed with the white moderate.”

“I am much more socialistic in my economic theory than capitalistic…So today capitalism has out-lived its usefulness.”

A line from Terence Davies' OF TIME AND THE CITY I think about frequently, in reference to Queen Elizabeth's coronation: "The problem with being poor is, it takes up all of your time. The problem with being rich is that it takes up everybody else's."

As I age and I think of legacy, more and more I believe "foundations" forged by the ultra-wealthy are a kind of lingering scar on the collective unconscious of the world. Long after their founders have passed, they remain to enforce on the world some twisted, degraded echo of their original desires.

Marx affords the "economic" sphere of life priority because his ultimate concern is with human self-creation, and in class societies, self-creation is limited by how daily life is organized, which in capitalist society is in accordance with the law of value. Marx is not deterministic, capitalism is.

“We have deluded ourselves into believing the myth that capitalism grew & prospered out of the Protestant ethic of hard work & sacrifice. The fact is capitalism was built on the exploitation & suffering of black slaves & continues to thrive on the exploitation of the poor both black & white.”
-MLK

There’s a myth that capitalism is driven by competition, in reality mega corporations dominate market share with monopolies & oligopolies, forming strategic alliances, sharing market data and manipulating prices, with much of R&D publicly subsidized, while the profits are private

A corporation is an entity controlled by shareholders that exploits its assets to create maximum benefit for its collective owners.
A union is an entity controlled by its members to exploit their assets (time, intellect, and labor) to create maximum benefit for its collective members.

By my calculation, as mostly a jaded Gen Xer, the only leverage we regular folks have is our labor. That’s it.
So what are we doing with it? And what are we doing to ensure those without that leverage (who can’t work) also have the freedom to live and thrive?

I wish people truly felt the sheer obscene size of a billionaire's wealth.

Exhibit A)
One million seconds is about 11.5 days.
One billion seconds is about 31.5 YEARS.

Exhibit B)
If you get 100,000/yr &never spend a cent, you'd still have to work for 10,000yrs to become a billionaire.

No. Just no.

i'm absolutely not saying that you just gotta hand it to capitalism, but if you look at electric guitar design in the 1950s, it does look like an example of market competition spurring everyone to be better in a way that I feel like we get told always happens but don't actually always see
Nothing says late-stage capitalism like closing a grocery store in one of the poorest neighborhoods in the county and replacing it with a plasma donation clinic.

babe you’re not mad at “poptimism” you’re mad at music being a canary in the coalmine for 21st century market capitalism systematically turning everything that brings you pleasure into a bucket of live crabs

The utopian promise of socialism is that everyone’s labor will matter, and the hours of your life you give over to it will have a purpose besides “enhancing shareholder value” or meeting arbitrary quotas (tbh I think the collapse of the eastern bloc ultimately stems from their inability to do this)

its so fucked up how much 10 grand would change the average persons life, and how to a billionaire its literally a meaningless expense. if i earned ten thousand dollars a DAY every day, it would take me almost 68,500 YEARS!! to reach the estimated net worth of elon musk of 250 billion

One thing that is especially striking about Killers Of The Flower Moon is its depiction of capitalism as something that is so inherent to our world that people are able to excuse themselves for committing the most heinous, vicious acts, because that’s the natural order of things under capitalism.

of course capitalism "reflects human nature". so did feudalism and slavery. so did suzerainties, principalities, khanates, caliphates. so do liberal democracies. so do kibbutzes and co-ops, so do any bits of land run by the Dalai Lama, or by cartels, or by leftist revolutionary vanguards, or by ISIS
A reminder that the only thing you can do with a charter school that you can’t with a special character state school is make a profit.

Sure capitalism is bad. Granted. But how can we possibly dismantle it without compromising on the axiom that human rights are exclusively an extension of property right? Do we even want to live in that world?

Capitalism kills community. It makes tenderness transactional, creates distrust and distance in fragile animals who need one another - who need congregation without expectation, exhalation and acceptance, who need to find one another in the long dark night and sing to the sky until morning.

"The accounting ledgers of the Dutch East India Company lay bare ... the term 'racial capitalism' is in fact a tautology. Capitalism is always racializing. Racialization is always capitalizing. What is capitalism, if not a system sorting who is most fit for exploitation and extraction?"

Geez 😮‍💨

it's actually so interesting how in many ways the transition from feudalism to capitalism was a nonlinear process that resulted in some very inefficient "developments" like transferring textile work from urban guilds to the rural putting out system only to bring it back to cities under factories
I don't know who need to hear this, but if you believe that companies shouldn't just pursue profit at the expense of all else... Congratulations! You're against capitalism.
A hallmark of both horror movies and capitalism is people making reckless decisions that lead them down dark paths because they're forced to choose between their safety and their livelihoods.

Billionaires will exist until the rest of us need that wealth. Billionaires exist right now because society lets a small number of people funnel the massive productive forces of society into their private coffers, and then hoard it. When the rest of society needs what was hoarded, we will take it.

An amazingly good litmus test of people: When they hear and agree with that old 'no ethical consumption under capitalism' axiom, do they interpret this to mean...

A) So we all have to settle for doing our best
B) So we shouldn't act like unethical behavior matters

I would never trust Type B.

It's wild hearing the advert on TV that say's "if you started at the battle of Hastings in and received a million pounds a year, you still wouldn't be a billionaire yet."

Helps to bring it into perspective that the actual existence of billionaires is a failure of civilisation on an epic scale.

Capital "create[d] the housewife to service the male worker physically, emotionally, and sexually, to ... patch up his ego when it is crushed by the work and the social relations (which are relations of loneliness) that capital has reserved for him."

Silvia Federici, Revolution at Point Zero.

Thinking about how the line "If they would rather die, they had better do it and decrease the surplus population" was Dickens making a dig at Malthusianism all the way back in 1843, and yet somehow in 2023 people are still going on about how the world is overpopulated and it's poor people's fault

People are confused by how bizarre billionaires are, but it's pretty simple.

They are surrounded at all times by a double layer of the world's best sycophants, who are paid 7 figures so that billionaire never hears the word "no".  

And they just drift further and further off into the weird.

I feel like the misunderstanding  of the differences between commerce vs capitalism and personal property vs private property is such a problem. It's sustainable to own what you make and sell the products of your labor, what causes problems is not that, but a different system based on exploitation.

Good morning bluesky❤️
"Capitalism does not permit an even flow of economic resources. With this system, a small privileged few are rich beyond conscience, and almost all others are doomed to be poor at some level. That's the way the system works."

I don't think people truly grasp the difference between being a millionaire and being a billionaire, the three extra zeroes makes some people treat it like the difference between a thousand and a million and that's so minuscule in comparison

media is trying very hard to convince me that a cafe in Tokyo that employs paralyzed/terminally ill people to operate robot servers from home is inspiring

it's nice to keep busy when bed ridden but it JUST feels like capitalism saying "Soon we can justify denying cancer victims SSDI 👍"

After many decades trying to perfect home made rotisserie chicken my 2 cents.
1. It doesn't matter what spice mix you use.
2. Use more salt than you think you should
3. Forget about slow cooker, sous vide, perfect oven.
4. Use a chicken brick. Roemertopf. Clay pot.
5. Reread 4 and act accordingly.🍜🍽️

Made in the air fryer. Secret is: starchy potato cut thinner, & soaked in cold water, with a rinse. Dry them well before fat/cooking, & brush w more fat 3/4 through cooking them.

Local cheeses from Piedmont Italy.
Top: Robiola di Rocaverno, a soft ripened goat cheese. Less salty and creamier than, say, the Loire Valley goat cheeses that most American goat cheeses are modeled on.
Bottom: Mataronne, a mild, nutty cow's milk cheese.

Orange, green olive, red onion, a sardine, and some chili flakes. Less than €10 at a sit-down restaurant, and one of the best things I ate in Sicily. An object lesson in humbling all preconceptions before the majesty of fresh simple local ingredients.

Afgan mantu (pronounced mantwo) are usually made with lamb, but pumpkin is a main ingredient in Afghan cuisine and I wanted mine with a version of borani kaddo: heavily spiced chunks of roasted pumpin and onion. The dumplings and sauces (yogurt, turmeric tomato, herby green chile) are plant-based.

Pappardelle coccolare, or Coddle pasta in Hiberno English. You need a good few fatty rashers and sausages here. Make a normal carbonara sauce w two extra egg yolks for a thicker sauce and some sharp white Dubliner cheese grated in. give it a good mix. Parsley to garnish and white pepper to serve.

Duck bibimbap. Tamari shiitake mushrooms, mirin carrot ribbons, sesame oil spring squash, quick pickle gochujang pepper cucumbers, kimchi, scallions, duck fat-fried egg over kimchi mayo, sautéed duck covered with a tamari, mirin, sesame oil, gochujang, and kimchi sauce.

Two of my favourite Bangladeshi dishes which evoke memories of childhood - “aloo satni”, potatoes mashed with raw diced onion, fried chillies and garlic, mustard oil, fresh coriander and “aloo bhazi”, finely chipped potatoes stir fried with onion, garlic, chilli, fresh coriander
One of my favorite simple beef hacks: get a scotch fillet (rib eye sans bone), sous vide it to a perfect medium rare, and sear it off in the cast iron. Blowtorch optional

Made the Short Rib Onion Soup from Smitten Kitchen yesterday. You spend 2.5 hours braising bone-in short ribs and—in the process—make the world’s best double beef stock, and while that’s happening you caramelize a shitton of onions and season them with sherry. Last: mix and top with Gruyère toasts!
Lamb breast with sauce tranche of cilantro, rice wine vinegar, red pepper flake, and pomegranate molasses atop orange-scented rice with cumin-roasted carrots.
Local baguette (Tall Grass), local hot mustard (Elman’s), Brunswick sardines in spring water, local pickled red onions (St-Léon Gardens). Delicious! 🍜 🍽️

Picked up a shoulder of lamb from Otway Prime and cooked it. It’s the simplest possible thing — an onion, eight garlic cloves, white wine, stock and a tiny splash of pasatta — probably less than a tablespoon. That’s it. Really not a lot of liquid. Five hours at about 140. So good!!
Thin-sliced steak marinated (rice vinegar, soy sauce, crushed red pepper, five spice) and stir-fried with onions and greens, next to some crispy potatoes.

Two things people do wrong with French toast:

One, use thin bread. Yuck. Should be 3/4” thick, or 1”. 

Two, don’t soak it long enough. I’ll be giving my bread an hour to soak up all the egg. If you soak too short, the inside of the bread gets no egg or flavour. 🍜🍽️

lao ganma (“old godmother”) is a chinese brand of fried chili crisp condiments (deep fried chili flakes in oil). it is a potent flavor enhancer

white rice + spoonful of lao ganma + fried egg on top is a class chinese college dorm room meal

Beef on Weck. Roast beef on a kummelweck roll, a roll that is topped with kosher salt and caraway seeds. The meat on the sandwich is traditionally served rare, thin cut, with the top bun getting a dip in jus and spread with horseradish.

Pickled red onions. Huzzah. It’s a riff on what Bobby Flay always makes — except I’m cheap & I use white vinegar instead of red wine vinegar with grenadine. 

Flay never adds anything past RWV/grenadine but I added a bay leaf, pinch salt/MSG, & several pink peppercorns. Keeps for a few months.🍜🍽️

How to make a good red sauce:

1. Use the best canned whole tomatoes you can get and crush em with your pawbs
2. Also add in a can of crushed tomatoes for extra depth of flavor
3. Cook your tomato paste!!!
4. Lots of fresh garlic
5. Season throughout cooking not just at the end
6. Use a wooden spoon

PSA: a tagine does brilliant roasts, especially beef. Doesn't broil them like a slow cooker or dry them out like a roasting  dish. Tonight's bolar roast was melt in the mouth tender. Coated in pomegranate molasses, salted lime and fermented garlic.

Scachatta! The Tampa Bay area is mostly known for Cuban sandwiches, but less so for schchatta. It’s similar to something from Sicily called sfincione, but schchatta has a bunch of egg yolk in the focaccia-like crust, as well as ground meat in the tomato sauce. Eaten at room temperature. Good stuff.

Main course is a rare rump roast with a sweet potato and carrot mash seasoned with sage and white pepper. A creamy whiskeysauce, steamed broccolini and brusselssprouts sautéed in bacon fat along with crispy fried diced bacon.

A salmon cake made with saltines is a beautiful thing (like a crab cake but with salmon!), why on EARTH would you ketchup it when there’s an entire universe of more interesting and helpful condiments available to you

Chicken marinated in gochujang, ginger, garlic and sake in a corn starch crust with gochugaru and sesame. Stir-fried vegetables with a Gochujang-ginger-sauce, and a fried egg. 🍜🍽️

olive oil —> pork fat —> allium —> brassica —> pasta —> butter —> aged cheese —> pasta water 

this will get you where you need to go for most simple weeknight pastas

Pic from my mom: she has once again made salenjaci, the specialty of Slavonia, the region of Croatia we’re from. They’re like croissant turnovers, made of dough laminated with leaf lard—unrendered, spread pork fat. They don’t taste porky, but they have a unique creaminess.

Here is a nearly-ready bolognese. It simmered for another 10 minutes or so and then I let it cool and put it in the fridge. a thing I like to make: roasted carrots in a honey-mustard glaze. slice your carrots an inch thick, mix with garlic powder, some olive oil, few heaping tablespoons of grainy mustard and honey. salt and pepper, add dried parsley if you've got any. bake at 400 for 20 mins, toss, bake another 20 mins.

PROTIP: skinning, seeding, and blending up a Honeycrisp apple, then adding it to standard Japanese-style curry made from roux blocks along with a squeeze of honey and a glug of soy sauce, takes the whole affair from pretty good to GOD DAMN.

Tonight's dinner 🍜: Dutch Hachee with braised red cabbage and apple and mashed potatoes. Hachee is a beef and ale stew, but it's flavoured with a slice or two of peperkoek - a sweet Dutch breakfast cake with tons of spices and sugars. So I made that as well.
Two attempts of Szechuan poached egg. One slightly over and one slightly under cooked. In crispy chili oil, sesame oil, rice vinegar, Szechuan peppercorn and soy sauce. 🍜🍽️🥚

once again, i would like to promote the homemade Taco Bell potatoes: create a 50/50 flour and corn starch dredge, season with all your favorite southwest dried spices and salt to taste, toss diced potatoes to coat, toss in cooking oil, convection roast until crispy. no par-boiling needed.

Pulled pork on homemade tortilla made from Masienda yellow heirloom corn masa harina. I strongly encourage you to make your own tortilla. Might take a time or two to get the moisture right but dang that’s the only way to go. 🍽️🍜

Naan disclosure: I’m making North Indian food and tried out a baked naan recipe on a salt brick instead of my usual griddled naan. I also made dal makhani, butter chicken, coriander crusted potatoes, and cumin rice. The house smells divine.

A salad hot (correct) take: 

If you are making a salad with kale, cranberries, goat cheese, and candied walnuts, do not under any circumstances also make the dressing sweet. Goat cheese is not enough savory/tangy on its own to deal with all that. This salad is like eating the saddest candy bar :(

The best "bad" meal I know how to make is a bag of frozen ravioli, a big jar of Prego, and a heap of low-moisture Mozzarella. Lightly grease an oven safe something, mix the frozen ravioli and sauce, smother with cheese, and bake at ~350 until bubbly. Shake parmesan to taste. It's stellar at effort

Kinshi tamago, also known as thinly shredded egg omelette, is a delightful Japanese culinary art. It involves cooking a thin omelette, then slicing it into delicate, narrow strips. These silky strands add a beautiful touch to various dishes and an interesting textural contrast.

冷やし中華は卵料理🥚

This is my new favourite way to make potatoes - steam until soft, then partially crush in a baking tray and drizzle with olive oil and a pinch of salt, then roast for about 90 min. You can also pour over some vinegar before roasting for salt and vinegar potatoes which goes really well with fish 😋

My new favorite way to make potatoes. Get the small variety.

Boil them for 15 minutes (start preheating yoir air fryer after 10).

Drain them and let the water steam off, then toss them in some butter or oil and salt.

Air fry them on 400 for 10 minutes. Toss them halfway through.

Serve and enjoy.

the best caramelized onions you will ever make:

4-5 onions of various kinds, sliced 

5 tbsp salted butter

In a large saucepan, melt the butter over medium heat. Add the onions and mix. Turn heat to low. Cook for 4-5 hours, till onions are browned and sweet, with an almost jam-like texture.

Malagasy vocabulary:

Laoka: a side dish that’s served with rice.

A “normal” Malagasy meal is supposed to be rice with laoka. Many Malagasy people are willing to accept a meal with very little laoka if necessary, but a meal with not enough rice is simply Not a True Meal.

🇲🇬

Lazy porridge recipe!

- 125g salted butter
- 250g oat groats
- 1L water
- 0.75L milk
- 1 tsp salt

1. Fry the oats in butter, salt
2. Pour in water, cook in rice cooker on "brown rice" setting
3. Pour in milk, leave on "keep warm" setting
4. Eat when chewy & delicious (~6hrs)

Hot sauce taxonomy:

Yellow: Caribbean; mustard, vinegar, pepper

Chili-Garlic: Sriracha defines the type, but is its most mid member

Cholula-Tapatio: Classic hot sauce, more pepper than vinegar, often with some garlic

Tabasco et al: mostly vinegar

Beliezian: Like Cholula/Tapatio, but with carrot

If you are someone who likes umami flavor, can I recommend toasted soy sunflower seeds? 

Heat sunflower seeds in skillet until toasted. Turn heat off. Add dashes of soy sauce / tamari to hot pan. Shake rattle roll. 

Add to salads, pasta, wherever you want a salty crunchy kick. Keeps for a while.

This is how we do coleslaw in Greece! It’s called lahanosalata (cabbage salsa). Finely sliced cabbage and onion heavily salted for an hour to soften, then rinsed and dressed with olive oil and either lemon or wine vinegar. So yummy but watch out later 💨💨😂😂

This charred broccoli is my new favorite way to make it. So simple. Cut it up, put it in the cast iron and then put a heavy pot on top of it. Flip a few times, squeeze some lemon, add some salt after. Delicious

Go-to quick and easy comfort food.
Spanish Granja beans (butterbeans). Onions. Spicy chorizo. Canned tomatoes. Oregano.
You don't need a recipe. Just chuck it all in and let simmer. Fry the chorizo a little and soften the onions in that first.
A bowl of cheer-you-up.
You're welcome.
A rustic Sunday lunch; Vegetable and sausage traybake, featuring potatoes, sweet peppers, fennel, mushrooms, courgette. onions and veal sausages all tossed with evoo and baked.

Back on my savoury porridge bullshit and can confirm that making the porridge with miso soup paste and stirring through some peanut rayu is probably the best idea I'll have in 2024. This one is topped with kimchi fried tofu and soft boiled egg and is the lunch of champions.
Mr A is cooking brunchfast: Bubble&squeak, mushrooms, rashers, sausage and eggs; most pleasing of all, I hear coffee beans being ground and I smell toast. Life is sweet.

Take a steaming bowl of hot rice. Crack a raw egg into it, add a little soy sauce, and mix it up. That's Tamago (Kake) Gohan, the breakfast of champions. To up your TKG game, separate the egg first, whip the whites, mix them in, then mix in the yolk. I once ate this for breakfast for 6m straight.

Sautéed onions and garlic in butter. Added cauliflower, pan cooked over medium for 10ish minutes, then added broth and steamed for five.
Added pumpkin purée, a little more broth, This Little Goat’s This Little Goat Went to Morocco spice mix, black pepper, chili flakes. 🍽️
Grünkohl AKA kale stew.   Once wilted, tossed the mixture, adding small potatoes and veggie stock. Pork chops go on top. Now simmering on low for a few hours. Will serve with spicy brown mustard.
Bun with charred guacamole, crispy baked halloumi with melted Gouda on it, and marinated tomato (cumin, la chinata, worcestersauce, salt, olive oil). Some left over cheese and guac as an amuse. 🍜🍽️🥑
Anthony made a traditional carnitas recipe, slow braised in lard, evaporated milk, and Mexican Coke. Incredible. I’m eating it in hard shell tacos because I’m a monster lol

All the fishcake steps at once I got hungry and just cooked, forgot to take pictures.
Mix flaked fish into potato mixture, form into patties, dip in egg wash and then bread crumbs. Fry 3 minutes a side until golden brown. Allow to drain.
I served them with a poached egg and hollandaise sauce. 🍜🍽️
When you’re a mom and a restaurant cook, you make your kids their fave dish from your restaurant for Valentine’s Day. Chicken liver patê with dried cranberry mostarda and sourdough toast. 🍜🍽️
I don't know who needs to hear this but two parts tahini to one part pickle juice (by weight), whisked together with a little water, makes a shockingly good little tahini sauce
In case you were wondering, mantis shrimp are delicious! Like lobster or langoustines with the texture of crayfish. But the meat is a little difficult to get out of the shell

todays goblin lunch is what i’m calling the “eggchilada bowl”— two soft boiled eggs & two fried bean n cheese corn tortilla tacos topped with salsa & tapatío . the yolks & salsas become the sauce for the tacos which then kinda taste like rich enchiladas. nom nom

i am a light vinaigrettes guy:

champagne vinegar, olive oil, meyer lemon juice, black pepper, thyme, and some good dijon mustard shaken together is one of my go-to dressings.

also don’t forget to salt your salad!
Based on clafoutis I maintain a cherry sour cream sauce would be great on schnitzel and roast potatoes and tonight I’m going to prove it

Tonight I had an excellent pasta salad. I made a Caesar dressing alla Italiano with balsamic instead of lemon juice, no anchovy (they’re very Italian but I don’t love them), and all olive oil. Added roasted red pepper and olives from a jar, tossed with rotini. So simple, so delicious, ate too much.

Steak seared and basted with butter, salt, cracked pepper and garlic.
Roasted turnips and persimmons, tosser with Nando's medium garlic sauce and finished with freshly grated parm cheese.
Lemon fettuccine with a lemon, butter, garlic, white wine sauce. Lots of Parmigiano-Reggiano on top. Not exactly pretty but absolutely delicious.

I baked this Camembert cheese seasoned with rosemary sprigs, Malden salt, and a little garlic, drizzled with a little honey and olive oil. The perfect bread companion; a crusty, homemade sourdough baguette, baked to perfection for that irresistible crunch - delicious!
Lamb leggy slow cooked for 10 hours. Carrots, gongura, sage, fenugreek, coriander, hot chili, onion, tomatoes. It’s excellent :3

all condiments are BEAUTIFUL

mustard: robust, bold
ketchup: humble, cherished
mayonnaise: unfairly reviled, the do-it-all sauce base
sriracha:
bbq sauce: a bedrock of cuisine

Potatoes are nutritionally complete. In addition to starch, potatoes contain vitamins, minerals, and fiber. They're rich in vitamin C, which is an antioxidant…

…I tell myself, reheating my 4th baked potato of the day.

kind of lukewarm on a lot of the American Culinary Contributions to the world but the sliced everything bagel with a sausage patty, flat-top fried egg, and cheddar cheese wrapped in foil is an all-timer
Scotch eggs, but instead of wrapping the eggs in pork sausage meat, it's curried sweet potato and butterbeans, making this suitable for vegetarians.

Happy Bolognese Day to all who celebrate. I usually tweak recipes, but I follow this one verbatim because it’s perfect. The simple ingredients are made to shine by slow cooking for nearly four hours. (Ope, I use stock rather than wine). 🍽️🇮🇹🍅

Cut up your jack-o-lantern, throw your spice rack (and a little brown sugar) at it, roast it, and then put a nice hanger steak on top. Simple, satisfying and yet at the same time a provocative mashup of various old and new worlds. Also just a nice break from potatoes. Eat them pumpkins!

Yesterday’s beans and tortillas got turned into crispy tostadas and topped with scrambled eggs with green onions and the salsa verde from making my carnitas. 

I just rub corn tortillas with ghee and start crisping them in a pan then finish them in a toaster oven before topping ‘em. 🍜

This hotel has three types of tacos:

- fried shrimp, with a mild and tasty avocado salsa

- carne asada, with a delicious and spicy tomato and jalapeno salsa

-carnitas, with a scotch bonnet-based salsa that’s like licking a volcano

European healthy breakfast: Dr. Fuckenberg’s original Sport-muesli with puffed rice and apple

Edible flowers can be a real visual treat, and Marigolds also have a bold and delicious flavor. There are distinct pepper and citrus flavors that combine to create a savory broth for tortellini. It also adds that touch of color that always makes your meal more appealing. Enjoy!

Lamb stew (made with real lamb stock) and fresh baked bread with Kerrygold butter.

dude this was fucking great. absolutely flawless execution of the fundamentals of macaroni and cheese. the breadcrumbs not as a topping but as a thickener after combining the milk and cheese totally transform it. the top would have come out crispier too but i had to turn off the oven early

This is the best turkey meatloaf I’ve ever made. The lentils gave it such a great texture and added moisture. The sauce is a spicy smoky bbq sauce which balances so well with the sweet potato. So good. This one is going in my cookbook. 😉🍜🍽️
Pro tip, if you get back from the chippy and your fish/sausage batter has gone a bit soft, want in the air fryer at 200 for 5 minutes, will crisp right up
Making tortilla soup for dinner later and it smells so good in my house with the chilies and onions and epazote simmering away in the tomato sauce. 🍽️🍜

I need to find the secret to a good stir fry. It's great for using up extra leftover bits. Every time I try to make it the meat seems lacking flavor and the sauce is just nothing or salty. Maybe I just don't jam with this kind of cuisine.
One of my special recipes I like to cook at home is to combine Parmesan flavor Goldfish with Pizza flavor Goldfish. I call it, "extra cheese pizza pleasy".

Fried egg sandwich, egg was fried in steak fond and seasoned with white pepper and mexican oregano, on toasted sourdough with a sauce made from 1tsp mayonnaise, 1tsp crema, glug of olive oil with powdered garlic and italian seasoning. Blowin’ my tits off ova here

Friday Kitchen Fuckery-

Homemade crispy Parmesan polenta cakes, over a roasted garlic and cherry tomato sauce, sautéed broccoli rabe with lemon, garlic, and red pepper flakes, and shrimp with dill walnut pesto.

folks I just made some really good roasted asparagus & it was literally the easiest recipe ever:

trim a bunch of thick asparagus spears, drizzle w olive oil, salt & pepper on a baking sheet, toss to cost & stick it in a 400° oven for 20 minutes

BOOM delicious vegetable!
Despite still being sick I cooked an absolute boss dinner tonight; fresh fish panfried with butter and a little lemon, creamy leeks with parmesan and simple grilled asparagus.
Prime rib w/horseradish sauce, yorkshire pudd, traybake, salad, brussels sprout salad with goat cheese & pomegranate seeds. Mulled wine.
Breakfast: scrambled eggs, an avocado, homemade pico de gallo, hot sauce, fresh dill. I like it when my meals are 50% condiment, dressing, sauce, or garnish by weight. 🍽️

Soft, cloud-like sesame milk buns made using the Tangzhong method. Sprinkled b&w white sesame seeds just before going into the oven and finished with a brush of garlic and spring onion in melted butter and flaky sea salt. 

Love the textures, will go well w/ an Asian dinner.

there are a lot of great salads but the BEST salad is just arugula, vine-ripe tomatoes, blue cheese crumbles, dressed with evoo + balsamic and dusted with maldon and fresh-cracked pepper

achievements in lunch: you can put a little bit of sticky rice in a mini waffle iron and then dump some soy sauce and furikake on there and plop a soft-boiled egg on top. you can do this and nobody can stop you

With shredded chicken, prawn and a bunch of veggies. 

it’s a favourite.

Roasted, spiced monkfish, Santorini fava, capers, and a sprinkling of bottarga.

青空ごはん部

カツ重 (katsudon) is a hearty and beloved Japanese dish that combines a crispy deep-fried pork cutlet (tonkatsu) with a bowl of fluffy rice, all smothered in a sweet and savory sauce. It's a popular choice for both lunch and dinner, offering a satisfying and flavorful experience.

I grabbed for myself a "BMore Effect!" from Munchin Burger. It's a ground beef patty that's seasoned with Old Bay topped with crab meat, bacon, arugula, and cheddar cheese. With some fries with cilantro lime sauce for dip.

I have some words. A few will understand. A crispy fried egg in butter. The edges of the egg are golden brown. A red country sausage, split in half fried in butter so it's charred on both sides. Now served on the plate there's a heap of salt butter & pepper grits. And that's a breakfast of the gods.

Little one bite salad from the deck pots while gardening. 

Recipe: 
1 Ethiopian kale leaf

Carbonara. Fresh pasta. Using bacon, like a savage. Ain’t buying guanciale in this economy for a bit. Mix of pecorino and parmigiano.
