Sri Lankan cabbage and carrot curry. Cooked with cashews, lots of spices, and coconut milk. Served with a tangy lime and coconut sambal.

Any time I find beef tongue on special I will always buy it. It's my second favourite part of the head, cheeks as number one.
Parboiled with citrus fruits, garlic, onions, celery, fresh herbs, salt and pepper.
Then sliced thin, dry rubbed, and seared. 🍜
Here we have a katsu curry burger! Crispy fried chicken seasoned with Japanese togarashi spice. Thick spicy Japanese curry sauce. Shredded cabbage, pickle and tomato on a wholewheat bun.

I’m going with my hometown of seattle here: teriyaki.

yes you can get teriyaki chicken anywhere but not the way seattle teriyaki places make it. it’s savory-sweet and tender and delicious and comes with a pound of rice and a salad and it’s perfect
I know it sounds bland and boring but I got myself an exquisitely good head of English celery. I cooked it as a gratin with Parmesan and breadcrumbs
Rocamadour cheese, local smoked duck breast and smoked almond salad with a honey mustard vinaigrette. Was just lacking an orange but still delicious 🍽

It doesn’t look glamorous but it’s tasty!

A chuck and mushroom stew (button, cremini, shiitake, oyster, king oyster) over brown and wild rice.

Kupus kifle ('cabbage' bread rolls) filled with ham and cheese. It's the same as any bread roll, just easier to make because you simply roll out the dough, put your toppings, roll into a log, slice and bake until golden.

I made a side for a steak dinner on the weekend called potato pavé, very finely sliced potatoes layered in garlic, sage and thyme infused cream, baked low for hours, compressed overnight then sliced and pan fried. It was very good

I love the challenge of making leftovers interesting on the fourth day. I rubbed my corn tortillas in carnitas fat and then crisped them up for tostada bases, then topped it with carnitas and a funky mix of leftover tomatoes, avocado, pickled red onions, cilantro, salsa verde. 🍜

This is μακαρόνια με κιμά (the Greek version of ragù), spiced with cinnamon, clove and allspice and topped with Parmesan and feta. If you’ve not tired this (and you’re not veg/vegan) you need this in your life 😋🥰🥰

You know what’s amazing?

A couple of yellow onions, halved, dressed with butter, salt and pepper… throw them in a hot oven for 45 minutes until they are practically falling apart.

Serve with balsamico.

Amazing.

The 'Ajitama,' a flavorful egg marinated in a Japanese-style dashi and soy sauce, is a popular dish. I used a concentrated stock called 'Shirodashi' and a citrusy soy sauce, 'Ponzu'. Its subtle acidity entices the appetite, and you can savor the depth of the egg flavor with a hint of stock.

Fascinating! Vegan meatballs made from kidney beans. Definitely need to try this. #veganfood 🍜🍽️

Tried making a meatball using cannellini beans. Was tasty but structurally wasn't as firm as I'd like in a veg ball. 

Note for US: Linseed is flax...which imo makes this meatball even more appealing.

Probably not a good idea to ask me what my fav Thai restaurant is, even in my “home” city - mostly because I cook the way I was taught to love Thai food growing up.
Today, my partner asked for Thai so I made แกงกะหรี่ไก่ (Thai yellow curry chicken) and ลาบหมุ (Thai pork laab) with sticky rice

ok back to loaded fries. home grown potatoes, peeled & sliced & boiled then drained & tossed in olive oil & smoked garlic salt. then grated (dairy free) cheese on top with chopped ham, spring onion from garden, chopped shallot from garden, with homemade guacamole and sour coconut yoghurt & hot sauce

Guinness braised beef short ribs. Mashed potatoes made with whole cream and butter. Carrots glazed in butter, honey, ginger, and garlic. 🍜🍽️
My go to easy fancy salad is just shrimp, diced celery, chives, hearts of palm and orange (good olive oil, sprinkle of s/p)

here’s my peppercorn roasted turkey, which I always bake pulled apart — and glazed in soy balsamic with garlic and orange and LOT Of thyme - came out pretty moist and crispy this time after several coats of the glaze.
Steakhouse dinner at home: perfectly reverse seared NY strip, mashed potatoes, and luscious creamed collard greens from my overwintering garden collards. 🌱🍽️🍜

The graham crackers and chocolate are the only defining features of a s'more. You can put anything in the middle, as long as you toast it over a fire. A banana. A tomato. A human thumb. Get creative with it.

since my last taco-related recommendation was such a hit, here's another one: instead of a spoon, use a frosting decorating bag filled with sour cream. that way you can perfectly control the amount and location of your sour cream in your taco
Pomegranate, persimmon, and pear salsa with onion, garlic, mint, sage, and rosemary, tossed with olive oil and salt. I roasted half the ingredients and left the others raw.
Toad in the hole, mash, cabbage and onion gravy with Dijon mustard. Delicious but a stodge fest too.

Important culinary secret for tomato-based sauces: Gochujang. Bolognese? Gochujang. Arrabiata? More gochujang. Chili of any description? You guessed it, gochujang. It hurts to type this because of my finger, but it's worth it to tell you this

For Sunday lunch I cooked Anna's @@31272 re-engineered Moroccan-style short ribs. wow, the meat fell off the bone, juicy, succulent and well-served with mashed potato - I didn't have any couscous on hand - the ultimate comfort food!

I made tacos. Ground beef, onions, shredded lettuce, carrots, street corn with hot peppers, cheddar and colors cheese, a slice of daikon radish on the bottom for crunch, all on a corn tortilla and covered in taco sauce + a friends homemade hot sauce for kick.

Bucatini all'Amatriciana tonight! Originally from the Sopranos cookbook (laugh if you will, every single recipe I've tried from it has been a BANGER) but tonight I'm using some modifications from Serious Eats. 

Oh, and no guanciale, just pancetta, because I live in the woods. It's perfectly fine.

Mia en place for a simple Chicken Cacciatore. That little block in front of the chicken is my secret to making really deep, rich tomato sauces. A hunk of the rind from Parmigiano Reggiano helps offset the often flat flavor of canned tomatoes. 🍽️🍝

spruce up leftover breakfast oatmeal for lunch by making little patties, frying it up in butter and putting syrup on it

I still do that to this day and never really thought about how it's probably not "normal."

I love it though.

This is a dish called "Gomoku Ankake Rice".
It is made with stir-fried vegetables and seafood, such as shrimp, and served over rice.It goes well with beer and is often served at Chinese restaurants.
All the warmth I needed: Cheese ravioli with diced pancetta, gruyere, garlic and lots of smoked paprika. Flash-finished in a really hot oven, it was amazing. 🍽️

I like to include cumin when I prepare pretty much all beans including black eyed peas. I like how Moroccans liberally use cumin as seasoning particularly in beans & other things & feel it's great for legumes.

Grilled pork steak recipe. Marinate in Mojo Cuban marinade 1-2 days. Grill on high to 165 internal. Pull and rest 10 mins. Serve with oven roasted rosemary potatoes and green beans.

bouillon is always my go-to but you can always punch up "box" stock by just boiling it ahead of time with vegetable trimmings day of with stuff you already have. If you have the space, saving your trimmings in a freezer bag is the way to go, otherwise, just buy a nice thing of Better Than Bouillon

Fun experiment from breakfast: Bacon and egg sandwiches dipped in French Toast mixture and then finished in a waffle iron 🧇 

The result was a savory/sweet waffle that could either be eaten as is or with some maple syrup! Next I wanna try some Nutella in the middle 🤤

Three must do Thanksgiving leftovers (assuming a traditional ish meal):

Turkey sandwiches with stuffing, gravy, and cranberry sauce 

Stuffing waffles with poached egg, gravy and maple syrup, celery leaves 

Turkey miso ramen with turkey chashu, hard boiled egg, etc.

THIS. Tofu is so versatile. When prepared properly is incredibly delicious.

Gamechanger. Freeze block of tofu. Defrost it for cooking. Freezing changes texture making it meatier. Example, this Bulgogi Fried Tofu + Shiitake main.

Made a vegan tofu scramble that parents thought was scrambled eggs. 🍽️

My first Moroccan eggs this year! Now that I can eat decent gluten-free bread again, I can revisit some of my fave eggs. :)

In Japan, you can almost always find this type of egg bread at local bakeries. It's bread filled with mashed egg, and it's almost always tasty. I assumed it'd be sold in other countries too, but my friend in Canada told me they don't have anything like it. 😱

had a bunch of surgery-recovery brain fog today so i forced myself to do yoga and cook dinner. baked sweet potatoes w nutritional yeast, plus tomatoes and white beans sauteed in ACV and sriracha, topped with garlic-lime-mayo. pretty good.

My Christmas beef wellington is the best (and well, second) one I’ve ever made. Made the puff pastry myself, crispy ALL around even the bottom, with no sogginess. ;_; A little amazed with myself!
Best sushi I've ever had was made with pistachios, lime, basil, and cilantro alongside the raw fish. And a vegan sushi made with nuts, basil, and brie was remarkably satisfying.

It was a day to make more of my home made Bolognese sauce. 2 lbs ground beef, 1 lb minced sweet Italian sausage, 30 oz crushed Roma tomatoes, 30 oz tomato sauce, 2 cups minced onions, 1 cup minced garlic, oregano, thyme, basil, sage, rosemary , black pepper to taste.

Ain’t gonna plate this but air fryer has completely changed how I cook my gyoza. Spray a lil bit of high smoke point oil on it (avocado oil in this case) and put it in at 400c for 10m (more if bigger uncooked ones)

Sourdough covered in a rosemary garlic butter, filled with sharp white cheddar, Gruyère and colby jack.

The golden potato pizza from Koreatown Pizza Co. Potato wedges, bacon, Ground beef, corn, onion, sour cream, tomato sauce, mozzarella cheese, mushrooms, sweet potato crust. This is so goddam good. The crust is genius.

a friend who went to Italy told me there are three kinds of Italian breakfast:
(1) coffee + cigarette
(2) wine + cigarette
(3) coffee + small pastry to dip inside

by mixing coffee with wine and placing a cigarette in pastry and dipping it in coffee wine, we can create the Ultimate Italian Breakfast

Seafood Doria is a type of gratin that combines seafood, white sauce, cheese. It is made by layering rice, seafood, and sauce in a baking dish, and then baking it until the cheese is melted and bubbly. Seafood Doria is a popular dish in Japan, and is often served hot and enjoyed as a comfort food.

Omelette stuffed w squash blossoms, tomatillos, shiitakes, cheese spread (grated cheddar mixed w herbed feta). Seasoned w rosemary, paprika, black truffle salt. The sliced avocado has scorpion salt (yes, real scorpions) #FoodIsGood

Low-energy meal prep plans:

1. Buy salad bags and rotisserie chicken. Remove the skin and bones, and shred the meat by hand to put it into an airtight container. Prep portions as needed with/ dressings or spices you like.

2. Get tuna packets, lite mayo, and street taco tortillas for a fast lunch.
Butternut squash and saffron soup with caramelized pistachios and herb salsa; raddichio and apple salad with celery and English cheddar

Bluefish with an abundance of (balcony) tarragon. Cucumber and feta over (balcony) sorrel. This needed salt and/or vinegar and/or something other than what I put on it, which was just oil and pepper. 🍽️🌱

This is the best boxed macaroni and cheese. 

Add some garden🌱 kale, some garden🌱broccoli, an onion, and leftover chicken and you got yourself a meal. 🍜🍽️

Unfortunately I already put all the garden jalapeños into a foraged cranberry jam. 🤷‍♀️

Started lunch at 6:30am hours of simmering has made a outstanding sauce. My meatballs are big I’d rather make 20 not 40! Topped with grated parmigiana reggiano & crushed pistachios, freshly baked bread, olive oil & balsamic vinegar reduction😊. #SundayDinner #spaghetti #meatballs 🍽️🍜

Saturday brunch is: Bacon and eggs. It’s nice thick dry-cured bacon from Costco, and the eggs are scrambled in the fat rendered from the bacon. 

I’ll be trying out Truff hot sauce on my eggs this time. I’ve heard it’s good.

Look at all the interesting vegetables you get with this. Nice grilled half jalapeno. Cucumbers. Lightly grilled and heavily seasoned huge green onions.
It's delicious! Never had tacos this way and yeah the carne asada was delicious

That’s French toast. 

It’s my sourdough buckwheat bread, toasted walnuts, maple syrup, and was cooked in •generous• butter. 🍜🍽️

The eggs the bread was soaked in hard vanilla, dark maple syrup, cinnamon, and salt. Tasty.

How to make Greek style roasted lemon chicken with potatoes!

1. Peel potatoes and mix with juice and zest of 4 lemons, olive oil, a spoonful of mustard, salt and pepper and half a cup of water. Lay on a bed of sliced onions and roast uncovered for 30 min at 200°C.
Made this for lunch, it’s spaghetti with freshly shredded parmesan, olive oil, chinese chive oil, truffle paste, and fresh cracker black pepper. Mostly just proud of the plating 🍽️

The best part of homemade stuffed peppers is: 

a) The pepper, mellow and sweet
b) The filling
c) The tomato-y pan juices and stray bits of filling in the bottom of the pan

I take a lot of pleasure out of making sure my Bub gets the most nutritious meals I can make, especially for breakfast.
This is sliced avocado with pepper, her favourite, and strawberries, oatmeal with French vanilla yoghurt.

At night she sometimes only has Mac n Cheese so day meals are healthiest.
You want quality leftover action? How about thinly sliced prime rib, cheese, and stuffing served on lightly toasted marbled rye with mayo and Coleman’s mustard?
I made Chili Colorado from scratch, it’s a dried red chili sauce that has more hidden flavors then heat

🇺🇸🇬🇧Quick poke bowl with vinegared rice, edamame, corn, grapefruit, soft-boiled eggs, fried onions, marinated salmon, a few seeds and wasabi peas
🇫🇷Poke bowl vite fait avec riz vinaigré, edamame, maïs, pamplemousse, oeufs mollets, oignons frits, saumon mariné, des graines et pois wasabi
🍽️🍜 Gohanclub

Some of you are still waiting for compliments and I’ll get to them! But I can’t just now because it’s time for the family to eat.

Granny’s meatballs and sauce, served over some cascatelli and dusted with pecorino romano.
Meatball Monday.  Teriyaki & pineapple chicken meatballs is a sweet and sour sauce with black pepper, garlic and soy sauce. Red onion and mixed colored peppers served over brown rice.
very happy with this meal: fish tacos with a slaw dressed in an avocado dressing and a corn, tomato and bean salad on the side. oh, and sourdough tortillas
Lomo saltado tonight for dinner, which, if you haven’t had it, please do. It’s a Peruvian dish and it’s one of our favorite things.
Comfort food this evening: Lincolnshire sausages  wrapped in bacon with baked poratoes, baked beans and home made plum tkemali. 🍜🍽

Potatoes were cultivated by indigenous people across the Americas, but likely 1st domesticated in the Andes region of Peru 7-10,000 years ago from wild species Solanum brevicaule.

Cultivated potatoes are all tetraploid, while wild potatoes can be diploid, tetraploid, or hexaploid.

Rhododendron loranthiflorum, an epiphytic vireya species from the Solomon Islands. Nocturnally scented white flowers suggest moth pollination.  Some clones are very compact, but my plant is long and lanky. Not sure what’s up with the species name—flowers don’t look much like a Loranthus to me. 🌱

In 1960, David Latimer planted a garden inside of a bottle and sealed it shut. He opened the bottle and watered the plant in 1972 and sealed it for good. It has been a self sustaining ecosystem for 60 years.

The Texas mountain laurels are officially in full bloom! Not a true mountain laurel, it’s a small shrub-like evergreen in the pea family endemic to central and west Texas through northern Mexico. Their blooming season (March through April) fills the air with a STRONG smell resembling grape soda! 🌿

#FridayFlower Raoulia bryoides is a large firm cushion plant from the New Zealand mountains, often called a vegetable sheep. This at 1700 m in Nelson Lakes National Park last Wednesday. The flowers are in tiny heads, showing that this is a daisy (Family Asteraceae). I invited the ladybug for scale.

I'm sorry to tell you again, but fire increases the germination of Cistaceae seeds in contrast to summer-type heat (which reduces germination!)
Paper: fireecology.springeropen.com/articles/10.... Blog: jgpausas.blogs.uv.es/2024/02/18/s...

🧪🌍🔥🌿🌾 wildfire botany #PlantBiology #PlantScience

My primary reason for being on Madeira was hoping to encounter one of the rarest native European orchids, Madeiran endemic Goodyera macrophylla. Only a few dozen plants are known to flower annually, and in dangerously inaccessible places. Seeing this plant was a moment of sheer euphoria.

it’s the first wildflower of spring, although it doesn’t look like it! 🌿

Symplocarpus foetidus | skunk cabbage is a wetland plant that can generate their own heat and are the first eastern plant to bloom each year. The stinky little dome flowers are pollinated by flies. 

Araceae, like Monstera

Scrambled eggs! Scrambled eggs! 🍳 This cute native wildflower is often called “scrambled eggs” or “yellow Corydalis.” Across their native range you can find them blooming in and around rocky areas, creek beds, and hillsides from late winter through early fall as temperatures and conditions allow! 🌿

What wheat looks like grown in winter on a classroom windowsill.

It's sparse but alive, several wheat berries from just one planted months before. 

Our town is fairly urban--many of our children had never seen a wheat plant before. Some had never planted anything before.

Reason enough to teach.

Callicarpa bodinieri var. giraldii 'Profusion', Verbenaceae. The species is from E to Western China. This is in a North Yorkshire garden. Even on a dull day in low light, the fruits are startling - almost unreal

the blooms of Cistus ladanifer look as though they're made of crumpled silk; they last only one day, but are produced in long succession. I would still grow it if it didn't bloom at all simply for the pervasive, sweet amber fragrance of the foliage. 
commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Ci...

my greatest plant discovery to date, a county record for Iris verna | dwarf iris 🌿

a truly tiny plant with full iris flowers that loves sandy places. I found this, the bigger of two populations in Wise County, VA, in an old forest fire site that brimmed with botanical wonders

Next up: the Texas yellowstar, native to Texas; Oklahoma; Arkansas; through to Coahuila. This pretty annual herb is commonly found in prairie-like habitats across north-central and southern east Texas, specifically along the Edwards Plateau. It does best in well-draining sand and limestone. 🌿⭐️

The creek disappears into a dark tree-cave at the back of my property. Last year while clearing nearby brush I went in, shocked to find pieces of hydrangeas clinging to the sandy, pebbled banks. Their stems/roots were mostly petrified driftwood. I relocated 5. 4 have recovered. 2 have bloomed! 🌱 🌿

🌱 🧪 🧵 -= Cauliflory =-

1/ Nope, nothing to do with cauliflower - cauliflory is flowering and fruiting from trunks and large woody branches (caulis = stem).

Let's start this thread off with a banger: meet jaboticaba (Plinia cauliflora), the Brazilian "grape"-tree.

Singleaf piñon, Pinus monophylla, is the only single-needle pine - all others have 2-5 needles in bundles. It has large seeds, piñones, that are delicious. Piñones are important to Native Americans throughout the  range of the tree. This is an old tree on the side of a canyon near Petrified Forest.

Today's Gardening Advice: If you're looking for a deep red daylily, Buckyballs is an excellent choice.  The flowers are extremely dark and the foliage tends to be evergreen in warmer climates. It's not especially picky about soil (like most daylilies) but does prefer full sun.  🌱

We had a lemon tree on the balcony that outgrew its pot. We gave it to the caretaker, who planted it in the garden of our block of flats. This year it’s groaning under the weight of dozens of lemons so I’ve just been down to relieve it of part of its burden. The smell in our kitchen is unbelievable.

Cardinal Basil! A highly fragrant basil that grows to 24” tall. Bright green leaves contrast large, wine red flower clusters that fade to a dusty plum. Beneficial to pollinators, aroma deters common garden pests. 🌱💚

My 15-year-old jade plant recently went through a rough patch where she looked horrible— shriveled, drooping, discolored. Did you know that your plants’ soil can become hydrophobic? Well, a good repotting took care of that, and now my girl is back to her old self. Much to my relief.

The plant with the blue-green leaves is, surprisingly to me, actually a fern! Coffee fern/coffee cliffbrake. It grows on rocky outcrops in the chaparral and other drier environments, and its leaves change from green to purplish red as they age (fern in the background is a polypody of some sort) 🌿
🌱 Second year growing these dwarf “cosmic orange” sulphur cosmos and they are as fantastic as ever. Incredibly bright orange, floriferous, easy care—can’t ask for a better late-summer annual.

I have been cataloguing my thistles today. This is Carduus nutans, Musk Thistle or Nodding Thistle, a plant of pasture, roadside verges, & other grassy habitats with us in North Yorkshire. Has a fragrance of musk, or almonds. Blooming in August.

I planted this Fuschia in the ground last summer because it got aphids and I just wanted it to live or die. It inexplicably overwintered (zone 6), regrew, and bloomed beautifully. Now that I know it can, I'm going to mulch it and cover it with snow and see if we can make it through another winter! 🌱

We report: the mimosa trees are blooming bright yellow in the freezing rain. Every year when they bloom, we get surprised - middle of the winter, every other plant is putting its energy into braving the cold, yet the mimosa tree loudly bursts into song. We have the sniffles.

TIL: Haas Avocado trees bloom March - May with each flower lasting only 2 days. The flower opens in the morning as a female, and then the following afternoon as a male. Seems wild and while they self pollinate the best harvests come from cross pollinating. 🌱

For the seed growers here, you probably know that starter soil is expensive to buy and/or time-consuming to make, so I did an experiment to see how important it is.

Both trays have the same 5 rows of seeds, left is potting soil, right is starter soil. Photos are 1 week apart.

🧑🏼‍🌾 gardening 🌱 plants

I have carried out an experiment. I’m planting multiple seeds straight in the ground in short beds, covering them until the birds can’t harm them. I can do this every 2-3 weeks and it’s a lot less labour intensive! And I get use use the whole seed packet.🌱

Pink evening primrose! Although this spreading perennial has a wide naturalized range, evening primroses are considered native to only Nebraska; Kansas; Missouri; Oklahoma; Texas; though northeastern Mexico where extensive colonies occupy grassy fields. They bloom from late winter through fall. 🌿
I know we all associate Cilantro with warm places, but it’s damn cold tolerant. My best crops are usually fall through the winter. 🌱this group has been harvested to the ground at least 8 times.

Today's Gardening Advice: Harry Lauder's Walking Stick is a neat shrub for winter interest. All twisty branches and early catkins. Japanese Beetles and sucker growth can be issues. Like full sun and average garden soil. 🌱

We smelled a corpse flower, today (amorphophallus titanum). It was AMAZING. It was like rancid Camembert and rotting raccoon, but also a PLANT with that green rich planty smell woven through, and it was GREAT.

Oh!! I’ve been growing fish peppers the last 3-4 years! They’re a fantastic little pepper, a little hotter than a jalapeño. the plants are breathtakingly beautiful with variegated leaves. The peppers start out gorgeously striped too, altho they ripen to a solid red.

Feeling like hot garbage so I’m amusing myself by looking for homes for the native persimmon trees I started from seed last fall. It’s working! I have two people wanting two seedlings each and I feel so happy about my neighbors’ future food security and the beauty of free stuff. 🌱

Western marbleseeds (Lithospermum bejariense) are interesting flowers, considered native to the southeastern USA. They are often named “soft hair” marbleseeds, though this common name belongs to a different but strikingly similar species (L. molle), endemic to the Nashville Basin in Tennessee. 🌿

Seed saving season.

Basil seeds time--I love the tiny crunching as I free the seeds from their pods, the feeling as I lightly blow the chaff away, the pile of tiny black seeds left in the creases of my palm.

Oh, and the aroma left behind. Seed collecting is its own gift....

A cyclamen seedling displaying a unique flower color pattern and with heavy purple coloring on the underside of the leaves, which none of the mother plants had, hopefully indicating a successful crossing! 🌱

#flowers #botany #cyclamen

🌱 Syringa 'Penda' Bloomerang®️/ reblooming dwarf lilac [Syringa × 'Josée f. × unknown hybrid m.; 'Josée' herself is [S. meyeri × (S. pubescens ssp. microphylla × S. p. ssp. patula)]. Spring features larger more pinker panicles; those in late Summer-Fall are smaller & purpler. Blooming now z7! 💜

Today's garden job was transplanting lupins from my nursery bed into the borders. These are so easy to grow from seed as a short-lived perennial and they self-seed everywhere on my #allotment. Much more resistant to slugs than plants from the garden centre! 😊🌱

Decided to plant potatoes on a whim in an overgrown area of the property surrounded by hedges. Had no idea if it got enough light or had good enough soil. Of all 3 areas I planted them, it’s flourishing best here. Sometimes rolling the dice pays off. 🌱

Two years ago, I planted passion flower seeds, but they didn't germinate — due to weather conditions. One seed found its way to the base of a small tree and sprouted. The vine began climbing the tree. This year the tree is covered with passion flowers and passion fruit. A delightful turn of events!

Born in the tropical forests of Sri Lanka True (Ceylon) Cinnamon is well suited to grow in our zone 9b. This tree can grow up to 50 ft, or be pruned to live well in a container. It will not tolerate temperatures below 40 degrees, be sure to protect it from rare frosts. 🌱

Image via Tanria Elementals

🌱 The perennial sunflowers are starting to bloom. It's nice to see them come back for the 2nd yr. Had to deadhead aggressively last year as I didn't want it taking over the yard.I'm still hoping to find some Mexican sunflowers. Not as tall, comes back every yearly & can be planted as a nice hedge.

Today's Gardening Advice: Check seed packet date ranges prior to starting seedlings.  Some vegetable seeds germinate quickly and grow just as fast.  Too often I hear from people, "Oh, I started my tomatoes in January and now they're all leggy." Yes, wait, read directions, don't start too soon. 🌱

The first harvested Sweet Sunset. It's starting to ripen and starting to get a little wrinkly around the damaged part so I decided the time was right.

Overall, a moderately sweet pepper (at least at this ripeness) with a very mild and very fruity flavour.

DRAMA! I just went out to talk to my veggie garden. The cucumbers were STRANGLING the tomato plants!! The cucumbers have taken over my garden and wrapped their tendrils around  the neighbouring plants. NO SIRS. You back off.

I re-staked the tomato plants and told them everything was going to be ok.
🌱🤣 My Apple Watch 🍏 about a week in. 💯 Macintosh germination rate, 25% Johnny Appleseed germination rate. Fertilizer is koi pond water. All the nitrates you could ever want! 🐠💩

Are those green flowers? Yep. These are Edwards Plateau five-eyes. They are summer bloomers but if you couldn’t tell from my other posts, we’re headed in that direction fast & early this year. A Chamaesaracha sp., this flower is native to central and south central Texas, preferring rocky habitats. 🌿

My heath aster is blooming! I’m so excited about this stuff. I planted 3-4 babies last fall and it has spread beautifully. Keeps down weeds, flowers in fall when the pollinators need food, and is native to much of N America. Seems hardy af so far. Why don’t I see this everywhere as ground cover? 🌱

My heritage tomato, a Cœur de Bœuf Rouge has finally ripened in the window & was ready for seed harvesting. The seeds were like orange segments in each bulge. I paid around $5 for this tomato. It came from seeds discovered in an attic that had been there for over 60 years & the cultivar was revived.

Native to the Mediterranean, Oregano loves to grow in sunny Florida! Give it a big spot in well-drained soil and it will flourish. After flowering the plant dies back some, but can still withstand a hearty trimming. Prune to a small bush as it can get leggy and try to spread. 🌱

📷: Tanria Elementals

Parochetus communis 🌱

This unique, spreading clover produces single gentian-blue flowers, as opposed to the florets containing many tiny flowers found in true clovers (Trifolium).

Beautiful and nitrogen-fixing!

It’s remarkably easy to grow the most expensive spice on Earth in your home garden. 
Saffron grows from bulbs / corms. They bloom in the Fall and produce 3 threads that you want to carefully hand harvest on flowering day. Dry in parchment paper and place in an airtight jar. 🌱

Watermelon seedling finding a new home in thick organic mulch, a mixture of dried grass with cow and donkey manure.

We are doing a trial to determine whether the manure is broken down enough to support seedling growth.

If it is, and we get fruits, they going be delicious! 🌱

Once the primary 🌱 agricultural focus of the region, #Meghri #Armenia has has shifted away from Soviet-enforced pomegranate orchards to the more profitable (and perishable) persimmon.

Deprioritized, pomegranates still get packed - but no new orchards get planted. Legacy trees straggle along.

Ilex dedidua | possumhaw 🌿

This little tree was less than a meter tall and growing out of a crack in a mossy granite boulder and was still laden with fruit for migrating birds.

This is one of our rarer hollies in eastern Virginia, and has the odd distinction of being deciduous
Glorious colours on the first day of this year’s Orchid Festival at Kew Gardens. Well worth a visit if orchids are your thing, or even if they’re not. On till 3rd March.

🌱 The wild columbine is blooming all over the garden. A native which is not planted so much as exuberantly spreads itself by seeds. Not hugely invasive and easy to pull up if it lands somewhere you really don't want it but a lovely early spring bloom to enjoy while waiting for other plants to bloom.

🌱  Behind the tomatoes there is a hardy strawberry in full sun. Its two siblings are in the raised bed but I kept this one here and it grows a ripe strawberry every few days.
Very hardy plant, tasty sweet fruit.

I am selecting plants for the next years, this strawberry is here to stay.

Happy New Year! Hoping for something completely different? How about Passiflora foetida the Stinking Passionflower? This one was blooming at the base of a heiau at La Perouse Bay, Jan. 2019. Certainly one of nature’s weirder floral arrangements!Wishing everyone a fun new year, maybe not this weird.🌿

A lovely old French iris from 1927, 'Floridor'. It has a wonderful, heavy grape fragrance, but its best feature is the unique shade of blue, often described as slate blue in the old catalogs. It really stands out in the garden and is such a good grower and bloomer.

One of our patches of Blue-eyed grass is blooming nicely even though it has been cool and mostly cloudy lately. It looks like grass (except for the blooms) but is in the Iris family. 
2nd photo is a bloom closeup. 🌿 #NativePlants Southern California

🌱 Eastern black swallowtail/ Papilio polyxenes asterius (Stoll) finally alights on a red dahlia/ Dahlia parvibracteata (Saar & P.D. Sørensen) so I can take her picture. 🦋
Black swallowtails, pipevine swallowtails, and monarchs, as well as many bees favor this nonnative to my area single dahlia.

Had drie jaar laurier met hagelschot (schimmel) erin. Niets aan gedaan, maar in de klikostraat gezet zodat die niet zo in het zicht stonden vanuit de keuken.
Jaartje later: blijkbaar vinden ze een dakterras op het zuiden niet fijn en gedijen ze uit de wind en in de halfschaduw.

Start with a small act of anti capitalism.

Next time you buy a tomato, save the seeds. The slimy stuff on the seed is a sprout inhibiter. Put the seeds onto a tissue or paper kitchen towel. Save it. Dry it. Stash it somewhere dark. 

Next spring, soak in water and place in dirt and grow tomatoes

This summer I grew alpine strawberries for the first time. Not only did they taste much more delicious than my regular strawberries, they were easier to grow from seed and produced constantly from spring to the last frost. I got a far bigger harvest from these than from my regular ones. 🌱🍓

Despite the chilly cold front this week, new patches of toothleaf goldeneyes along the Salado Greenway went in for a fall bloom! These attractive asters grow in bunched colonies and can be up to 3-6 ft tall. I think I saw three or four patches in flower while out on my run yesterday. 🌼🌿

The first California native wildflower to bloom from seed in my newly-created meadow section in my yard, Nemophila menziesii or Baby Blue Eyes. It’s a part of the Japanese-inspired native garden I’ve been working on for the last year after removing the lawn. 🌱

Nasturtiums are a must for any garden. Beautiful, unusual, and fully edible. The bees also go nuts for them.

Not sold? They also love poor soil and will help fix it, great companion plants to deter pests, and perfect cut flowers.

🌺 nasturtiums 🌸 flowers 🧑🏼‍🌾 gardening 🌱 plants 🥗 harvest 💦 irrigation

just learned that cotton is a mallow (i've never seen it in flower, just yoinked standing dry stalks off the roadside in oklahoma) and now understand why the weird prickly seed heads of hollyhocks are vaguely familiar... plants are so cool dude

Thyme is exceptionally easy to grow. It needs well draining soil and full sun, and loves to be trimmed. Waiting until just before flowering ensures the leaves have the most oil available. Our rainy season can damage the plant, but moving it indoors or away from the elements can help. 🌱

This snazzy little flower is a rain lily, Zephyranthes atamasca is native to a chunk of the Southeast but threatened in some of its range. I planted them lining the walkway in the back. They look like a clump of chives most of the year, but after a good rain, they flower (hence the name.)

Sunflowers may be the symbol of Ukraine, but they're native to North America, where they were first domesticated in the Late Woodland period.

Seeds found in Mexico are all pre-contact, suggesting Spanish priests suppressed them to block their importance in Sun-worship cults.

Question for the gardening folk: last year, my cosmos grew tall before they flowered—the ones on the sunnier patch got as tall as me and flowered pretty late into fall. This year, they have been staying much smaller, but blooming earlier. What might be the reason for this difference? 🌱

🌱 I planted some Two Tasty hybrid tomatoes from Burpee Seeds. In my area (NJ), they are performing well - fast growing & abundant with a decent cherry tomato flavor. The fruit size is about halfway between cherry and plum.

As you can see in the photo I took, they have an heirloom look to them.
A suberized exodermis is required for tomato drought tolerance. Exciting, impactful work from the Brady lab and I’m delighted to have contributed to it! doi.org/10.1038/s414...

While they love to grow in limestone, the Carnation doesn’t like the overly hot Summers of Florida. If you want to enjoy these blooms through Spring, best to plant in Fall. Small bushy plants that do not like wet conditions, be sure to monitor moisture levels during wet season!

Onion seedlings on a window sill with a bonus blurry view of the back garden. I like growing onions from seed because you can try different varieties and grow them in clumps, these are new ones for us, “Long Red Florence”, or “Rossa Lunga di Firenze”, if you prefer. 
#Allotment 🌱

Today's Gardening Advice: Certain wildflowers deserve to stay right in the woods.  Various orchids and trillium are difficult to establish in the average garden.  Orchids like the common lady slipper have symbiotic relationships with fungus.  Gardeners are too often tempted to try them and fail. 🌱

Considered by some to be the most important plant for pollination in North America, the Goldenrod (Solidago spp) is a gorgeous keystone species. Providing food for hundreds of insects and animals, this fall blooming flower can grow prolifically, even becoming weedy in some areas. 🌱

Romanesco cauliflower.

Wikipedia: "The inflorescence (the bud) is self-similar in character, with the branched meristems making up a logarithmic spiral, giving a form approximating a natural fractal; each bud is composed of a series of smaller buds, all arranged in yet another logarithmic spiral."

I've picked, roasted, and frozen the last of the cherry tomatoes. They got blight, thanks to the terrible weather, but they did OK. These tomatoes are ripening now in the small greenhouse. They're from seeds saved last year from shop-bought tomatoes and have done very well. 🌱
#Gardening #Tomatoes

Did you know saffron crocuses - which grow in your ordinary twenty-first-century garden - are basically clones of plants that grew in Minoan Crete, hundreds of years before Homer? Blows my mind. vertigrow.substack.com/p/saffron-cr...
Every year I plant a few less tomatoes (last year I had 700 cherry tomato plants, this year it's 650), every year my yield goes up faster than I planned and I end up with lots of surplus.

Growing dahlias from seed is a crap shoot, at best, but I adore the uncertainty of it. It makes every seedling a revelation.

This one came from a Yellow Cherish seed 🤷‍♀️.

You don't necessarily propogate your seed plants, but I really like this one and I'll probably save her tubers if they come.🌱

Whatever colour viridian is (viridis) a multitude of plants show us by way of example: and infinite though they are, their variety is such nevertheless that no one equals another.
Telesio, Libellus de Coloribus, Venice, 1528.
Green Hellebore, Helleborus viridis ssp. occidentalis (Reutter) Schiffner

First wildflower of…winter??? Texas baby-blue-eyes are native annuals distributed from Texas, Oklahoma, Louisiana, to Alabama. They generally have an early bloom season, starting from around mid/late February where winter temps are moderate, through the end of spring, and dying back by summer. 🌿

Ignore the fact my hand is all sweaty, I was finishing up a run lol…

Happy to report that it’s Texas persimmon (Diospyros texana) season! Persimmon fruits ripen around early August and are a fav among wildlife. TX persimmons have a small range from central and west TX through northeastern Mexico. 🧪

🌱 I must report that the apples are now wearing a nice blush on! 

This is one of four columnar trees I planted last year as bare roots; all are fruiting this year with about 7-12 fruits each tree. Looking to harvest in 5-6 weeks.🤞

Photinia × fraseri 'Little Red Robin'
Aka: red tip photinia and Christmas berry
Of garden origin
A cross between between Photinia glabra and Photinia serratifolia
Reaching just 2 to 3ft in height this lovely evergreen shrub is perfect for winter pot displays and is hardy to -10

Cosmos is the flower for the birth month of October and symbolizes balance and harmony.It attracts butterflies and is easily used as an attractive border or backdrop plant.Cosmos is simple and easy to grow from seed sown in the spring, readily reseeds itself,and tolerates a wide range of soil types.

I know it’s hella naive to say this, but at this point, I feel like I’ve grown every tomato 🍅 San Marzano, the Cherokees, the Rainbows…

And yet, nothing comes close to these two 🏆 B.K. is a vigorous shade/cold-tolerant heirloom with notes of thyme and oregano 🌱 and Sungold needs zero introduction.

The winter rains turned my yard of mulch into a meadow, and the first California native plants are starting to bloom! Closeups: wartleaf ceanothus, pitcher sage, and blueblossom. (If you're just tuning in, this was a grass lawn 3 years ago)

🌱 This bright yellow forsythia was engulfed in an invasive laurel hedge and barely hanging on when I found it in my side yard in March, 2020. 

One of countless ways I'm doing better than I was four years ago.

Aubergines in the greenhouse are finally getting going which is much appreciated but somewhat pointless given it's mid-October.
One normal sized aubergine and these three babies means the grafted aubergines have not paid for themselves this year (cf last year's ridiculous over production of 30+)🌱

🌱 Perennial hollyhocks (Alcalthaea suffrutescens "Parkallee & "Parkfrieden") in the morning light of the autumn equinox. They bloom from July until October (sometimes longer) and are very popular with wild bees.

A little Sunday trivia for ya:

I love Trillium 🤍 seeing them means spring is coming. It’s also Wisconsin’s State Flower. Don’t pick them because doing so can disrupt the plant’s ability to sustain itself and it can take years to bloom again- once established they can live 100 years!

Today's Gardening Advice: Hemerocallis "Katahdin" is one of my favorite daylilies. Tall scapes, prolific flowers, and grows well. A Ron Valente introduction, it is cold hardy and named for a mountain in Maine. Not the most fancy flower, but a robust, dependable performer. 🌱

My single cucumber plant may take over the world. It’s now over 6’ tall and 4 feet wide after training it up the lattice. The huge bushy tomato plants pale beside it. You can barely see the corn or peas at all. This is shaping up to be a banner garden year. There must be over 100 cucumber flowers! 🌱

You know it’s winter when my garage starts looking like a dispensary 😂 

Seriously though, if you’re paying for basil, you’re overpaying. Get yourself a grow lamp, a cardboard box, and some foil.

Oh, and one basil plant.

👨‍🌾 gardening 🌱 plants 🍃 basil 💧 hydroponics

My mother’s rose - Munstead Wood. Mum grew this in a pot by her back door. Now it is beside my back door, repotted and thriving.

It smells as glorious as it looks.

pulled it a bunch more morning glory today trying to give the creeping thyme a fighting chance to be the new groundcover of choice here. i planted a dwarf cherry this morning and i can’t stop walking out front to just look at it one more time🍒🌱

桃の花は割と短い期間しか綺麗でないので、見る時期が難しいものです。長野県の昼神温泉付近の花桃の里は、標高差があって、長期間どこか咲いていることが多いです。鯉のぼりがあるように、これは5月の写真です #flowers

Peach blossoms have a short blooming period and are difficult to see, but the flowers here can be seen for a long time (due to the difference in altitude). This is a photo taken in May

This Mandevilla used to climb a trellis over the old pond that is now empty. Had been neglected but now fed, watered and in a semi shade spot it has come back to life.
Still lots of warm weather to come so growth and flowering should keep improving.
Certainly worth saving.
🌱🇳🇿

Some lilacs are tolerant of heat and can flourish (zone-pusher) in the hot inland valleys of Southern California, with just that right location. “Now that the lilacs are in bloom, she has a bowl of lilacs in her room.” - T.S. Eliot 🌱#GardeningFeed
 
Syringa x hyacinthiflora  'Matthews Purple’
Spent a wet Sunday planting 250 flaxes into the second half of our wetland project. Most still need their PJs on :-) Still a few to go, but we should get close to 1000 trees when it’s all done.

Did you know we breed our own “black” sunflowers? Our Dark Chocolate sunflowers grow to 6ft with multiple branching heads, purple stems, and are uniformly darker than most chocolate varieties on the market.🌱💚🌻

Torrential rain here today so I spent it in the greenhouse. Sowed thyme, basil, brussels sprouts, peas, tomatoes, aubergines, broccoli and agastache. I also pricked out lettuce, sunflowers and heleniums. Busy busy! 🌱😊

Bought some blousy marigolds and spiky salvias for the garden. Trying to keep the local hover flies/bumblebees on-side (partially because I just love them and want them to hang out, but also I kind of sort of want them to pollinate my beans/squash plants).

Monterey cypress macrcarpa group in a large development tray.
2nd/3rd year from seed.
Picture of how they often grow in Northern Aotearoa. Masses of big timber, this one is on a northeastern beach front nearby.
#Bonsai 🌱🌳✂️🌲🪴

When my family lived in California in the late 60s, my mom planted a geranium next to the garage, later adding like this wire trellis system... it grew year round and covered the garage wall in red flowers. There were wild horned toads running all over catching ants. It was awesome.

Many daisies in Aotearoa (New Zealand) are shrubs or small trees. Heketara (Olearia rani) is flowering now. This is a cluster of tiny florets: 12 outer white ray florets each have a bilobed stigma; 5 inner yellow disk florets, each with 5 stamens joined in a tube & a stigma that pushes out pollen.

Every year I plant some heirloom tomato varieties & every year I get a few fragile, late-ripening heirloom tomatoes, while the hybrids & new varieties produce circles around them & are even more delicious & nostalgia is bad, actually
Almost unreal. Camellia flowers in full bloom in South East England in the month of January. They usually bloom in late February or early March.

If I only had room for one pepper plant, I would grow the Ethiopian Berbere Pepper. 

It’s hardy, productive, and the fruit has a perfect medium heat, with a distinct smoky flavor and notes of paprika and cacao. Versatile too, great for sauces, frying, or eating raw.

👨‍🌾 gardening 🌱 plants 🌶️ peppers

If you love summer squash, I highly recommend picking up some Lebanese koosa squash seeds.

This fast-growing, high-yield, compact bush plant can produce huge, flavorful fruit that are very versatile in the kitchen.

🍆 squash 🧑🏼‍🌾 gardening 🌱 plants 🥗 harvest 💦 irrigation

It’s 34F outside, raining tiny beads of sleet, & my previously barren camellia is in full bloom.I have learned three things about this plant this season: it does not like fall pruning(LOL), it likes plenty of acid fertilizer, and & it thrives in cooler weather .🌱Flower Report  #Gardening #Nature

This week marks the start of my tomato harvest, featuring hearty Romas, radiant Sungolds, and classic cherry tomatoes. My foray into organic gardening was a labour of love, using seaweed mulch, mushroom compost, etc. If the weather had been a tad warmer, this would have been a bumper crop!

When I was young, I thought hydrangeas were old-lady flowers. I aged into them. 20 years ago we bought this house w/a blue one & a purple one, & I learned to propagate, became obsessed, took cuttings from other people’s plants, & started a hydrangea garden here. I love how they age
🌱

As long as there's good drainage, Borage grows in most soil types even in containers! Known to be a great companion plant in the garden as a decoy and is said to improve the flavor of plants it's grown with. Pruning flowers will encourage more blooming plus they’re delicious!

📷:Tanria Elementals

I threw a few collard starts in the ground 🌱 in early summer, and I wasn’t sure how they would do around/under my peppers. Well… now I have collards for dayyyyyys. This plant is almost up to my waist. I’m making saag paneer tomorrow to go with aloo gobi from our own potatoes.

Montauk Daisy buds. These are always the last flowers to bloom in the yard, even later than the Chrysanthemums. In another week or so, these will be brilliant white patches of color against the green of the leaves. 🌱

My garden is starting to look quite good. This was a crumbling bank of “decorative” grasses when we moved in. After a few years I got them dug out, set to work building up the soil, laid bricks to create levels and hold the soil in place, and started planting about 18 months ago.

I had a couple handfuls of Two-Row Stonecrop on the property in a shady spot. Moved them to this sunny area and BLAMMO. They’ve filled in nicely AND have pretty pink flowers. They’re also amazing at weed suppression (even crab & quack grass).

First white dandelion of the spring.  We grow dandelions for chicken treats and occasional salad greens, but it gets boring growing the same old yellow ones.  Taraxacum albidum for the botany geeks. 🌱

🌱 Gardening friends, what was the best tomato you have ever grown, or one that you grow year after year? 

Starting my tomato list for next year. Looking for ones with good taste and disease resistance. Cold tolerance would be nice, too, but not a must have trait. Many thanks!

I like to think the citrus will be happier this winter with an east-facing window, ceiling light tube & grow lights. 🌱🌞🍋

Last year, the lemons taught us that overwintering in the kitchen with its north-facing window & single grow light was not the thing.

In Sydney for the next few weeks, and it’s noticeable how much more common brush turkeys are here in the suburbs than 10 or 20 years ago. The jacarandas are a sight (in October, thanks to the very warm spring; traditionally they don’t bloom until November.)

These warm humid North Carolina days has done wonders to the garden. I tested out some crops to see what'll do well in my region & the tomatoes, peppers, cucumbers, mint, sunflowers (even after the deer demolished them) onions & carrots are doing well. The cabbage & beans are pest magnets though.

We picked our first tomato today! True black brandywine, just over 17oz. It’s about halfway ripe, so we still need to wait a bit before eating it. (There is another from the same plant, of similar size, that we are letting ripen further on the vine as a point of comparison)

For fellow gardeners surrounded/ shaded out by black walnut trees 🌱: I sowed 3 tomato plants (sun gold) in my backyard, mere feet from the canopy edges of the dreaded juglone zone. Absolutely unaffected. Further context: my death-strip is the south canopy edges where squirrels drop the most husks.

This spring, exhausted with work stress, I chose to take a break and grow things. I grew 8 leaves, 12 herbs, and 2 tomato varieties, all from heirloom (pre-WWII) seeds. Everything was grown in chemical fertilizer and pesticide free soil, with a little vermicompost. It has been amazing.

For the 🌱/🍜 feeds:

This broadleaf variety of culinary sage has lasted several years in my garden. For me, the variegated & more common narrow-leaf varieties grew progressively weaker after each (US, NJ) winter before failing.

It’s tougher & more pungent than other varieties, but it’s thriving.
First harvest of sack grown potatoes (rocket, earlies). Day 60. 1.1kg. Not many, but they look good. Surprised how clean they’ve come out. Māra Kikorangi 🌱

Mums in the morning light: the 1st blossom of tiny "Herbstbrokat" is finally opening. And "Mary Stoker", one of the earliest Chrysanthemums who has been blooming in bright yellow for some reason, decided to finally produce the proper colour - and in a double blossom, too!
First tomato harvest, the yellows have so much flavor that the reds seem bland in comparison. Also giving my garden a weeding in this beautiful weather. 🌱

My bestie’s late husband gave me this desert rose housewarming gift 7+ yrs ago. It moved w/me 5 times & 4 mo ago I realized it might not make it. It had root rot. I dug it up, cut off all the roots, cleaned it up & put it in fresh cactus soil. It bloomed immediately & now 2 new arms are growing! 🌱☺️

Started all my nightshade seeds: tomatoes, peppers, and eggplant. 100+ cells planted and labeled. A huge hassle but worth it for the control, sturdier plants, money saved, and rarer varieties.

Now, we wait. 🌱

Brought a 20oz heirloom slicing tomato to the farmer’s market for the folks at our favorite farmstand. Ostensibly a TN varietal, we got it at a local nursery. It was the only plant and the tag was handwritten. They traded us for one of their own slivers but were SO happy to get ours.

Thought you folks might like to see skunk cabbage. Yes, these are actually flowers. They are one of the earliest spring blooms, so early that they generate heat to melt through snow, if there is any. Which makes a little oasis for winter pollinators

A volunteer ginger plant.
This was from two seasons ago.
🌱 
Now that the rain has started, plants will be popping up in places where they were previously planted. 
This local ginger is more pungent than what is sold in the supermarkets.

🌱Many techniques to speed germination of Lupinus like these 'Tutti Frutti Mix', as emergence can straggle. For me, boiling water less of a knucklebuster & easier on the carpal tunnel than sandpaper, nicking, or filing. I spoon them out as they swell, 3 rounds usually gets most.

Proud of this Baptisia australis (false blue indigo) plant for exceeding 1 foot in height. I started my Baptisia plants from seed last year, and... yeah, it's going to be a while before they get to full size. Which is fine. I love growing from seed, even though native perennials are slow. 🌱

Came across this beauty, a bald cypress, whose leaves - unlike those of the cypress in my backyard - haven't yet started to turn their customary autumn cinnamon-colour. I love how each tree has its own timing & expresses itself differently despite commonalities...sort of like us humans. 🌱

Sunchokes (Helianthus tuberosus) and sunflowers (H. annuum) can hybridize. The difficulty is that sunchokes bloom so late.

Fortunately, the giant sunflowers had a few secondary flowers that were shedding pollen. Now I just need to watch for seeds.

🌸 harbinger of spring 🌿
Erigenia bulbosa

the red anthers of these little flowers turns black over time, giving rise to another common name for them, pepper and salt, which is a really good common name but unfortunately doesn't go quite as hard as HARBINGER OF SPRING

#flowerreport

Paid a couple of bucks for a rosemary plant over a decade ago, stuck it into the ground, and it keeps on giving and giving and giving.

It flowers off and on all winter, which makes no sense, except to the lone bee that will find it on a freakishly warm February day.

Here's today's tiny show. 🍽️🌱

Today's Gardening Advice: Try some gardening adjacent activities in the new year.  Make a brush pile, put that half rotten log into the garden, add large rocks, start a compost pile, remove a patch of lawn, paint those reclaimed boards wild colors and put them in the garden.  Make an environment. 🌱

Harvested my first ever fingerling potatoes 🙂🌱 Planted using the Ruth Stout method in an attempt to kill a section of periwinkle. I turned the surface & pulled roots till I ran out of energy. Plunked the potatoes down & covered them with a foot of leaves. Watered 2x. Soil is now excellent.

Today's Gardening Advice: Thalictrum pubescens (also listed as Polygamum--same plant?) is a part shade loving perennial that tolerates moist soils.  This clump was found growing alongside a road in Newfane Vermont in a roadside ditch.  Attracts pollinators and easy to grow. 🌱

Sowed my first Spring seeds at the allotment 😀🌱

This time each year, I put all my leftover salad stuff leaves in a packet, and sprinkle some every couple of weeks. Gives me signals on how the soil is warming, when and if they germinate. And I usually get some early salads, too. 🌱👩‍🌾

Previous owners filled this space with goutweed. Last year, finished lugging rocks up from the creek. I turned & plucked as much of the goutweed as I could, transplanted some shade plants & a mossy log from other parts of the garden, and hoped for the best. Quite happy with the transformation 🌱

This week: Ericaceae are a cosmopolitan family with diverse habits. The morphological innovation that unites them all is the presence of appendages on the anthers, seen here on this dissected flower of Vaccinium pallidum. #Ericaceae #Botany #PlantScience 🌾🧪

Living room grow tent update
This is an amalgamation of a few pictures of what I now refer to as my “beautiful chaos tent” The cucumber is almost a foot long and I get a few cherry tomatoes every so often lettuce almost as needed and some peppers and other surprises will be ready soon 🌱

Scarlet and Dazzling Blue Kale are just gorgeous now. Transplanted half to greenhouse beds a month or so ago. The other half remained indoors, getting more light, and are much more "scarlet." Going to pick some this weekend. 🌱

Cistus ladanifer L. Gum Cistus. Native of the Western Mediterranean, the evergreen foliage exudes an intensely fragrant resin, used to lend amber notes in perfumery. The foliage is fragrant through most of the year but especially pervasive on warm summer days.
commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Ci...

🌱 the first of the viva la vida lilies have finally opened. One of the most glorious lilies, I think, although not the most aromatic. It's a flower that is both bold and vigorous, and just can't be ignored.

Not a pretty flower, but beautiful to me. In April of 2019, we spread 5" of compost on a 20' x 15' patch of undisturbed ground and have added  1" of compost every winter since then. It has never been dug or tilled. Today I pushed a blunt stick gently into the ground and measured the hole. 🌱

Ripped out an old rose that has sadly had its day (taking plenty of cuttings on the way), dug in soil improver and deep mulched then replanted with a few woodland favourites: hydrangea, hellebores, pittosporum, edgeworthia. Underplanted with epimediums and anemones. The rain did the watering-in.
fun plant fact: some species seeds need to go through a forest fire in order to germinate and you can simulate that by soaking them in water with a bit of liquid smoke. the more you know 🫴✨🌈
This is Leptospermum squarrosum (peach blossom tea-tree), with its gorgeous pink-tinged, five-petalled flowers. Common around Sydney and coastal NSW and flowering now. #FlowerReport 🌸

🌱 The "bosque blue" tomato is shaping up but compared to the black strawberry cherry tomato I grew last year, there is no shade of blue here, just a dark, almost black color. Have not tasted it yet but will likely grow the blk starwberry again instead of the bb next year.

yellow jessamine | Gelsemium sempervirens 🌿

one of the great plants of the deep south, but it only gets into the southeastern corner of Virginia, and is not common.

After a month of searching the Appomattox valley, I found enough seed for my friend to try to grow at her local native plant nursery.
One of the species that all tall bearded irises are descended from. Still a great garden plant, and the heavy grape fragrance is an added bonus.

🌱 The Jerusalem artichokes are now taller than my sunflowers from this past summer. It is flowering, and the cold weather should make the tubers taste better. Will not harvest till the stems are done in a few weeks.

So I finally tried what the cool kids are doing and didn’t bury my seeds, instead I sifted my potting soil to make it soft, then put the seeds on top, and covered in  perlite.

Well the results blew me away 😳 these seedlings have only been growing for 8 days!

👨‍🌾 gardening 🌱 plants 🫛 peas 🍈 melons

These are carambola (star) fruits. 
This plant grew up in an area that I didn't want it to, right in the middle of my garden path. I was so tempted to remove it, until I simply starting walking around it.
Now it's laden with fruit. 🌱

I nursed this bush back from death over the summer. The whole thing was brown sticks. I didn't even know it could flower. 

I'm bad at growing things and I have trouble maintaining the yard with my disability. And somehow, it blossomed in the fall anyway ❤️

I'm impressed with these Aunt Molly's ground cherries. They were volunteer sprouts from old berries from the plant I grew in spring, AND a few weeks ago survived a light frost with only about half the foliage being lost! 🌱 

#AuntMollysCherries #groundCherries

"Fairy Pepper" is the name I came to for this new miniature variety. Most plants grow to under six inches, but this one is a little smaller.

The pods are very hot, much like the wild pepper ancestor (Capsicum annuum var glabriusculum) that was a parent in the original cross leading to this variety.

A few years ago I bought some pawpaws (Asimina triloba) because I wanted to try growing them in the inland Northwest. Two of the four grafted trees have survived, but they are STRUGGLING. The rootstock survived on one of the dead ones and it is growing so much better than the selected stock. 😡🌱

Normal people grow alliums, and once they are done, take them out of the garden and dispose of them.

What I did was carefully take them out of there garden, spray them gold and silver, store them (again very carefully), then put them into a Christmas planter. 🌱

Cécile Brunner, the Sweetheart Rose, small but perfectly formed, like tiny Tea Roses, in abundance throughout summer. The scent is light, but nevertheless it makes the perfect buttonhole rose - which is one reason why i had to grow it. Buttonholes for the boss.

Just a light trim of the macrocarpa group. Mostly growing how I want so only the odd bar-branch removed or space made for preferred growth. Will allow some to become vigorous primary branches, others near the canopy kept slender. It's all at a very nice stage changing from soft growth to woody.🌱🇳🇿🪴

This is maybe one of the least impressive-looking feats of cultivation, but I cannot emphasise how *bad* this ground was a year ago. Clay so baked in that I would get sparks with the mattock.

🌱

Tromboncino Summer Squash! It's a zucchini-like summer squash - with a *twist* - demonstrated here by our youngest. It can grow up to three feet long, but you can harvest it at any stage before it is full size. 🌱💚

Today's Gardening Advice: This is the Iris "Cherry Garden." In person, it's a reddish-purple. Low growing and early flowering it also spreads quickly once established. Does best in full sun, but my initial planting is partly shaded. A lovely addition to the garden.🌱

I was in ATX over the weekend visiting some good friends! I went on a chilly morning run at the Lake Creek Trail and came across THESE. 

Maxamilian sunflowers are fall-time bloomers native from NM, USA to SK, CN + MO, OK & TX, USA, and have a wide naturalized distribution eastward. 🌻

🌿
Maximilian sunflower seems to love irrigated land in W Oregon. Tolerates dryland too. Pollineighbors love it though it's not locally native (it's a prairie and E coast species)

Today's Gardening Advice: Siberian Iris Paprikash is one of the stranger Siberian Iris I have. The flower color is decidedly different. Goes well with other colors in the garden. Like other Iris relatives it prefers full sun and tolerates dry when established. Not picky about soil. 🌱

Recently, I have been looking into the Late Pleistocene climate and vegetation of Argentina for a work project, and was delighted to find out that pink peppercorn was - and still is - part of the native flora in the area. Maybe Macrauchenia developed a taste for the spicy tree.

🌱 The bosque blue tomatoes are starting to ripe. The bottom of ripe tomatoes turn from greenish to a red hue. The top part remains the dark side of blue violet, about the color of eggplant. These are larger than the strawberry blk cherry tomatoes, but the taste is kind of unmemorable.

🌱 The pink bumblebee is here. You can see some pink pigmentation on this fruit but this is still mostly orange. As it ripens the color will change to more pink strains.  This was the best tasting tomato I grew last year.
It’s propagation time! My marble queen pothos was too leggy, and a piece of coleus snapped off my main plant when I moved it. They get dipped in rooting hormone, then water, and sit in the sun 🧪
Lovely late-season color from the Mexican sage. And it's drought resistant/water wise. I like the contrast with the golden calmagrostis stalks. 🌱

A rescued rose, well, sort of. The plant has had powdery mildew really badly and a regular spray of a hydrogen peroxide solution (1 part H2O2 to 3 parts water) has knocked that way back. The bloom is much paler and subtler in appearance than my phone camera can reproduce. 🌱

The new soil works! My tomatoes went from shrivelled to lush, sparse to abundant, fleeing this mortal coil to smothering my neighbourhood’s native flora. 

More than I can store. Family & friends won’t accept them. Cupboards are full. They’re in my bed. I dream tomatoes.

The vine is in charge.
My favorite part of a sunflower is its frame. It’s very muscular. That’s a lot of cells and DNA and magic holding up a 3lb head and the occasional marauding squirrel

IM Derek Jarman 

Monday 19 February 1990

I counted 77 blooms on the early daffodils I planted two years ago. They are multiplying very slowly. At the water’s edge the sea kale is sprouting. The plants have small leaves of two or three inches; they are a deep purple. 

Modern Nature —

Now that I like for some reason.

I found two pink katydid nymphs in the same spot in 2021, and haven't seen another since. That hasn't stopped me from superstitiously checking the same spot on the regular. The pink is a genetic mutation, and they're fine, other than not looking like leaves. 🌿

Although they do produce seeds, trembling aspen reproduce almost entirely by cloning. This is a great example of new clones ￼shooting up into a once open meadow from the roots of the trees behind them. 🌿
Echeveria Pollux blooming after five years. Worth the wait. Blown away by this color palette the snake rattle shape at the end of a shepherd's crook. Never seen it before. 🌱

Last year’s dwarf irises—among my very favourite plants—were a treasure. This year we learn whether they’ll perennialize in the spots I picked for them, but as insurance, we planted another fifty. There can never be too many! They take up no space; their post-bloom foliage is narrow and subtle 🌱

This 2nd year hydrangea has more than doubled in size. The flowers are all fairly low on the bush (which is where the sun hits), and there's another foot of greenery at the top, out of the picture frame, which is its way of telling me that it wants to be transplanted forward this fall. 🌱

The precious Paperwhite Narcissus grows well in Florida, as long as you give it a cooler location to live in. Being one of the few varieties that doesn’t need chill to flower, they will bloom in clusters of showy, fragrant petals. Bulbs can be planted in either soil or a rock bed.

Recovering from learning:
1) Pineapples grow from shrubs (not trees)
2) The plant is a perennial and can produce multiple fruits, each taking about a year to grow.
3) Wild pineapples are pollinated mostly by hummingbirds.
4) Of course, there's messed up colonial history of pineapple monoculture.

This is a tomato seedling in my unheated hoop house.
As long as it is under double coverage and down in the canopy it can survive outdoor temperatures down to 10° f
🌱

Today's Gardening Advice: Need a flowering tree for a small space? This is Cercis canadensis 'Covey', also sold as 'Lavender Twist.' Flowers form all up and down the stems and the weeping form reaches about 10'. Prefers full sun to partial shade.  They don't tend to do well with dry soil/hot sun. 🌱

Two of my favorite plants for low-medium (sprawling) border color for the garden, the Erigeron karvinskianus – ‘Santa Barbara Daisy’ (1’-2’) white & pink flowers. Cuphea hybrid – ‘Starfire Cuphea’ is slightly taller (2’-3’) pinkish-red, glossy foliage & attracts hummingbirds. 🌱 #FlowerReport

🌱 So.. I am having roaring success with orchids ,  these days.. 
Not in direct sunlight, plunge them in rainwater +feed, for 20 mins, every 2 weeks or so. Then let them drain. 
The end.

Books say Lucerne (Medicago sativa subsp. sativa) can be separated from other subspecies by:
Pods spiralling 2, 3 or even 4 times ✅
Flowers pale mauve to violet ✅+🟡!!
From a field margin near #Cambridge #wildflowerhour

One thing I'm especially proud of with my garden is that I grow it almost entirely with reclaimed water. 💦

I live in CA where water conservation is a big deal, and we save every bit of water we can.

I have ~20 plants, and I've only watered with the hose twice this year.

The case for planting Allium: they are stunning and hardy. They are onion-family and a natural pest deterrent. They naturalize and multiply! They bring lovely height to a garden. Bees and butterflies adore them! 🌱

🌱 A few of the happy blooms of the Mexican Zinnias for you. About a foot tall in a dry area with infrequent watering. Can more than double it's height when watered more often. Size of flowers remain small at about 2inches in diameter. A delight!

We have a ridiculous volume of California bluebell seeds and the temptation to seed-bomb with them is pretty high. Who wouldn’t love them? Good foliage, they die back completely to make room for summer blooms, and that BLUE—it’s unmatched 🌱

We've just found copious hydrangeas bushes in Batumi Botanical Garden, on the hidden slope, far from tourist roads. They are unreal vibrant colours. What colour are your favourite hydrangeas? In Batumi, we mostly meet deep blue with a few white ones
#art #watercolour

Putting creeping thyme (& other hardy groundcovers) on our little back patch of dirt, instead of grass, was great. 🌱

One-or-more of these are always blooming, most are hardy AF, bees buzz happily around and it needs maybe 1/10 the water 💦 or fertilizer. By mid-next-summer they’ll cover the gaps.
Sassafras gives us leaf buds - soft, slightly fuzzy and pungent - in Spring - it ends the Leaf Season in many shades 🌿

🌱Due to less rain and higher temperatures, I mow the grass only twice a year (also good for insects and #birds) and I planted mostly mediterranean plants and herbs (olive, thyme, sage, lavender...). They need less water and are beautiful.🍃
Btw: Sage and thyme reproduce by themselves through seeds.

With higher temperatures and less rainfall, we are forced to change the way we plant food.

This image shows a row of black eyed peas growing between 10 year old date palm trees. The area was not tilled.

I kinda thing this is how it was done before we got smart. 🌱

🌱 Dendranthema arcticum "Roseum", which I've been growing in front of my kitchen window, only ever has a rosey hue in the beginning & the end (today, pic 4) of its blooming season. Mainly on the outside of the petals. This mum is a bit of a bully so I would only plant it next to strong neighbours.

Back in the day, we grew dozens of sunflowers in the field beside our house, attracting a riot of blue jays and cardinals at this time of year. No more farmhouse, no more field, but lots of good memories are held in this vase on my table.🌱

🌱The tomato seeds* I planted 8 days ago are coming up like gang busters.

Today I'm buying a new LED shop light (4800° - 5500 lumens) as the daylight coming through the windows is just too weak.

Tomatoes & sunshine.

One grown on the Ballymaloe Cookery School organic farm and bought from their vegetable stall today. The other everywhere, all at once. This is a week for making big platters of tomato salad and soaking up that sun.

The sunchokes are blooming! For reference, the shortest yellow flowers are my height (5.5 ft). We figure the tallest stalks are about 11 or 13 feet. 🌱

One of the 'easy Sunday' plans is to plant some banana and plantain suckers on the farm.
I will plant this one as well, silk banana or some call it golden banana.
It is not the best type of sucker to plant. This is a water sucker, the better ones are sword suckers but it will still grow. 🌱

Tomato seedlings for high tunnel production. This is Kakao and Caiman.
I do not recommend these varieties for home gardens bc they both require protection to produce to their potential.
🌱
Kakao is similar to Kumato but a little better flavor and higher productivity in the hoop house.
an architectural plant, inspires monochrome, and could be grown at the back of the border in a garden. Cirsium eriophyllum, Woolly Thistle
Not a real bloom but just as striking. Cold enough this morning for this frost flower to "bloom" from the stem of a husk of a dried up flower. Beauty from decay. 🌱

Sowed 4 different peppers 🌶️ and aubergine ‘Violetta di Firenze’, they are on a heat mat set to 22 degrees C in a propagator with dome. (That’s why the picture is a bit blurry.)

We’re here to share our passion for roses, gorgeous plants and gardens. Introducing ‘Meteor’ Rose of the Year 2024. 
The golden yellow-orange petals flush vermillion-red in sunlight. Glossy, lush & healthy foliage.
Bred by Kordes.

I spent today digging up yet more strawberry runners that have rooted all over my #allotment. I've already got 3 raised beds full of strawberries so I'm going to put them in the polytunnel for some early fruit. 🌱🍓♥️

For 2 years I unsuccessfully tried to grow aubergines. Just because. This year I realised that growing them in a pot on my patio was likely to be more successful, because heat. Now I need to know what to do with aubergines 🇳🇿🌱

🌱#flowerreport #zone9B Somehow my badly- planned timing of planting this #Mandarinorange tree turned out OK *now that it has survived summer '23. In previous decades,backyard had wall to wall grass. Now, mostly growing herbs / trying to create more shade, as trees have aged out since 1970s planting.

The earliest #FlowerReport of 2024 from north central West Virginia — the first sign of daffodils. These are the naturalized ones that pop up in the field behind the rail trail where an old homestead used to be. They bloom every year in March and smell wonderful.

I've been living with orchids for many (many!) years but this is a first for me. Lovely little supermarket bargain, finished its bloom weeks ago and then surprised me with a baby keiki. It's thriving and I'm thrilled!  🌱

🌸 winged monkeyflower 🌿

usually the middle lobe on the bottom lip folds down not up, but it looks 🤔 a little more like a monkey face this way i think. close as it's liable to get anyways
🌱 I grow the thousand head kale every year because I love it's strong, large leaves and it's flowers in late winter. The bees also love it's flowers and the songbirds enjoy the seed pods.

🌱 Saying goodbye to the last of the flowering gladioli. First time growing them this year and I adore them. Just need to give them better support next year. Thinking of planting some dwarf ones given the wind we get here.
Here’s the progress of baby San Pedro cacti I planted a year ago. I tried to plant some Peyote at the same time but let’s be honest, the climate here isn’t really suitable so nothing came of it 🌱
The Desiree harvest is in. Lots of very small potatoes - I suspect effects of the very dry weather we had this summer. Should take care of us for a while. #aotearoagardening 🌱

Here's a more descriptive photo. It is difficult to find a camellia that is this tall and has beautiful flowers all the way to the edges ✨

The garden is looking very Barbiecore today 🌱

Saponaria officinalis - it can be aggressive, so it's in a raised bed with a few other rowdy herbs, and I cut it back before it goes to seed. 

The flowers have a lovely sweet clove scent that lasts a long time even after drying.
This is my favourite bit of flowering, when the colas are beautifully cone shaped bunches of white pistils and before the first start to brown. It always reminds me of echinacea 🌲

Hibiscus plants are renowned for their vibrant and showy flowers, making them a beloved addition to gardens and landscapes. Beyond their ornamental value, they have cultural, medicinal, and culinary significance, contributing to their diverse appeal and usefulness in various parts of the world.

Today's Gardening Advice: If you're prepping for spring seed planting, be sure to wash any previously used pots.  Use a cap full of bleach in a gallon of water and allow pots to soak.  Rinse them well.  Bleach bath helps remove mold and potential insect problems.  🌱

The peanut and strawberry beds are looking happy. Strawberries have started sending out runners so I filled a couple of gaps and lopped the rest off to encourage fruit.

We've had a thrush move in and it's discovered all the berries, so this year I need to net them. 🌱

Belle de Jour floribunda rose looking fabulous after overnight rain. A lovely apricot fragrance, she is full of blooms & will continue flowering until the first frost. Rose of the Year, 2021, bred by Maison Delbard.

💕HEARTSTOPPER! Who knew that a dwarf, seed-grown cultivar of Hardy Hibiscus, 'Luna™ Rose' - would be the last one standing - still popping buds into late October, as groups of 4 other vegetatively propagated varieties have already called it a day? 🍨Delicious color!
Malvaceae - Mallow Family

The Tamarisk trees are also in full flower right now. Quite a special tree, being drought and salt tolerant. I know it is invasive in some countries but at home here in Spain. 
I only noticed the heavily pollen laden bee in the background afterwards. 🐝🌱🦉

There is a scruffy little urban park on my route home from work in Christchurch that has been brightened up by a local committee, recently, and someone even planted a row of tomatoes which are now ripening 🥰

Hydrangeas, I love them. This particular plant makes me laugh. I liberally dosed it with blue colour maker aluminium sulfate but the alkaline game is strong in my soil so I have a multi- coloured bush this year. Can’t say I mind. 🤣😂🤣

🌱The purple gladiolus is not a volunteer; it got mixed up with the princess Margaret which is the yellow blooms with orange edges. This is my first time growing them. They are gorgeous but it's stalks need support, and it should be planted where it's protected from wind.

Starting the tomato planting ritual. I just sit on a chair in the advisory role at the moment.

My hibiscus plants have been champions in this terrible heat. They’re producing much smaller blooms than usual, but still doing it.

The goal with dahlias is a closed face (no yellow pollen disc showing), so you want the ones with loads of petals. These are all second year seedlings, so unlikely to get much more appealing. 🌱🇳🇿
