Thought for sure the random pumpkin & butternut squash seeds I’d saved from last year’s dinners wouldn’t germinate. 10 of the 11 grew! Moved 9 plants to spots around the property & kept 1 pumpkin in the container. No idea if they’ll produce fruit (or what exact type). But yay! 🌱
Yesterday, Today, Tomorrow (Brunfelsia pauciflora) blooming today in my garden. The blooms fade from purple to lavender to white as they age, thus the common name.  🌱

Velvet-leaf mallows are going in for a fall-time bloom! I saw this super tall one (it was taller than me!) in BEAUTIFUL bloom on the N Salado Creek Greenway while on a run this afternoon. 

Velvet-leaf mallows are easily one of my favorite native wildflowers. They bloom from May through November! 🌿

Planted more carrots today. 
... I'm using flat iron between the rows to keep the weeds down this time. Black polythene with slits in it did *not* perform well: in cyclonic winds it literally beat most of the carrot seedlings flat, so I've upgraded to solid steel mulch🦾🦾😂

Advent calendar day 17

The joyful sight of cherry blossom has always signified spring to me
We had a massive cherry tree in our back garden growing up and it's blossoms falling like snow was always heart lifting, as was it's intense almond scent
Hellebores are such great garden plants. I love the muted shades and am always amazed at the variation in the colour and form of the blooms even on the same plant 🌱

exciting accomplishment of the day is that I had saved some seeds from farmer's market produce when I had energy last summer

and I have just planted a few of those bad boys, and packed up some of the others so my mom can maybe swap them for things like tomato seeds

Got up this morning at ass o'clock & immediately tore out & replanted half my garden. I'm exhausted & feel creaky, but I'll have a very fun autumn harvest full of Hakurei turnips, red choi, spinach, lacinato kale, cabbage, radicchio, radishes, puntarelle, etc.

It’s a really hard frost out there, but oh my the Erysium (Bowles Mauve) is a hardy one! Three years old now so not sure if this is its last year, but it starts flowering back in the spring and just keeps on going like the Duracell bunny! Have a Good Friday ☺️🌱

Plants update: Got two pumpkins growing on each of the long vines, one of which has started climbing a nearby tree, two squash babies have just popped up which is exciting, and I have about 16 tomatoes between the two tomato plants, no two are the same size

Sugar Magnolia Tendril Peas! Attractive mottled pale green plants with fuschia flowers make a show in the spring garden before warmer weather crops take off. A kid approved favorite, excellent for snacking and stir-fry. The blossoms are said to symbolize happiness and success. 🌱💚

The balcony garden 🌱 is chopped, mixed, fertilized, and watered. I’ll likely be watering it weekly through the winter so the plants decompose and so I don’t have to deal with hydrophobic soil again next spring and I still need to plant my garlic but for today I’m done.

Unbelievable these tomatoes 🍅 were still growing in the veg patch a week ago, however, as green as apples. After a week in a paper bag in the cupboard under the stairs, they are now perfectly ripe and ready to enjoy.

Hello, friends! One of the highlights of the garden at the moment is a huge stand of these swamp sunflowers. They always bloom so late that we think they aren't going to bloom at all but then they go wild! Nice late pollinator and food source for wildlife. 🌱

Today's Gardening Advice:  As you get ready to set up planters for spring, remember that "Potting Mix" contains no soil. Potting soil should contain some percentage of soil.  In my experience, potting soil works better with outdoor containers as it retains moisture better.  🌱

Gorse is a wonderful hedgerow plant, it keeps all kinds of bugs safe over winter and is one the vital first foods in spring. This gorse is still damp after a rain shower, it’s so cheery flowering right through winter 🌼💛

Saturday Morning Garden Report 

A volunteer Korean melon vine is producing a melon! Paul Robeson tomato is getting larger, still very green. The volunteer cherry tomato might not be one of the tasteless hybrids - looks lt. orange, not red! And the Korean red pepper plant has lots of green babies.

Pretty unruly, but that's because she almost died this year. I grew this Maple from seed 15 years. Last winter some mice decided to munch on her bark, killed her sibling, and almost killing her 🌱 . Light pruning only this year.

i've got a classic red metal radio flyer wagon with a broken axle here and i'm thinking if i knock some holes in the bottom i could use it as a planter but obviously it would have to be something with quite shallow roots and drought tolerant as i imagine it will dry out quick in the sun. ideas? 🌱

Todays 🌱 harvest is red and green-red.

The lucky tiger tomatoes are very sweet, even though they aren’t very red. Great taste, warmed by the sun.

Served with mozzarella and basil (we have an „African Basil“, green/purple, a lot more taste than the light green one) and Balsamico.

🌱 I am mesmerized by the absurd difference between the paprika seedling on the right (bit leggy, but healthy and developing its first set of true leaves), and the strawberry on the left - which also carries its first true leaf, but look how TINY it is, and THIN! 

I always fear every single breeze😶

Primroses also kicking off in Rakesden, along with yesterday's hellebores. Spring is definitely starting to spring, and with buds showing green on this and that, pruning has already started--a bunch of St John's worts getting hard pruned today to clear off all last year's dead leaves and berries. 🌱

Erythrina sykesii (Australian Coral/Flame tree) can make the perfect focal point in one’s garden. They are just waking up (semi-deciduous) now and starting to bloom in Southern California. USDA zones 9 and up. “Look deep into nature, and you will understand everything better.” – Albert Einstein 🌱

Today's Gardening Advice: This is another favorite Iris of mine. It's called Loreley and is from 1909. It has grown rapidly for me. Likes full sun and average garden soil (add compost when planting, fertilizer in spring). Yellow parts of flower often have purple streaks.🌱
I knew in theory that pansies (violas, Johnny-jump-ups) are perennial and very hardy but it’s a whole other thing to see them blooming in my yard after several -8C overnights without snow cover 🌱

Hello, friends! The swamp sunflowers are finally blooming! They are a very welcome late burst of color and attraction for pollinators and songbirds. We neglected to cut them back a bit so they are mostly well over our heads. Tall fall sentinels!🌱

Maybe these 🌲 would have been bigger in bigger pots with added fertilizer and lots of work and care. But they are just fine by my estimates. They spent their lives in these same pots full of cheapo potting soil and received only normal tap water. Are all of the fancy soils and equipment necessary?

This year has been APPALLING for growing chili 🌱s without a greenhouse in Denmark, but though this is only my first harvest of gochu (i.e. real Korean pepper strains), I've some hope of getting at least an equal second harvest before we hold a kimjang (communal kimchi making) in a couple of weeks.

Harvested my Crapaudine beets today. It was an experiment...I tend not to have a ton of luck with beets because we have heavy soil. I had half and half luck with these-- maybe six or seven good sized beets and a bunch of small ones. I have a ton of greens, though, so that's exciting!

We generally grow quite a few varieties of chilli peppers, but not so much this year. This time we limited to a few species including these freshly picked Thai Chiang Rai chilli peppers. They produce a medium heat and taste quite good with Asian dishes.

Christensonia vietnamica  /  กุหลาบเวียดนาม (Vietnam Rose)
Family: Orchidaceae
Tribe: Vanda
Subtribe: Aeridinae

This species was only identified & described in 1993 from samples taken in the forests of Vietnam. It's the only species of its genus. The flowers are quite small (<1" diam.).

📍Thailand 🌱

Adelaide d'Orleans - a Sempervirens hybrid, dainty, hanging sprays of bloom - so elegant and with a myrrh scent. (1826, Jacques, France). I needed absolutely no encouragement or special reason to grow this one - her sheer beauty was quite enough.

Swamp cypress seedlings have done well this season. All over 30cm. Have enough for a nice forest. So tempting to put them together now but I'll see if I can develop them for a few years.
🌱🇳🇿🪴
🌳✂️🌲

My native blue grama lawn is getting well established now. I started it with seed last Fall, & it’s filling in the bare patches using stolons & seeds. You can see the pretty blond seedheads in the 2nd picture. 🌱

🌱The Mexican sunflowers are just starting to bloom next to the lavender at the neighbor's farm. The flowers are small like daisies, and usually abundant. Not as tall as the perennial sunflowers I'm growing the last two years.
Rose of Sharon sure is pretty but the fact that this one is growing from the center of a dense hedge should tell you to think real hard before planting one 🌱

I’m in zone 8b and it’s almost January but look at my alyssum! Everything else has given up the ghost but it is absolutely thriving!

I will grow from seed from now on, all the nursery alyssum is long dead. 🌱
One of the biggest sunflowers on my balcony. You can see that the pattern of seeds within the sunflower follows the Fibonacci sequence.

Garden report 🌱
It’s either pouring rain or 100 degree heat index here in Nash. The basil went from beautiful to black overnight, squash bugs finally got the cucumbers, jalapeños are tiny, tomatillos huge but scarce. Tomatoes are surprisingly fine but must ripen indoors?
Mostly growing mosquitos

last year I started 20+ plant species native to Arkansas in one of our library garden's raised beds, my mini meadow experiment

this spring so many little green guys are already waking up, look at em grow!

Corn plant propagation going well now that I've added an aquarium pump to the vases. Propagation station looking healthy. The schefflera, on the other hand, is getting cramped. Should have pruned it months ago. 🌱

I love Love LOVE black flowers. They are my hands-down favorite.

This one needed a bit more sun, or maybe it's just a later bloomer, which I've heard from others growing this unfortunately-named variety (Fidalgo Blacky).

It's a nice size and shape for arrangements, though.

When we moved in the front garden was a sort of boggy grass that never thrived. Last year I dug it all out, sank some old log slices that were free off FB market place, and planted up the whole thing. It's quite small this year, but I'm hoping next year will be amazing. You really don't need grass 🌱

Mirabelle plum tree - over endowed with blossoms. ( oops pruning fail).
Delighting the bees in this break between showers.
Serra Sao Mamede, Portugal.

Looks like a low growing bush outside of my house is actually a “winter rose” camellia?  Hasn’t bloomed EVER in the 11 years I’ve owned this house - until this year.

I found this flowering with 2 more buds to come hopefully. What I like about Paphs & Phrags is that they are less fussy about hard water.

Friendly suggestion, if you're not already, to harvest the moment your tomatoes start to get some colour. Especially now because the critters are getting extra hungry with the weather cooling down 🌱🥲

We usually have long springs here, so I would normally start my "warm weather" seedlings in March -- but I'm going to start some signet marigolds this afternoon.

I grew these adorable marigolds for the first time last year, and love them! Lots of tiny, brilliant flowers.

More in plants gave me moments of joy today: the fall sedum is dripping in pollinators, my domino cactus is pushing a bloom, and one of our infrequently mowed, weeded for invasive, but otherwise untouched areas is filled with hoary aster blooms.

I haven't weeded in my first year of my native plant bed and this is why. Every plant ID app told me in the early stages that this was horse weed, an annual that I hadn't planted. But now that it's more mature, it's easily identified as the goldenrod seeds I did plant.

Just gotta give it time 🌱

My peppers and eggplants sprouted!!! This gives me so much joy. I had to get a grow tent so these would sprout in the basement (they need 70+ degree temps to germinate). My tomatoes germed last year w/just heat mat, but got stunted at 2 inches. Ambient heat just wasn't enough. Hopefully tent helps

My other half got this “Waxed Amaryllis” at Trader Joe’s. It grew as promised & is in full bloom. It's lovely, but I hate “disposable” plants. I’m going to try to liberate it from its plastic jacket, get it into a pot, & save it. #Hippeastrum ‘Christmas Gift’ #Amaryllidaceae 🌱
A clematis! My vine very rarely produces flowers, and the fact that it got one this year suggests the solution is simply to water it more, not whine about how it gets very little light. 🌱

My mom gave me a cutting from her Christmas cactus years ago. We had it for a couple years, and it barely did anything. In 2021, I moved it into a window with better light, and it started growing like crazy. This is the second year it's flowered, and it makes me so happy to see :)

My nasturtiums, which had done nothing for the most part since I first planted them in May, are suddenly going wild now that it's nearly November. Climate change, man. Everything is weird now. (Bonus Harvey in this pic because it's his morning meditations spot.) 🌱
We don't often get good blooms any longer on this camellia, owing to camellia petal blight. Camellia 'Mimosa Jury', one of the prettiest Mark's dad, Felix Jury, bred and he named it for his wife.
Well the pollinators did their work on the magnificent display of hawthorn blossom in spring and now the spread of haws is phenomenal - on Devil's Dyke, Sussex

What a weird year we are having for gardens in Victoria. My place gets a lot of wind and only afternoon sun. But the strawberries are finally coming in — I have never had this many blossoms in my 3 years here.

And my “saucy Mary” green Roma tomatoes are my surprise performers. I need to stake it! 🌱

My toddler loves cherry tomatoes so much that she doesn't let them get fully ripe.  As soon as they're red enough, she demands her tomato tribute.

I have only eaten three cherry tomatoes that were fully ripe the whole season so far.

I think this is a sign to plant more next year. 🌱

Two years ago when we moved here, this hydrangea was one single branch with a couple leaves. I thought it would not make it. The following summer, it had a tiny bloom. This year it has exploded in size and blossomed beautifully. We will have to move it this fall as it is crowding the gate 🌱

Had a literal field day with strawberry growers near Charlotte NC!

NC grows the 3rd largest strawberry crop in the US after CA & FL. But unlike those states, most NC strawberries come from small farms.

Gangly wild August garden. 🌱 Butternut squash ripening on the vines just everywhere. Tomatoes fully escaped all the supports I can think of. Watermelon hanging on. A couple of happy pumpkins. Not bad for year one.

I succumbed to Black Friday and got myself more Greenstalk self-watering planters. Highly recommended if you are short on space or have mobility issues. I grew a ton of tomatoes, beans and herbs in mine and want to expand.

Amaryllis doing a bloom! Looks very red there but it’s that amaryllis coral shade that’s the exact midpoint between red, orange and pink, and looks different colours in different lights. Love the patterning on the petals 🌱

Been amazed at how prolific these modest size pepper plants have been this year. I pick all the fruit and a week or so later they’re back. I dont feed them or anything just water regularly.

Got the onion, leek, celery, and some pansy/viola seeds started today. All tucked into LED light tower. Hoping we get a warmer, earlier #Spring this year so I can move them out to the greenhouse. We had so many cold nights in March last year my living room was a disaster. #Gardening #Minneapolis 🌱

Planted a Little Lemon clematis in a tall pit along a sunny hog wire fence. 🌱 I’ve tried a few annual vibes here without good results, so I’m at peace with the fact that this is a non-vining clematis (learned after planting).

🌱 In #syracuse #ny this forsythia bush is blooming in my neighbor’s yard. It should bloom in the spring, so this is wicked early.   Flowers blooming out of season is a bad omen. For example, spring flowers blooming in the winter means the temperatures are too warm & that has lots of ramifications.

I loved this combination so much last year that my big replanning of this garden bed for 2024 is focused on centering the Allium Millenium, Royal Raspberry Agastache, Salvia Blue Hill, and (not pictured) Blue Paradise Phlox as the main players with the best sun exposure. 🌱
Lady Banks Rose, 80+ years old, an heirloom. Aggressive climber, grows up to 20 feet tall. It keeps trying to take over the house. #gardening 🌱

One of the easiest and most fuss-free varieties of cauliflower we have grown! Medium sized heads with kid-friendly mild flavor. Both heat and cold tolerant, though afternoon shade is recommended for hot southern climates. 🌱💚
(link in comments)
Grew radishes in hanging baskets this year and let some go to seed. Was rather impressed with the majestic boughs vibe they went with 🌱

New blossoms are starting to open up! I wasn't sure what to expect when I brought this baby home last month, it was in the discount section, looking a bit yellowed and haggard. It has perked up so much! greened right up, and is now busy filling my home with the scent meyer lemon blossoms 🌱

Two more teeny tiny shishito peppers and the first sungold cherry tomato! The sungolds may be my absolute fave thing to grow because they're so tasty. I'd hoped more for the shishitos but the plants are out there flowering like crazy and living their best life so I can't be mad. 🌱

I planted three lots of beans. Dwarf beans from old seeds that I wasn’t sure would sprout, and runner beans & dwarf beans that @@164020 gave to me. The old beans I had don’t seem to be sprouting, so far at least but they are doing a good job of growing fungi. 🌱 🇳🇿

aka limestone bittercress, appropriate as they like calcareous soil. these were growing on the moist bank of a creek running over bare bedrock up in the hills

Second round of raspberries has been pretty mediocre, but the strawberries don’t seem to care that it’s getting cold and wet! Here’s hoping the peppers get a move on here soon though because that’s just the first two ripe on a bush with about sixty 🌱🌶️

Hey 🌱 team, this is the variety of Shasta daisies that I have. While they are great in bloom, this variety only seems to produce over bloom a year for me. Deadheading doesn't generate a new bloom. Any recommendations on ones that rebloom throughout the season?

Chilli peppers move through a range of colours as they ripen on the vine. As long as the fruit remains solid it is safe to say it is healthy and on its way… 🌱

kitchen-garden.be/diary/summer...
I've been watching this group of fawn lilies at my local park for the last ten years since it was just a dozen plants or so and now it's a glorious patch of native in spring flowers.
I'm on holiday in the Bayrische Wald, which had one of the coldest summers in Germany in average. Since around five years my host grows oranges, peppers, figs and passionfruits. 🌱
The first narcissus are out on the kitchen windowsill. The cheerful tête à tête - a miniature daffodil - and a little pot of crocus Orange Monarch is on the way. 🌱
Goldenrod might be literally my favorite flower. The biggest highlight of the cold months for me. It literally smells like honey.
I cut all the thriving nasturtium blossoms and a few buds & seed pods from my monster success plant to bring in ahead of the predicted freeze 🌱 I told it it did an amazing job and I would miss it.

Cardinal Basil! 
It's pretty, it smells amazing, and of course it's delicious! Patio/balcony gardeners take note: this one is great for container planting! Basil is often seen as a symbol of love and protection, and it is also said to grow in the presence of great heroism. 🌱💚
my nasturtiums are so much happier this year than last. i didn’t even know they could climb, and look how high they got up the clematis trellis 🌱
The most sci-fi of flowers, Passiflora incarnata (purple passionflower). Native in the US from Pennsylvania south down to Florida and west to Texas. 🌱

Throwback to my first mini garden. I planted with no knowledge and ended up with mostly diseased crops 💀 I learned so much from trial/error alone but do some research before throwing seeds around! I'm excited to take what I have learned by ACTUALLY studying and make something magical this year. 🌱
My tomato plants didn't do a damn thing this year so apparently the apple trees decided to make up for it. This is from one of four trees, just from today. Halp.  🌱
The first year for my bike garage roof garden. 4 potato plants had great foliage & a really nice haul of potatoes.

I'm so glad I grew some melons in the greenhouse this year. 🌱 I've gotten multiple ripe melons from the greenhouse, which was such a striking comparison this morning, comparing the most developed melon out in the garden against this morning's breakfast.

🌱 I planted some apricotta and xsenia cosmos this summer. I think this one is the xsenia but it looks darker than the photo in the seed packet. I love it anyway, so will be collecting seeds for planting next year.

🌱To play the long game I also bought some Amaryllis seeds. The seller described them as, "xjohnsonii x La Paz/ cybister". Since I have a real love for cybister Amaryllis, but hate their prices, I thought I'd throw the dice and grow some from seeds.
My coleus plant flowered before the frost killed it and I was able to harvest some seeds. They're already growing nicely, though I didn't space them out very well. The seeds were so tiny! 🌱
I let this cucumber get huge to harvest its seeds to grow next year. It did so well. Almost 20 cucumbers from one plant!

Bought a couple of 4" pots of Phlox stolonifera (which are impossible to find as seeds, which usually means they're practically impossible to grow from seed), one of the creeping phloxes, this spring, and they seem to like where I put them. Maybe they're happy enough to give me flowers next year. 🌱

I had really nice success growing pumpkins and squash last year, I would like to do it again next year and grow even more this time, here's the thing though, last year I grew like eight plants and they completely took over my entire property.
Todays harvest has HEFT. I thought my courgette plants were done because it’s been raining every day for a week and they look like shit bit NOPE. They persevere.
Wandering through spongy, nodding, star-spangled fields of snowdrops and winter aconites—some of the earliest flowers to bloom in temperate zones

🌱 I sat out in the glorious sunshine today. It was so good for the soul. I also planted my tomato, chilli, capsicum, & lettuce seeds into punnets and put them in my mini greenhouse. I am hoping for a good uptake this year. 

The tomato, chilli & capsicum seeds are all saved from last year's crops.

Some wildflowers need a period of freezing temps in order to sprout in spring. We plant ours in fall and let nature take care of the rest. Our Rocket Larkspur is a little confused by the warm weather! 🌱💚 #gardening
Growing sunflower microgreens from a 5lb bag of birdseed has so far been a roaring success, here’s one of several plastic takeout containers I’ve been sprouting seeds in, after ~6 days of growth.

Not too much color in the garden currently so on a trip to get milkweed, I couldn't resist some African daisies, pink guara, and a provence lavender. I hope the butterflies enjoy the milkweed among the mistplant this year.

drying & collecting tomato seeds is very simple. u just take them out of the fruit, put them on a plate & leave them on the side for a day or so to dry. after that u scrape them off with a blunt knife or a fingernail, & tip them into lil envelopes & u have seeds ready for the next few years!

from @@259481

"WVU is...the state’s major land-grant institution as well as its public flagship. While closures, cutbacks, and hiring freezes have become routine, the spectacle of a state giving up on its flagship university—for that is what these cuts amount to—is something new."

Turning most faculty into adjuncts (which never bugged the tenured class) was the wedge that creeps like Gordon Gee and Ben Sasse are using to gut and financialize the rest of the university. It always goes back to how the bosses handle Labor. There's a reason every institution hates unions.

My research team's newest article is out in Educational Researcher. We examined the extent to which different state funding models are associated with college access and success. Short answer: not much, which aligns with research on performance funding.

“Low Major Enrollment” is the most grindingly obnoxious budgetary MacGuffin imaginable. Professors can be teaching 4-5 sections of courses in their field with every seat filled for years and it doesn’t necessarily mean students are *majoring* in that discipline.

The real story, a buried lede: "Higher education funding in West Virginia has dropped by about 24 percent, or $146 million, over the past decade"
Slashing Its Budget, West Virginia University Asks, What Is Essential?

INVASION OF THE BODY SNATCHERS (1978) is not only the best film adaptation of the story, it's also one of the most frightening sci-fi movies ever made. A quiet apocalypse, where the end is written on expressionless faces, background shadows, and whispered conspiracies. A horror masterpiece.

Dangerous Encounters of the First Kind (1980 dir. Tsui Hark). Hong Kong Noir Wave classic comes off like the slimiest, most ruthless poliziotteschi Italy never made. A furious death-scream for a colonized enclave that no longer recognizes itself. All warnings apply. (Those poor mice 😬)

I WALK ALONE is worth seeing for its blockbuster cast alone, but the gorgeous photography and high growing tension ain’t too shabby. Last night I watched the intoxicatingly *strange* adaptation of Muriel Spark's THE DRIVER'S SEAT (1974). Absolute weird banger. Liz Taylor drifts around Rome encountering political violence, lecherous men and uncanny retail rituals. Woozy amber hues shot by the great Vittorio Storaro, mmm...

Tonight I saw St Drogo. It’s low-budget, queer, slow-burn, folk horror. I loved the cinematography, and the interesting critical takes on status & community. I also think there’s a lot going on about the nature of desire, and the diverse ways it’s regulated by religion, money, and social groups.

DEATH MACHINE (1994); endearingly loving tribute to the 80s genre flick, loosing a robotic ALIEN into a BLADE RUNNER-esque megacorp HQ during a DIE HARD-style heist. Brad Dourif delightful as an evil tech nerd incel. Stretches its low budget beautifully across its smoky, steely cyberpunk surface.

Bringing Out The Dead doesn't get its due, both as a Scorsese film and as a film capturing the spirit of the late 90s, with possibly Nicholas Cage's best performance. 

It's surprisingly gothic in atmosphere and manages to capture Scorsese's twin passions of New York grime and human spirituality.

the amazing mr x is a twisty, highly-atmospheric horror/thriller from eagle-lion films about a phony spiritualist. it’s pretty great… pretty low budget but the photography is beautiful and the script is tight and clever. shades of the uninvited and nightmare alley….this one is legit. check it out. 📽️

MOSS ROSE is deeply, deliciously weird. Love even more that we are supposed to root for her and want her scheme to succeed.

Just rewatched Week-End in Havana (1941), which is getting to be one of my comfort movies. Uncle Peckerhead is one of the best, most heartfelt / punk horror comedies in years, with really great original music, and i feel like a lot of people didn't give it a chance because it has an odd name. You should watch it!

watch Ravenous (1999) for a film that combines a sharp critique of manifest destiny, a suggestion that cannibalism somehow gives you super powers, deep homoeroticism and one of the wildest tones you've ever seen - all in one really weird movie

The Thing. 1982. dir: John Carpenter

There’s a LOT to like about this film: the ensemble cast, Carpenter’s direction, Ennio Morricone’s score, Rob Bottin’s astounding physical effects work, the tense paranoia among the crew, and Dean Cundey’s exceptional cinematography.

Serie Noire (1979)

A superb, bleakly hilarious film noir about a door-to-door salesman who is lured into crime by a teenage prostitute.  Based on the Jim Thompson novel A Hell of a Woman.  

Directed by Alain Corneau

Screenplay by Corneau and Georges Perec, who was BOTD in 1936

A few years ago, I came upon an excellent and suspenseful British hidden gem called THE APPPOINTMENT (1982). There was no hope for a re-release back then, but BFI did the impossible. Wrote some words about the film at Bloody Disgusting.

Didn't know what to expect from Doctor X (1932) but I was not prepared for a movie this luscious, creepy and funny. A two-toned marvel full of gloomy Gothic settings and Art Deco mad science in stunning, sickly Technicolor. A blast from Michael Curtiz (Casablanca!) in one of his rare horror forays.
Poor Things is enchantingly weird, existential, even hilarious. A visual feast, and a fucking delight. Brilliant! I loved it 🖤

A shockingly violent film, but also a very stylish/thrilling one. I won't even begin to pretend that this flick is in anything but bad taste.  But bad taste is what fans of a film like this want, and Takashi Harada grasps how far he can push the limits without descending into out and out vileness.

THEY CLONED TYRONE - provocative, satirical sci-fi with an exceptional cast that blends Blaxploitation and absurdist humor. Wildly entertaining and dripping with style and social commentary. A mix of THEY LIVE, GROUNDHOG'S DAY, and US. Boyega, Foxx, and Parris are FANTASTIC.

Fifty years today, arguably Ray Harryhausen’s magnum opus, and certainly one of my seminal texts, received its London release. A truly astonishing cast, a hugely evocative Miklós Rózsa score, an inventive screenplay, and some of cinema’s most monstrously beautiful creatures!

Just watched Cry of the City (1948), a terrific noir film with Richard Conte as an escaped murderer trying to escape the city. Fast paced, hard dialogue, and exceedingly good performances by Conte and Victor Mature. Highly recommended for noir aficionados.

Palm Springs (2020) really impressed me. It's not the greatest film ever, but it is one of those rare movies that quickly established its tone and expectations before accomplishing exactly what it set out to accomplish in a tight 90 minutes. Plus, it was fun! 📽️

A film that’s haunted me since it’s release in 1990 is Jacob's Ladder. Vietnam vet Tim Robbins sees demons wherever he goes & has no idea why. Watched a couple different rom-coms last night, My Best Girl from 1927 and No Hard Feelings from 2023. The formula  stays the same over a century — two mismatched people come together over a lie, they have some crazy fun, they fall for each other, the lie is revealed, but they get a happy ending.

Another one I love so very much that, like Ravenous, has finally started to get the love and attention it so richly deserves is Pontypool. It's such an odd little film. Shot almost entirely in a single location, with much if not all of the action taking place outside, offscreen. But it is so *TENSE*

"Talk To Me" on Netflix is a cracking little horror movie. I went into it thinking it was going to be the usual thing - bunch of pretty American teenagers running and screaming. It's Australian and SO much better than that. Actually pretty scary. Loved the ending.

SALTBURN. Starts as A SEPARATE PEACE, becomes THE GREAT GATSBY meets CALL ME BY MY NAME, then becomes something so dark and sinister it’s very much its own thing. Sharply hilarious and pitch black to the core. Critics will not be able to stop talking about this all awards season. LOVED IT.

THE EXORCIST hasn’t lost a shred of the sheer power it held over me as a kid. Horrific, vicious animal of a horror film that claws and bites at the soul. Will never lose its place as one of if not THE scariest films ever made

MARTIN (1977) is the strangest film of Romero's storied career. Of all the damn things, I'm reminded of MEMENTO, with an unreliable narrator explaining how the vampire world works over the phone to an unseen voice while mysteries unfold in a mix of b&w and color. One of my fav vampire films.

Watched Švankmajer's FAUST (1994) again. Probably my favourite of his Surrealist delights. Rabbit hole layers of wonderful strangeness and endless tactile invention. The lifesize puppets trotting around are never not very funny. One of the best screen devils. Proper magic of cinema, baby...

A strong recommendation for 'Poker Face', Rian 'Knives Out' Johnson's TV series which partially answers that question about whether Natasha Lyonne would make a good Columbo. Each episode is a compact movie and episode 9, 'Escape from Shit Mountain', is especially cinematic. Not bleak! Not grey! 📺📽

Still thinking about last night’s viewing, THE LEGEND OF HELL HOUSE (1973). Loved it. For me it worked as an occult heist film: putting together the crew, scoping out the job, dealing with tensions in the gang etc. Apparently the Richard Matheson source novel goes harder, going to have to read it

The Gorgon (1964)

A beautiful, tragic Hammer horror movie about a strange series of murders in which all the victims have turned to stone

Starring Peter Cushing, Christopher Lee, and Barbara Shelley

Directed by Terence Fisher, BOTD in 1904

Putting this here in the hidden opinion vault: Authentic cut of Twixt is my favorite Coppola film. Feels like someone reversed the wiring on a guy maddin homage to PS2 survival horror cut scenes, seems like it should be nothing but archness but it is blisteringly, painfully earnest. Just incredible.

MIDSOMMAR (2019)
Dir. Ari Aster
United States, Sweden
Horror, Drama, Cult

A grieving woman accompanies her boyfriend and his grad-school colleagues to a remote Swedish village's fabled mid-summer festival, only to discover that the idyllic commune isn't what it appears to be.

Ed Lachman’s cinematography for EL CONDE is mesmerizing: its exquisite, uncanny abstractions of shadow and light are the film’s main artistic triumph, at times genuinely evocative of Dreyer and Murnau in its visual and spiritual depth. Sumptuous, awe-striking work.

Demon Queen (1987, Donald Farmer). Hypnotic, near-plotless SOV full of wicked vibes and droney synthscapes. Incredible banger soundtrack by Jan Haflin that sounds like Patrick Cowley (in both his italo/NRG and porn soundtrack modes). 50 minutes of magic. Hell yeah hell yeah

My god, Cronenberg's THE FLY is so profoundly sad. It could've been a cheap shocker, but Jeff Goldblum portrays a character that we *genuinely* care for. If you've ever had a loved one with a degenerative disease, this hits like a sledgehammer.

A recent online poll judged Monty Python's Holy Grail to be the best film of the 70s. Considering that decade gave us the first two Godfathers, Jaws, Star Wars, Dog Day Afternoon, and Chinatown (to name but a few), I'm not persuaded we have 20/20 hindsight yet. Even Rocky Horror holds up better.

Quentin Dupieux’s Smoking Causes Coughing gave me some of my hardest laughs of the year. Deeply silly, kinda lovely, best environmental message movie this year. Power Rangers via Tales From the Crypt. Great stuff.

Thinking about The Archers (as every film buff does) I just realized that THE RED SHOES and BLACK NARCISSUS are fundamentally horror films that happen to be family-friendly. Especially BLACK NARCISSUS. What a weird, astonishing Technicolour nightmare!

Rewatched Peter Weir’s MASTER AND COMMANDER: THE FAR SIDE OF THE WORLD on the big screen in 35mm at Karlovy Vary International Film Festival and I came so near to weeping for the kind of epic cinema we’re never gonna get again. A masterpiece.

Rewatched GLENGARRY GLEN ROSS last night. It’s a truly a prime example of how you can drop an audience in the middle of something, give them almost no exposition, and not only will they figure things out—but it will be damn compelling.

I will die on the hill that 1988's remake of The Blob is as amazing and important as Carpenter's The Thing when it comes to its practical effects. The garbage disposal guy? The PHONE BOOTH DEATH?? Ugh, it's so good. Plus, the lesser Dillon and his glorious mullet.

Breaking news in two things can be true: IT FOLLOWS is a brilliant, formally ingenious horror movie that absolutely did not cry out for a sequel; and I will be first in line to see what David Robert Mitchell does with a second shot at the premise.

22. BROTHERHOOD OF THE WOLF (2001) A dizzying mélange of genres—kung fu, romantic period piece, horror—and themes—reason vs superstition, anti and also pro papal/monarchy/colonialism—induces whiplash. Also, one of the most mindboggling dissolves in history.
#31HorrorFilms31Days

Richard Jenkins - 📽️ Flirting with Disaster (David O. Russell, 1996)

Jenkins is such a riot in this road trip comedy. He and Josh Brolin play ATF agents and squabbling lovers who pursue Ben Stiller across the United States. At one point he ingests acid and is hysterical without overplaying anything.

There are two types of hetero relationships: one where Jimmy Stewart succumbs to a paranoid delusion and (maybe) pushes Kim Novak off a bell tower a la Vertigo (1958), the other is Kim Novak giving up witchcraft to be with Jimmy Stewart as seen in Bell, Book and Candle (1958).

Daisy Doodad's Dial (1914)

A silent comedy short about a face-pulling contest.  Florence Turner, who directed and stars, puts on a tour de force exhibition of facial contorting.  Turner was BOTD in 1885.  She's historically noteworthy as one of the first movie stars.

It was understandably overshadowed last weekend but I just saw Cobweb and thought it ruled. Super atmospheric, looks great, genuinely kinda scary and has a goofy Malignant-like sensibility to it. Really fun movie.

The Florida Project (2017) This accurately delves into the lives of people who live in motels long term. Excellent storytelling. Brooklynn & Bria were so transcendent you’d think they were actually mother & daughter. 2 hours of well saturated Floridian landscape & hard reality. Stunning. #filmsky

Alone in the Dark (1982)

A stone cold early 80s horror classic.  Not unlike The Dream Team (1989) if Michael Keaton and co. were all homicidal maniacs who wanted to kill their doctor.

Directed by Jack Sholder

Starring Martin Landau, Donald Pleasence, and Jack Palance, who was BOTD in 1919

One of those films where so much effort and detail is put into convincing you a character is an evil murderer that it makes no sense when he turns out to be a righteous dude. Still, a pretty swell noir. Though he initially seems miscast, Mitchum is great as a milquetoast salesman Hunter once spurned

Rewatch Evil Dead II and then marvel at the brilliance of Raimi’s direction and Campbell’s physical comedy. What makes the movie so sublime is its arch sense of self-awareness — rooted entirely in a focus on the interior. Wrote a whole article about it last year in @@19757:

JOHNNY GUNMAN heavily borrows its melodrama from 50s’ juvenile delinquency films, giving this one-night-in-NYC gangster noir a distinctive attitude of romantic nihilism. A low budget & raw performances add rather than detract from its air of authenticity: Art Ford shoulda made more! #Noirvember

learning that Konaka wrote Evil Dead Trap 2 is v. unsurprising, "what if the lain guy wrote a giallo" is the films whole deal. televised grotesquerie, memetic paranoia, predatory cults, the lurking industrial under the sheen of the misogynist capitalist neon-soaked no-future- *the* Lost Decade movie

Clive Owen - 📽️ Croupier (Mike Hodges, 1999)

A star is born in this one, where Owen takes the lead as a real fuckin’ cool casino croupier and aspiring author caught in a suave noirish crime web. I’m a huge Owen fan, and here he’s one of the few people who makes me love narration in modern film.

I WAKE UP SCREAMING (1941)

No one actually wakes up screaming but otherwise this is a top notch noir about the murder of an actress.  

Directed by H. Bruce Humberstone

Starring Victor Mature, Laird Cregar (one of the great actor's best performances), and Betty Grable, who was BOTD in 1916.

THE BICYCLE THIEF (1948)
Dir. Vittorio De Sica
Italy
Drama

Unemployed Antonio is elated when he finally finds work hanging posters around war-torn Rome. However on his first day, his bicycle — essential to his work — gets stolen. His job is doomed unless he can find the thief.

I hadn’t known Peter Gabriel did the score for THE LAST TEMPTATION OF CHRIST. Scorsese & Schrader craft an interesting rumination on the internal, eternal push-pull between man’s wants & his spiritual needs/ calling. Willem Dafoe gives a incredibly compelling performance.

This will sound like a joke but it’s not: the Milli Vanilli documentary is absolutely fucking riveting. 

Having lived through that era I knew most of the details of the story, but the film is an amazing deep-dive into what seemed like little more than a punchline at the time. Highly recommended.

Last night's watch, the Criterion Collection's restored  ISLAND OF LOST SOULS (1932). I've only seen the butchered, sanitized version that was on tv. 

HG Wells hated it, but it's a disturbing film, and Charles Laughton is magnificent as the malevolent Dr. Moreau.

James Mason - 📽️ Odd Man Out (Carol Reed, 1947)

Noir setups don’t get much leaner than watching Mason as an IRA man hiding from police, but the Belfast setting and Reed’s English sensibilities make it distinct from the American stuff. Mason is one of the all-timers, and this is close to his best.

Just watched The Sky’s the Limit (1943), loved it. Kind of a trifle, plot wise, but Fred gets to do some dramatic acting, and he does it well. Joan Leslie is fine but this is almost like a solo Astaire movie. The scene where he’s drunk and dancing through a bar destroying things is just marvelous.

n(r)w: “The Haunting” (1963)

Something to fold clothes to. IMO this flick is one of the all-time horror films. Its lushness B&W framing, its use of sound, its psychosexual underpinnings. Robert Wise putting to use everything he learned at RKO, working off arguably the best of all horror novels.

Ida Lupino was impressed enough by Peggy Dow’s work in “Woman in Hiding” that she tried to get Dow on loan out to star in her production, “Outrage” (1950). Universal-International and Howard Hughes couldn’t agree on terms and Lupino eventually cast Mala Powers. 

#NoirAlley

This week in 1982, E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial, Blade Runner, Poltergeist, John Carpenter’s The Thing, Star Trek II: The Wrath of Khan, Rocky III, and Conan The Barbarian were all playing in movie theaters AT THE SAME TIME. 🤯
Saw Poor Things, which is either a radical masterpiece or an offensive trainwreck, and I'll be able to decide which in about five years.

How can you not smile after something like Roger Corman’s A BUCKET OF BLOOD? Dick Miller plays a timid busboy whose thriving art career is born of murder. Deviously funny sense of humor. Julian Burton is captivating as the esteemed art snob.

psycho iii (1986, perkins) 

loved this so much. disorienting use sound design & burwell's score. diana scarwid's performance is so molecular it doesn't even feel like acting, making her plight that much more uncomfortable against the cruel eccentricities perkins deploys throughout

Thief (1981) is so much better than Heat (1995), not only for omitting the disgusting and unnecessary Waingro sequences—scenes whose only function is "proving the bad guys are bad" and inevitably the proof is violence against working women, but I think it has a different view of commitment

enraptured by the lurid pink/green two-tone technicolor palette of Doctor X (1932).

directed by Michael Curtiz (Casablanca), this pre-code mad scientist horror movie was so clearly one of the many inspirations behind the Rocky Horror Picture Show.

Europa (1991)

Lars von Trier's playful, visually spectacular experimental thriller.  Remains his best film in my book.

Cinematography by Edward Klosinski, BOTD in 1943

Watched Hellboy (2004) tonight and surprised at how well it holds up, in it's clear storytelling & effects. It's nearly 20 years old, but Del Toro's choices with the effects were really good ones, hardly any uncanny shocks. Can't say that for some big budget movies half that age. Recommend.

Also watched Talk To Me, which was excellent. Really creative and dread-inducing small scale horror. Performances were outstanding and the ghost scenes are grotesquely creative. It’s an A24 movie, so you know you’re getting something unusual and high quality.

wrote about Bottoms, a very weird and silly movie about two high school girls who start a fight club to try to hook up with cheerleaders

there are explosions, plural

Marshawn Lynch plays their teacher

it's the funniest movie i've seen in a long time

uproxx.com/movies/botto...

Ladies and Gentleman, the Fabulous Stains (1982)

An entertaining but caustic punksploitation film about three teenage girls forming a band and going on tour.  

Directed by Lou Adler

Starring Diane Lane, BOTD in 1965

It's a great night to watch Lifeforce (1985), dir. by Tobe Hooper (Texas Chainsaw), written by Dan O'Bannon (Alien), based on the book The Space Vampires, has an unforgettable performance from Patrick Stewart, is a zombie apocalypse film and a love story, and happens to be perfect.

Just watched Lovely, Dark, and Deep (2023), a very slow-burn horror movie set in the woods. Georgina Campbell (the lead from Barbarian) plays a new park ranger investigating several missing visitors. It’s beautifully shot, with some startlingly vivid imagery, especially towards the end.

THOROUGHBREDS, starring Anya Taylor-Joy and Olivia Cooke, is now streaming on Hulu. A ruthlessly stylish, razor-sharp, pitch-black psychodrama, set in a picture-perfect enclave where two privileged, amoral teens hatch a murder plot and practice the technique of crying on command.

Julianne Moore - Cast a Deadly Spell (Martin Campbell, 1991)

This “fantasy detective” HBO film is unlike much you’ve seen, taking place in a Los Angeles full of zombies, gargoyles, and magic. Moore is a great tone performer and goes exactly large enough as the (possible) femme fatale lounge singer.

#Shocktober 2023 - Horror Rises from the Tomb (1973) - You know how Nosferatu is Dracula with the Bram Stoker serial numbers filed off? This is a vampire story with the vampire serial numbers filed off. Fun sleazy Paul Naschy Spanish gothic full of nudity and gore, like a trashy late Hammer film.

Shocktober ‘23 #16: EVIL DEAD 2

A perfect movie. Campbell is so hot and such a movie star. Raimi is in his element mixing Looney Toons, Three Stooges, and EC horror comics with the exuberance of a man who knows he is getting one over on the studio who is paying. Everything I love about movies.

Watched Sexy Beast for the first time in years last night and it is still just a ridiculously good screenplay. Ben Kingsley yelling “No, you’ve got to turn this opportunity yes!” gives me “short’s the best position they is”-level delight.

I finally saw Past Lives, which is lovely, and I appreciate that John Magaro resolved the whole "Jews can play Italians and Italians can play Jews" thing simply by being both. (He and Greta Lee and Teo Yoo are all excellent and the movie actually managed to make me want to take the Circle Line.) 👍

The Silencer (dir. Amy Goldstein, 1992) can best be described as "Naked Killer for white people" and it's close to being as good. A sleazy, ultra-fetish triangulation of grindhouse, arthouse and comic book. Foremost it is a movie about smoking and sick fits.

In trying to find guides to non-American/non-Anglo horror for my writing students, I found a top-10 list of "movies based on African folklore." 8/10 were films by African filmmakers (good!), 1 was His House (British, but good), and the last was, I shit you not, The Mummy (1932).

WHY WOULD YOU

IT’S A WONDERFUL KNIFE: A warm and fuzzy Christmas slasher that feels like a Hallmark special gone haywire. That’s a good thing! It’s as much a celebration of the holiday as it is a bloody killing spree. Charming, deadly, and with that same FREAKY or HAPPY DEATH DAY attitude.

watched CONFIDENTIAL REPORT aka MR. ARKADIN last night, what a movie - you can see the constraints Welles was forced to deal with (to this day there is not a definitive cut, this was the Criterion version from 2006) but still brilliant filmmaking.

I liked Poor Things a lot: it captures the topsy-turviness of Alasdair Gray's brain, his acerbic wit, and his horrors at how cruel human beings can be. Like Frankenstein, both the book & the film ask us who the real monsters are.

Now watching

Notorious shocker from 1970 in which a young model comes to stay with a creepy photographer and his randy bisexual aunt.  Bad things ensue.  This was the debut film of director Jose Ramon Larraz, BOTD in 1929.

This was pointed out in an AV Club write-up for the greatest horror movies of the 80s, but it bears repeating, as it sure feels right: The Thing is the greatest special effects movie ever made.

The way I see it, there isn't a bit of CGI work out there that can't compare w/ the practical FX here.

📽️

Shocked THE BOYS NEXT DOOR isn't better known. Spheeris' film is certainly worthy of more attention/acclaim.  Among other things, it's a great example of the "female gaze." Seldom do you see the camera so entranced with the beauty of a movie's male stars.  It's also a film of rare tenderness/empathy

Love Lies Bleeding was everything I hoped it would be. One of the absolute best modern sapphic films, equally hot, passionate, toxic and uncomfortable. It's complex and messy and never shies away from taking the most visceral punch possible. Absolutely loved it, best film I've seen this year.

THE LOST BOYS is my favorite vampire movie ever. It’s got an incredible soundtrack, the two Coreys, and some guy named Kiefer. I love Technicolor films. Say what you want about Shumacher and his wild aesthetics. He sure nailed this one. Sometimes it’s better to stay off the boardwalk.🩸

such a beautiful & elegant minute-plus long crane shot in the funhouse. tracks the little brother making his way towards the exit as stragglers clear out. captures unspoken confusion of this kid by considering space around him. this kid's experience on this night is the best stuff in the film imo

The World's Great Sinner (1962)

Absolutely batty film about an insurance salesman who gets canned from his job and becomes a cult leader

Starring, written, and directed by Timothy Carey, BOTD in 1929

Delighting in one of my true comfort films, Pacific Rim, put through a monochrome filter

Stripped of colour you're left with only contrasts, with details, and you see the film as something different. Harsher and raw in a beautiful way
More Metropolis than monster thriller

Yuen Qiu - 📽️ Kung Fu Hustle (Stephen Chow, 2004)

Need I elaborate? The Landlady of Pigsty Alley is the greatest image and character of any Chow movie, a strong-willed snarker who steals the movie and fires sonic screams that destroy all matter. It’s one of the peak comedy performances.

🎥 filmsky

NO ONE IS GOING TO SAVE YOU is dark and atmospheric. The film has 1 line of dialogue and relies heavily on lush visuals and a perfect score. This is bold filmmaking at its finest. It’s a vintage alien sci-fi flick meets home invasion. The ending is so strange and satisfying 📽️🕸️🍿🗃️

If the Argyle trailer makes you crave a cheeky spy romp that doesn't make you feel like watching a child slowly die from an incurable illness, Deadlier Than The Male (1967) is only 98 minutes long and has Elke Sommer in a bikini kill a man with a spear gun. tubitv.com/movies/46500...

Just revisited Miami Vice (2006). It has the same problem I have with most of Mann’s movies (heavy on style, light on substance). But it’s an exceedingly stylish movie, Mann has a hell of an eye. I just wish it wasn’t entirely bereft of thought. But it’s fun and it moves and it looks terrific.

VALERIE AND HER WEEK OF WONDERS (1970)
Dir. Jaromil Jireš
Czechoslovakia
Avant-Garde, Drama, Queer, Horror, Fantasy

Valerie feels the first stirs of sexual awakening after receiving a pair of magic earrings. When her fantasy adventures begin, the line between dreams and reality starts to blur.

ANTICHRIST (2009)
Dir. Lars von Trier
Denmark, Germany
Thriller, Horror, Avant-Garde, Drama

Grieving for the loss of their infant son, a couple attempt to repair their marriage by retreating to their remote woodland cabin. But as they descend into madness, a violent battle of the sexes emerges.
still find it absolutely wild how for X-Men: First Class they shot one of the most compelling, subtle superhero movie scenes ever and surrounded it with a movie i cannot remember anything about

I watched Terminator 2 last night for the first time since 1991. Action scenes still amazing, Robert Patrick still terrifying, Linda Hamilton still badass, all the stuff with the kid still incredibly stupid & poorly acted, final thumb up still the most cringe-inducing thing I’ve ever seen. A++/D-

The cinematic equivalent getting sucker punched with a roll of quarters.  Sure, the score's ludicrously melodramatic, the dialogue rife with moralizing.  Overall, though, the film's a satisfying slice of nasty goodness, with Lawrence Tierney at his Lawrence Tierney-est as the rottenest of thugs.

Lisa Frankenstein: Kathryn Newton falls for Cole Sprouse's reanimated corpse and starts murdering people to fix him up, in Zelda Williams' macabre rom-com, that shoots for a Tim Burton meets Heathers vibe, but Diablo Cody's script feels weak, and the PG-13 rating blunts the much-needed edge.

My last four watched 

ARREBATO (1979) blew my mind.  I can't think of another film that gets into the addict's warped headspace so terrifyingly well.

Something Wicked This Way Comes is the best of the dark Disney era, by far. The perfect balancing act between nostalgia for a childhood that is almost over and nostalgia for a childhood that is long past is bittersweet and beautiful. 1/2

BAD GIRL BOOGEY is an intense, Queer slasher that features compelling performances, gruesomely creative kills, and profound commentary on the deadly consequences of allowing bigotry and hatred to go unchecked. Streaming on Shudder now. 

My review from Chattanooga Film Festival 2023. 🎥 #filmsky

four years running, there has been a best picture nominee with a trans actor in a significant role:

2020: PROMISING YOUNG WOMAN (Laverne Cox)
2021: WEST SIDE STORY (iris menas)
2022: WOMEN TALKING (August Winter)
2023: BARBIE (Hari Nef)

Images from films starring Arthur Kennedy, BOTD in 1914.  He perpetually looks like he's about one second away from punching you square in the jaw.  

The Desperate Hours (1955)
Boomerang (1947)
The Lusty Men (1952)
Too Late for Tears (1949)

Finally saw WHERE THE DEVIL ROAMS, from The Adams Family. Dark, brooding period piece about a murderous family of carnival workers with haunting visuals. Really enjoyed it. Big fan of The Adams Family's work. Coolest family working in indie genre film today. Streaming on Tubi. 🎥 #filmsky #horrorsky

All of us Strangers is probably the most mature and nuanced gay film about adult romance and trauma to come out of North America in…forever?  But it didn’t get an Oscar nom because it’s actually for gay people.

I feel like I’m the only person who likes Abel Ferrara’s Body Snatchers (1993). For me Don Siegel, Philip Kaufman and Ferrara all made really good movies. I’ve never seen the 2000s version but three out of four interesting movies is a very good track record.

A noir with a weird dream-like vibe and wildly unpredictable structure. Robert Cummings as a down-at-heel ex-military man hired to be chauffeur for an impressively menacing gangster played by Steve Cochran (backed up by Peter Lorre), and falling for the gangster's wife Michèle Morgan.

Watched The Doorway to Hell (1930) this afternoon… an early Warner Bros gangster film, and James Cagney’s 2nd film appearance. Not especially notable, except for how great Mr Cagney is. He’s every inch the gangster tough guy here, it’s obvious why they quickly started giving him lead roles.

I won't spoil it, but TWILIGHT DINNER has possibly the greatest, grossest opening line of all time.  An impressive film overall.  While the sex scenes are abundant, they manage to be both disturbing and alluring.  Indeed, this is a provocative horror movie about sex, not dissimilar from Cronenberg.

My last four watched on Letterboxd

GIALLO IN VENICE (1979) is a super scuzzy mix of video nasty and softcore porn.  It stands out for featuring the most egg-obsessed character this side of Edith Massey in PINK FLAMINGOS, a detective who eats at least 3 or 4 hardboiled eggs in every scene he's in.

#NowWatching Dr. Caligari (1989) - while there are technically “erotic” elements, they feel like a ruse, to lure the audience into a bizarre Brechtian fever dream of body horror, gender fluidity, and sexual insanity accompanied by buck wild practical effects and German expressionism. Wow. #filmsky

Day 11 of Spooky Silents-The most famous film no one has seen-the long lost London After Midnight 1927. Directed by Tod Browning & starring Lon Chaney in another elaborate makeup. Not 1 inch of footage has been found.

Saw a masterwork from Iran. "The Wasteland", directed by Ahmad Bahrami. 

Deeply indebted to Béla Tarr, shot in gorgeous B&W, with patient long takes. A contemplation on how capitalism has hollowed out the Earth entire. 

Absolutely my favorite film of 2020, if not the decade so far.

Lady Bloodfight (2016). Remnant of 2008 in regards to hyperactive editing and Maxim male gaze, and you could predict every plot beat in your sleep. But earnestness and great visuals (from the cinematographer of Taken) make this rise above. Makes most other flicks at this budget look positively lazy.

#Watched Hong Kong Godfather (1985) - late Shaws has one of the most insanely gory heroic bloodshed finales I’ve ever seen. I can’t believe this came out BEFORE A Better Tomorrow. Also featuring the most insane destruction of a child since the sleeping bag smash in Prophecy. #filmsky

Next up, a real favorite of mine, 1932's The Old Dark House! It was such an unexpected delight. It's James Whale's followup to Frankenstein, and tbh, I think it's a superior film. Witty, snappy, gay as hell, and so gorgeous. Plus it has Ernest Thesiger, and if you don't love him, you have no heart

Malina (1991)

A staggering, opaque near-masterpiece in which a mentally ill writer, torn between two lovers, deals with her pain by setting fires

Directed by Werner Schroeter

Starring Isabelle Huppert in one of her best and most intense performances.  Huppert was BOTD in 1953.

Just rewatched BIG NIGHT. Fantastic, even though made when a film could be about a genuinely “big night” even though Galactus isn’t trying to eat the world and the story is just an exploration of brotherhood, with a lot of great Italian cooking thrown in as “special effects”.

LOLA (1981)

Rainer Werner Fassbinder's gorgeous, very underrated reworking of THE BLUE ANGEL.  

Starring Barbara Sukowa and Armin Mueller-Stahl, who was BOTD in 1930

#Watched The Boys Next Door (1985) - goddamn! Think ‘80s spring break teen comedies full of shenanigans, except Spheeris moves the sociopathic nature of the leads from unexamined subtext to explicit text. These guys think they are the heroes of a sex comedy, not the villains of a thriller. #filmsky
Watched Tremors on SyFy and it still holds up. A simple premise executed in brilliant B-movie fashion. Imagine trying to get that made today.

30 mins left, so here’s today’s “thing I love” - Lake Mungo. A perfect example of low budget ingenuity, and one of the most innovative horror films of its decade, maybe the 21st century so far, in storytelling terms. Heart breaking, profoundly creepy, a testament to the power of horror storytelling
I finally got to watch Past Lives and it was everything it was made out to be. Exquisite, beautiful, and full of a sort of longing that's hard to pull off without ever being maudlin or sentimental.

Rewatched Interstellar which I enjoyed when I first watched it. Now I think it’s almost perfect. It’s just so rich and cinematic. And there is a genuine sense of familial grief to it and paired with Oppenheimer a coherent horror at what is happening to the planet.

Rewatched All The President’s Men (1976) tonight, it’s so great. Every journalism film in the last 40 years has been chasing this one. I think the key is a laser-tight focus on the investigation. No bullshit love story, no subplots, no whatever. It never stops moving, and it’s flooded with details.

TEOREMA (1968)
Dir. Pier Paolo Pasolini
Italy
Drama, Mystery, Queer

A handsome and enigmatic stranger arrives at an upstanding wealthy household in Milan and successively seduces the son, the mother, the daughter, the father, and the maid, but as abruptly and mysteriously as he arrived, he departs.

saw a BEAUTIFUL 35mm print of this film after a q&a with steven spielberg back in film school. this movie is a cinematic achievement and something the french new wave guys could only dream of (if you really want me to go down a rabbit hole, you can ask me what i mean by this). it's also racist.

Tonight's recommendation: BLOODY ORANGES

A retired couple enter a dance contest, a corrupt politician, a girl eager to lose her virginity, and a young lawyer obsessed with social status - a seemingly benign look into theses daily lives goes haywire in this shocking black comedy.

A friend & I have been working through notorious/reviled films. Last night was CANNIBAL FEROX (1981). These Italian cannibal films are fascinating. They follow virtually no narrative rules & stories exist in a pre-globalised international zero setting. The violence is full on, esp against animals

just watched TALK TO ME, a pretty boilerplate possession story punched up w excellent performances, sturdy emotional core & a well executed, properly depressing addiction allegory. the hand is silly but it doesnt matter, it opens the door to something much bigger & they pull it off w flying colors

DAVID HOLMES: THE BOY WHO LIVED (2023, dir: Hartley) a genuinely moving portrait of Daniel Radcliffe’s stunt double, who was paralyzed by an on-set accident gets perverted by the filmmakers insistance on making the story uplifting and redemptive despite what the interviewees actually say.

The Dunwich Horror (1970) is a very horny yet deeply unsexy movie. Silly and hamfisted—but I did like the psychedelic representation of the monster. “Mind-altering” works just as well to describe encounters with unfathomable being as it does the effects of hallucinogenics

I was expecting to be thrilled by Casablanca on the big screen last night. I wasn't expecting to revise my opinion upward on what I already thought was likely the best movie ever made. letterboxd.com/studentactiv...

We have just watched Black Belt Jones (1974) which was full of groovy clothes, Afros, kung fu chops, trampoline girls, funky music, Italian stereotype Mafia men, and gigantic American cars, with a climactic car wash fight scene. It rules, you should watch it.

It's funny after all "it's what you DON'T SEE that's scarier!" types pearl clutching over Carpenter's THE THING to rewatch that movie and realize what a generally low key and restrained move it is...until it needs to let things off the chain.

The Pumpkin Eater (1964)

Classic British drama about a woman who seems to get pregnant every time the wind blows and her unhappy marriage to a screenwriter.  

Starring Anne Bancroft

Screenplay by Harold Pinter

Alfre Woodard - 📽️ Crooklyn (Spike Lee, 1994)

🧪 Cat ankles!
Clouded leopards and margays are the only two types of cats that can climb headfirst down trees, owing to the unique flexibility of their ankles (tarsi). 
The tarsi of these arboreal predators rotate around ~180° so that their hind feet can grip even when they are face down/ass up.

A protostar embedded in a protoplanetary disk embedded in the dark molecular cloud LDN1527. You’re seeing an infant (100,000 years old) solar system about 447 light years away. The star itself is hidden from view by the edge-on protoplanetary disk occupying the ‘neck’ of the hourglass shaped nebula.

(16) Psyche was discovered by Annibale de Gasparis at Napoli in 1852; the 22-petatonne asteroid is about 220 km across and its heliocentric orbit is 2.53 x 3.32 AU x 3.1 deg. NASA's Psyche probe is scheduled to reach Psyche in 2029.

OSIRIS-REX was launched in Sep 2016 on an Atlas V rocket from Canaveral to a 0.78 x 1.17 AU solar orbit; it arrived at asteroid (101955) Bennu in Oct 2018, collected samples in Oct 2020, and departed Bennu for the return trip to Earth in Apr 2021. Welcome home O-REX!

Here's the light curves of 1635 supernovae measured in four colours.  They brighten and fade in a couple of months, but their peak brightness is always about the same -- allowing us to use them as standard candles to measure the acceleration of the expansion of the Universe.

NGC 2392, a planetary nebula in Gemini (much brighter than yesterday's offering). Here, I've imaged it through a diffraction grating to show that the light of the planetary is almost entirely emitted by two spectral lines (blue OIII and deep red H-alpha). Stars emit a continuum.  #astrophotography

JWST confirmed the redshift of the extremely distant galaxy GLASS-z12 to z=12.342.
This finding places it to be the 5th most distant galaxy ever discovered, existing only 360 Myr after the Big Bang.
arxiv.org/abs/2403.102...
🔭 🧪

The most energetic single particle ever detected, a cosmic ray dubbed the "Oh-My-God" particle, was observed by the Fly's Eye Cosmic Ray Detector OTD in 1991.

Its energy was about 3.2 x 10²⁰ eV ~ 51 J, equivalent to a baseball moving at almost 60 mph. 🧪 🔭

Image: Olena Shmahalo/Quanta Magazine

Supernovae forge a Moon's worth of gold, but kilonovae, born from neutron star collisions, churn out a Jupiter's worth! Despite our galaxy seeing ~100-1000x more SN than KN, KN contribute >10x more gold!
Your gold ring? A cosmic treasure made of stuff forged in the hearts of colliding neutron stars.

Ingenious descent profile of the first soft-landing on the surface of the Moon, Luna-9, in 1966, where the lander was encased in a pressurized 'balloon,' which bounced several times on the Moon before splitting into 2 halves and then releasing the spacecraft which then deployed all its instruments.

Look at these fab plots showing SN flux light curves from the new DES paper, showcasing 1500 new high redshift type Ia supernovae. 

Aust. astronomers, led by the excellent Prof. Tamara Davis, made big contributions! 

Universe might be ~100 million years younger 👀

📸 arxiv.org/abs/2401.02929 🔭🧪

Eta Carinae lies in the center of the Homunculus Nebula. A star on the verge of supernova, it initially went through a "Great Eruption" in the mid 1800s that ejected stellar gases and formed the nebula seen here. It is the only star known to emit natural LASER light, here in the ultraviolet spectrum

Rubin Observatory will measure the ghostly glow of innumerable stars stripped from their home galaxies and left to wander vast intergalactic space. Such "intracluster light" will help us understand the elusive dark matter known to pervade galaxy clusters rubinobservatory.org/news/fossil-...  🔭

The JWST found the most distant supermassive black hole (SMBH) yet! It's so far that it existed when the Universe was only 570 million years old. This SMBH is a whopping 9 million times the mass of the sun. It was found in the CEERS survey in a galaxy called CEERS 1019. 1/

The fastest spinning object ever measured is a millisecond pulsar, rotating at ~24% the speed of light. 

A new study says that with SKA, we should find a handful more of these that could be spinning slightly faster. But there are unlikely any sub-millisecond pulsars.

arxiv.org/abs/2312.12185 🧪🔭

The Orion Nebula (right) is about 24 light years across but only has a total mass of ~2,000 suns.

Most of the material is spread out over this vast distance making the gas very thinned out. 

That’s why (even it’s the size of two full moons) you can’t see it with naked eyes, it’s very dim. 🔭🧪

Jupiter’s Ganymede is the largest moon in the solar system. Its surface is covered in ice and it’s believed to have a subsurface salt water ocean. Along with that, Ganymede is the only known moon with an internally generated magnetic field, making the Galilean moon very alluring.

The intricate Simeis 147, or "Spaghetti Nebula," reveals twisting filaments covering nearly 3 degrees in the Taurus-Auriga boundary. Composed of hydrogen (red) and oxygen (blue) emissions, it spans 150 light-years at an estimated distance of 3,000 light-years.

This is the North America nebula region in the constellation Cygnus and covers the area of 4x the size of the full moon in the sky. This patch of ionized hydrogen lies about 1800 ly from Earth. I imaged this with an OSC camera, a WO RedCat 51 and 2 Antlia narrowband filters from my deck in August.🔭

Happy Rotating Black Hole Day!

Roy Kerr submitted his paper describing the spacetime around a rotating black hole OTD in 1963.

Physicists had been searching for decades for this solution to Einstein's field equations. It describes, at least approximately, every black hole in the Universe. 🧪 🔭

Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar was born OTD in 1910. He established an upper limit on the mass of stable white dwarf stars. Above this Chandrasekhar Limit – about 1.44 solar masses – stars eventually explode and then collapse into a neutron star or black hole. 🧪 🔭

A late ArXiV post since I wasn’t active here — we present a field dwarf pair where one is starbursting and the other is *quenched*! VLA data reveal that the non-SF dwarf's HI is in large tidal tails, showing that field dwarfs can temporarily quench even as non-satellites. arxiv.org/abs/2311.09280
Comet 12P/Pons-Brooks continues to brighten. Appears close to the star Alpheratz (Alpha Andromedae). They will be at their closest next week. Binoculars needed, looking west in the evening. 🔭 🧪

Reentry of the EP-9 battery pallet jettisoned from ISS in 2021 is currently predicted (by Space Force) between 1230 UTC Mar 8 and 0830 UTC Mar 9.  It will not totally burn up on reentry - about half a tonne of fragments will likely hit the Earth's surface. Casualty risk is a few tenths of a percent

As the ring turns: our first results are out from the 2018 Event Horizon Telescope 🔭🧪 campaign, and the ring around the M87* black hole has shifted 30°! Congrats to the team!  We're not entirely sure what it means but we have a few ideas, stay tuned for the Theory & Interpretation paper coming soon!

Did you know there is a helicopter on Mars? 

The Ingenuity Helicopter is an autonomous craft that flew to Mars along with the Perserverance rover. While the rover searches for signs of former life, Ingenuity is a test of powered flight on Mars, which has less gravity but an very thin atmosphere!

SUPER NEAT NEW SCIENCE!

The delicate rings of Saturn. Remember, the rings aren’t solid objects but are instead composed of billions of bits of ice. In some places, you can see right through the rings!

In orbit around the Milky Way are dozens of satellite galaxies, each containing millions or even billions of stars. In their wakes, tidal forces draw out streamers of gas and stars, some of which are ancient. Just imagine the view any planets in these streams might have in their night skies.

Uranus is wreathed in auroras! UV auroras were observed lighting up Uranus in 1986, and astronomers have gathered the first measurements of infrared auroras, using the Keck II telescope

Energy generated by the auroras could explain why Uranus is hotter than expected 🔭🧪

This new image of NGC 2264, aka the “Christmas Tree Cluster,” shows the shape of a cosmic tree with the glow of stellar lights. NGC 2264 is, in fact, a cluster of young stars — with ages between about one and five million years old — in our Milky Way about 2,500 light-years away from Earth.

No mirror image here! These are two interacting galaxies, known as NGC 5331. This pair will collide and merge over hundreds of millions of years. They are 450 million light-years away from Earth in the constellation Virgo.

Congratulations to India and the ISRO, Chandrayaan-3 has landed and that makes India the 4th nation to successfully soft land on the moon. 

With Luna-25, the moon has eaten 4 landers in the past few years so Chandrayaan-3 has broken the streak.

Juan Farias uses the end of STARFORGE simulations as initial conditions for long-term integration with NBODY6, to understand the way that star clusters disperse and evolve in the galactic environment. Congrats Juan! 🧪🔭

The Universe, and our ability to catalog it, are pretty amazing. Here's an image just released today from ESA's Euclid mission. Euclid can see 100x wider than JWST (though not as deep). This picture shows a globular cluster, a collection of coeval stars nearly as old as the Universe itself. 🧪

Happy Galaxies Day! A century ago tonight, Edwin Hubble exposed a photograph at the 100-inch telescope on Mt. Wilson whose interpretation enlarged our mental Universe incomparably. Hubble recognized that a tiny brightening speck in what was then called the Andromeda Nebula had to be a Cepheid...

Ooooooh cool result from Emily Pass showing that Jupiter-analogs to terrestrial M-dwarf planets are rare. This may have huge implications for water delivery to these rocky, temperate planets, which are the only potentially habitable planets we can study in the near future. #aas243 🔭🪐

The IAU names mountains on Saturn's moon Titan using names from Lord of the Rings, so Mount Doom (technically 'Doom Mons') is a real cryovolcano, possibly the largest in the solar system.

The Cassini–Huygens probe didn't detect any hobbits climbing it, but it's ever watchful.

One of my favorite science history bits is that Betty White was about to turn three years old when Edwin Hubble's evidence for galaxies outside the Milky Way was presented at a 1925 AAS meeting. Jimmy Carter was a few months old. There are folks alive today who were born into an island universe. 🧪 🔭

🔊Calling all primary school teachers: time to get the kids to paint Neptune pale bluey-green! 

In 1989 when Voyager2 snapped images of Neptune, they upped the blue filter to enhance features in the atmosphere, leading to a global misconception that Neptune is deep blue. 🧪🔭

This one of my favorite views from the Cassini mission: backlit views of Titan’s upper atmosphere. Titan is the only moon in the solar system with a significant atmosphere, and that atmosphere extends higher than does on Earth. Titan never disappoints!

New in neat planetary seismology: fifty-year-old seismic records contained the signals of the Apollo 17 lunar module warming and cooling at sunrise and sunset. Congrats to the trio of authors! Imagine what we'll be able to hear with new instruments on the Moon.

On this day in 1612 Galileo Galilei first observed the planet Neptune. However, he didn't recognise it as a planet.

It was found again in 1846, when Urbain Le Verrier predicted its position from irregularities in the (first observed) orbit of Uranus.

Mars is a stunning fossil. The planet isn’t geographically active like Earth. 

A new study reports the discovery of cracks in the Martian mud that are billions of years old. This means ancient Mars had wet-dry cycles, which suggests the possibility of life. 1/2

You could call it the "Oh-My-Goddess" particle. Meet “Amaterasu”: Astronomers detect highest energy cosmic ray since 1991. The Telescope Array in Utah's West Desert picked up a rare particle with 244 EeV energy. arstechnica.com/science/2023...

A pleasing evening calculation, prepping for my Introductory Astrophysics class:  

If the Sun is orbiting the Galactic centre at the typical stellar rotation velocity (~225 km/s), how many times has the Sun been around the Galaxy during its ~5 billion year lifetime?

Answer: around 20 times 💃

composite image of saturn in the infrared from cassini. the icy rings are blue, thermal radiation from below appears red, backlighting cloud layers, and the aurora of the southern hemisphere is bright green, where particles drawn by the magnetic field precipitate through the atmosphere. 🔭

One way to answer the question: Where is the center of the Universe?

You are the center.

Because you're not measuring the entire Universe, but the Observable Universe, which necessarily is centered on the observer.

And you cannot ever know if there's anything beyond 46.5 Bly.

I love this type of astro paper. @@19288 & T Bakx show simply and convincingly, that high photometric redshifts are biased upwards. Using mostly counting statistics arguments a la Eddington (1913), it's shown that this gets worse with photo-z error.
Bonus points for the title!
🔭

Voyager 2 was launched into deep space 46 years ago this month. It was expected to last 5 years, but is still going. Voyager 2 lost contact last month when the wrong command turned its antenna away from Earth. Today, its "heartbeat" signal was detected, so this amazing craft is still at work.

Then I read the term "mean-motion orbital resonance" which is when 2 bodies have revolutions that are a "simple integer ratio of each other" (per wiki.) When 2 bodies have mean-motion orbital resonance, they either stabilize (sync up but never touch) or destabilize (one gets ejected).

M42, the Orion Nebula, is the nearest stellar nursery to Earth. New stars are formed here in the hot gasses of the nebula, and even some small star systems are being born! At 1500 light years away, it is far away, but close enough to appear as a smudge in the sky to the naked eye.

At some point, not long after the Universe itself was born, the very first stars ignited and lit up the cosmos.

We may now have indirect evidence of their existence.

The thing people don't realize about a lot of playtesting in a game dev scenario is that for a long time you're trying to play catch with a ball made out of barbed wire, and you're tossing it back and forth, trying to clip off all the barbs that make it unplayable.

the (more or less) failure of VR is evidence that games aren’t really about “immersion” per se. what you want are small details, novelties, made by people. things that say “yeah i thought of that too”

not immersion, but a conversation with developer, as long and deep as you like.
